VT/PBW — 4–8 mL/kg
VE — 5–10 L/min

Bedside Reference · Critical Care

Invasive Mechanical Ventilation

Mechanical ventilation as a teaching reference, written for the people learning to manage vented patients — from the IM resident covering nights to the hospitalist running a stepdown to the PCCM fellow building toward independent ICU practice. Physiology-first and decision-driven: the framework to walk up to a ventilator, read the screen, and pick the next change without guessing. Coverage spans physiology, mode mechanics, waveforms, disease-specific strategy, advanced concepts (driving pressure, transpulmonary pressure, mechanical power, prone, ECMO), and liberation, ending in bedside troubleshooting and cases.

Vol. I · Compiled May 2026 · Last reviewed May 2026 · Reflects ATS / ESICM / SCCM ARDS practice guideline 2024 and the Global Definition of ARDS 2023

How to read

Three ways in

① Linear
Cover to cover

Parts I–IX build sequentially. ~14 h end-to-end.

② Reference
Look up & out

Start from high-yield cards or the bedside cheat sheet. Chapters stand alone.

③ Bedside
Patient in front of you

Jump to cases (Ch 40), troubleshooting (Ch 39), or the disease chapter in Part IV.

Disclaimer

This reference is for educational use only. It does not constitute medical advice and is not a substitute for clinical judgment, institutional protocols, or the care of a qualified clinician at the bedside.

Drug doses, equipment specifications, contraindications, and practice patterns change. Verify every clinically actionable detail against primary sources, current guidelines, and your institution's standards before applying it to a patient. Content reflects evidence and consensus as of May 2026. Bedside Reference accepts no liability for outcomes arising from use of this material. By reading further, you acknowledge these terms.

★Bedside cheat sheet

The short version: everything you need to remember on rounds, condensed onto one page. Use this as your at-a-glance reference; depth lives in the chapters below.

★ Initial settings by phenotype (default)
First-pass vent settings
PhenotypeVT (mL/kg PBW)RRPEEPFiO₂ goalOther
General adult6–812–165SpO₂ 92–96%VC-A/C
ARDS4–620–35per high-PEEP tableSpO₂ 88–95%Pplat ≤ 30, ΔP ≤ 15
Asthma / COPD severe6 / 6–88–10 / 10–140–5 / 5–8SpO₂ 92–96% / 88–92%I:E 1:4–5; permissive hypercapnia
Cardiogenic edema6168–12SpO₂ 92–96%Diuresis is the treatment
↑ ICP / TBI6–8titrate to PaCO₂ 35–405–10PaO₂ ≥ 80HOB 30°, neutral neck
Neuromuscular8–10 (lungs normal)match drive5–8SpO₂ 95%Aggressive secretion clearance
Massive PE / RV failure614–18 (PaCO₂ 35–40)5–8 (low!)SpO₂ > 92%iNO; norepi; consider ECMO
Obese6–8 by PBW14–1810–15SpO₂ 92–96%Reverse Trendelenburg
★ Lung-protective bundle (apply to every vented patient)
  1. VT 4–8 mL/kg PBW (start 6 if any ARDS suspicion)
  2. Pplat ≤ 30 cmH₂O
  3. Driving pressure (Pplat − PEEP) ≤ 15 cmH₂O
  4. SpO₂ 88–96% (avoid both hypoxia and hyperoxia)
  5. Permissive hypercapnia OK; pH ≥ 7.20
  6. HOB 30°, sedation light (RASS −1 to −2), DVT/stress ulcer ppx
★ Liberation criteria (daily)
  1. Off pressors / minimal vasopressor (≤ 0.1 norepi)
  2. FiO₂ ≤ 0.5, PEEP ≤ 8, SpO₂ ≥ 92%
  3. Adequate cough, manageable secretions, follows commands
  4. Underlying cause improving
  5. If ALL met → SBT: PSV 5–8 over PEEP 5 (most common variant) or PSV 0 + PEEP 0, T-piece (most rigorous); both acceptable; 30–120 min
  6. SBT pass = RR < 35, RSBI < 105, VT > 4 mL/kg PBW, no distress
  7. Cuff leak adequate + airway protection → extubate; high-risk → post-extubation HFNC + NIV
★ Numbers worth memorizing
  • PBW (men) = 50 + 2.3 × (height inches − 60); women = 45.5 + 2.3 × (height inches − 60)
  • Normal A–a gradient ≈ age/4 + 4 (room air)
  • Normal Cstat 60–100 mL/cmH₂O; ARDS 20–40
  • Normal ΔP ≤ 15 (target ≤ 13 in ARDS)
  • Mechanical power > 17 J/min = harm
  • Auto-PEEP if expiratory flow ≠ 0 at start of next breath
  • SpO₂ 90% ≈ PaO₂ 60 (the cliff)
  • P/F < 100 = severe ARDS; < 80 = ECMO consideration
  • RSBI = RR / VT(L); < 105 predicts SBT success
  • Diaphragm thickening fraction < 15% predicts extubation failure
★ Sudden deterioration on the vent — DOPES then DOTTS

DOPES (the differential): Displaced ETT · Obstruction · Pneumothorax · Equipment failure · Stacked breaths (auto-PEEP)

DOTTS (the actions): Disconnect from vent (bag with PEEP, FiO₂ 1.0) · Oxygenate · Tube position (capnography + auscultation) · Tweak the vent · Sonography (POCUS for PTX, ETT, sliding)

★ Drug doses you'll need first hour
RSI + immediate post-intubation
ClassDrugDose
Induction (stable)Etomidate0.3 mg/kg IV
Induction (shock / asthma)Ketamine1.5–2 mg/kg IV
NMB (RSI)Rocuronium1.2 mg/kg IV
NMB alt (no contraindication)Succinylcholine1.5 mg/kg IV
Sedation infusionPropofol5–80 mcg/kg/min
Sedation alt (light)Dexmedetomidine0.2–1.5 mcg/kg/h
AnalgesiaFentanyl25–250 mcg/h infusion; 25–100 mcg bolus
Push-dose pressorPhenylephrine50–200 mcg IV q2 min PRN
Push-dose pressor (β + α)Epinephrine5–20 mcg IV q2 min PRN
NMB infusion (ARDS)Cisatracurium15 mg bolus → 37.5 mg/h × 48 h
NMB reversalSugammadex2–16 mg/kg (depth-dependent)
★ Decision algorithm 1: NIV vs intubate?
NIV trial vs go straight to intubation
IndicationNIV first?Reassess at 1–2 h
COPD exacerbation, pH < 7.35, PaCO₂ > 45, alertYES — first-linepH up + RR down + GCS stable → continue. pH falling / GCS dropping → intubate.
Cardiogenic edema, alert, no shockYES — CPAP 8–12 or BiPAP 14/8SpO₂ up + RR down + clinical relief → continue + diurese. Failure → intubate.
Hypoxemic respiratory failure, P/F 200–300, otherwise stableHFNC first-line; NIV controversialROX index > 4.88 at 12 h → continue HFNC. ROX < 3.85 → intubate.
Immunocompromised hypoxemiaHFNC preferredSame ROX criteria
GCS < 8, can't protect airway, copious secretions, hemodynamic instability, vomiting/aspiration riskNO — intubate—
Refractory hypoxemia despite max NIV/HFNC, accessory muscle use, exhaustionNO — intubate—
Cardiac arrest, severe agitation, post-op nausea, recent upper GI surgeryNO — intubate or alternative—
★ Decision algorithm 2: PEEP up or down?
PEEP titration decision flow
SituationDirectionMagnitude / next step
SpO₂ < 92% on FiO₂ > 0.6, recruitable phenotype↑ PEEP+2 cmH₂O steps; recheck SpO₂ + Pplat + ΔP + MAP at 5 min
Pplat > 30 or ΔP > 15 after PEEP step↓ PEEP backTry VT reduction first; PEEP optimization is bounded by Pplat
MAP drops > 10 mmHg or lactate rises with PEEP step↓ PEEP backHemodynamic price too high; accept lower P/F if needed
SpO₂ improves AND Pplat stable AND MAP stableStay or modest ↑Found a good operating point; check ΔP — if minimum here, lock in
SpO₂ > 96% on FiO₂ ≤ 0.4, PEEP > 8↓ PEEPWean PEEP first, then FiO₂ (not vice versa); 1–2 cmH₂O daily
Focal lobar ARDSLower PEEP (8–12)High PEEP overdistends healthy lung; consider prone earlier
Diffuse ARDS, recruitable phenotypeHigher PEEP (12–18) per high-PEEP tableBest-compliance or esophageal-guided strategies
RV failure / high PVRLower PEEP (5–8)RV-protective; accept lower oxygenation; iNO
★ Decision algorithm 3: Is the patient ready for SBT today?
Daily readiness screen
CriterionThresholdIf NO
Underlying cause improvingYesTreat the cause; reassess tomorrow
HemodynamicsOff pressors or norepi ≤ 0.1 mcg/kg/minWean pressors; treat shock; reassess
OxygenationFiO₂ ≤ 0.5 AND PEEP ≤ 8 with SpO₂ ≥ 92%Continue current settings; recheck
Acid-basepH > 7.30Treat acidosis or hypercapnia first
Mental statusFollows commands; RASS −1 to +1Wean sedation first (SAT); treat delirium
Cough / secretionsAdequate cough; suctioning > q2h not neededTreat infection; physical therapy
All YES→ SBT: PSV 5–8 over PEEP 5 (most common) or PSV 0 + PEEP 0, T-piece (most rigorous); both acceptable; 30–120 min. Pass = RR < 35, RSBI < 105, VT > 4 mL/kg PBW, no distress.
★ Decision algorithm 4: Refractory hypoxemia in ARDS — the rescue ladder
Stepwise rescue when ARDSnet basics aren't enough
StepThreshold to escalateAction
1Any worseningRe-confirm basics: VT 6, Pplat < 30, ΔP ≤ 15, RR matched, sedation adequate, no PTX/mucus plug
2Visible asynchrony / P-SILI riskDeepen sedation; trial NMB bolus to assess
3P/F 100–200, recruitablePEEP titration (high-PEEP table, decremental, or esophageal-guided)
4P/F < 150 on FiO₂ ≥ 0.6, PEEP ≥ 5Prone ≥ 16 h/day (PROSEVA criteria)
5Persistent dys-synchrony or ΔP > 15 despite sedationNMB infusion (cisatracurium) × 48 h
6P/F < 100 refractory or RV dysfunctionInhaled NO 5–20 ppm or epoprostenol; bridge only
7P/F < 80 sustained, or pH < 7.20 with PaCO₂ > 60 despite max ventVV-ECMO consult (transfer early)
The discipline of one-step-at-a-timeEach rung is a discrete decision. After moving up, wait 2–4 h. If the step doesn't help, undo it before adding the next — otherwise you can't tell what's working.

∑Bedside calculator

Threshold-coded outputs, lung-protective bundle status, ARDSnet PEEP/FiO₂ ladder, dead-space and oxygen-delivery calculations, transpulmonary pressure, and saveable snapshots — at the bedside or on rounds. Also available as a sticky ∑ button (bottom-right of every page) and as a standalone page.

LPV bundle VT/kg — Pplat — ΔP — MP —
Patient
Arterial blood gas
Ventilator settings
Optional — dead space, oxygen delivery, transpulmonary pressure
Oxygenation
PBW —kg \(\text{PBW} = \text{base} + 2.3 \times (\text{ht}_\text{in} - 60)\)
A–a gradient —mmHg \(A{-}a = P_\text{A}O_2 - P_aO_2;\; P_\text{A}O_2 = F_iO_2(P_\text{atm} - P_{H_2O}) - P_aCO_2/R\)
A–a — predicted —mmHg \(\text{normal} = \text{age}/4 + 4\) (room air); on supplemental O₂ this underestimates
P/F ratio —— \(\text{P/F} = P_aO_2 / F_iO_2\) — Berlin oxygenation index
S/F ratio — \(\text{S/F} = SpO_2 / F_iO_2\)
ROX index — \(\text{ROX} = (SpO_2 / F_iO_2) / RR\) — HFNC weaning predictor
CaO₂ — oxygen content —mL/dL \(CaO_2 = 1.34 \cdot Hb \cdot SaO_2 + 0.003 \cdot P_aO_2\)
DO₂ — oxygen delivery —mL O₂/min \(DO_2 = CO \times CaO_2 \times 10\) — needs cardiac output
OI — Oxygenation Index — \(OI = (\text{MAP} \times F_iO_2 \times 100) / P_aO_2\)
Ventilation & dead space
VE — minute ventilation —L/min \(V_E = V_T \times RR\)
Vd/Vt — dead-space fraction — \(\frac{V_d}{V_T} = \frac{P_aCO_2 - P_{ET}CO_2}{P_aCO_2}\) — Bohr-Enghoff
Target PaCO₂ helper — \(\frac{V_E^\text{new}}{V_E^\text{cur}} = \frac{P_aCO_2^\text{cur}}{P_aCO_2^\text{target}}\) — adjust RR (or VT) by this factor
Mechanics
Driving pressure (ΔP) —cmH₂O \(\Delta P = P_\text{plat} - PEEP\)
Cstat — static compliance —mL/cmH₂O \(C_\text{stat} = V_T / \Delta P\)
Raw — airway resistance —cmH₂O·s/L \(R_\text{aw} = (PIP - P_\text{plat}) / \dot{V}\) (\(\dot{V}\) = peak flow, L/s)
Time constant (τ) —s \(\tau = R_\text{aw} \times C_\text{stat}\); \(T_e \geq 3\tau\) avoids air-trapping
Max RR by I:E (Te ≥ 3τ)
I:E 1:1—I:E 1:3—
I:E 1:2—I:E 1:4—
 I:E 1:5—
\(RR_\text{max} = 60n / [(1+n)\cdot 3\tau]\) bpm (n = E:I ratio)
PL,insp — transpulmonary insp. —cmH₂O \(P_{L,insp} = P_\text{plat} - P_\text{esoph}^\text{insp}\) (target ≤ 20–25)
PL,exp — transpulmonary end-exp. —cmH₂O \(P_{L,exp} = PEEP - P_\text{esoph}^\text{exp}\) (target slightly > 0)
Power & lung protection
VT / PBW —mL/kg \(V_T / \text{PBW}\) — target 4–8 mL/kg lung-protective
MP formula mode:
Mechanical power (VC) —J/min \(MP = 0.098 \cdot RR \cdot V_T \cdot (PIP - \tfrac{1}{2}\Delta P)\) — Becker (VC)
Liberation
RSBI — Yang-Tobin — \(\text{RSBI} = RR / V_T(L)\) — target < 105 for SBT readiness
Decision aids
PEEP / FiO₂ ARDSnet ladder
FiO₂ rowLow-PEEP tableHigh-PEEP (LOVS)Your PEEP
Closest———
Lookup of ARDSnet low-PEEP and ALVEOLI / LOVS high-PEEP tables for the given FiO₂
Snapshots
LabelTime VT/kgΔP P/FMP RSBI
No snapshots saved. Useful for tracking PEEP titration, SBT, or prone effects. Persist in this browser only.

Reference values. PBW (men) = 50 + 2.3·(ht in − 60); women = 45.5 + 2.3·(ht in − 60). A–a normal = age/4 + 4 (room air; underestimates on supplemental O₂). MP (VC) = 0.098 × RR × VT × (PIP − ½ΔP); MP (PC) = 0.098 × RR × VT × (PEEP + ΔP). CaO₂ = 1.34·Hb·SaO₂ + 0.003·PaO₂. ROX = (SpO₂/FiO₂)/RR. Vd/Vt = (PaCO₂ − ETCO₂)/PaCO₂. OI = MAP × FiO₂ × 100 / PaO₂. DO₂ = CO × CaO₂ × 10. PL,insp = Pplat − Pesoph,insp; PL,exp = PEEP − Pesoph,exp. Raw = (PIP − Pplat) / flow_L/s. τ = Raw × Cstat (in L/cmH₂O); Te ≥ 3τ avoids air-trapping.

Part I

Foundations

Build the conceptual base: who needs ventilation and why; the respiratory physiology that ventilator settings interact with — gas-exchange essentials, hypoxemia, and CO₂ transport with the control of breathing; then the bedside PFTs that map onto every chronic-vent patient and the lung mechanics that govern every alveolus.

1Why we ventilate — types of respiratory failure

Mechanical ventilation does four jobs: oxygenate, ventilate, off-load the work of breathing, and protect the airway. Every chapter that follows is an answer to which job is failing and which lever fixes it.

Figure 1.1 — What the ventilator does. Four physiologic jobs and the levers that act on each. Every indication that follows is one of these jobs failing.

Six clinical patterns drive most intubations. They map onto the four jobs above.

  1. Hypoxemic respiratory failure — arterial partial pressure of O₂ (PaO₂) < 60 mmHg, or peripheral oxygen saturation (SpO₂) < 90% on adequate fraction of inspired O₂ (FiO₂). Five physiologic mechanisms produce hypoxemia at the alveolar–capillary level (hypoventilation, low FiO₂, V/Q mismatch, shunt, diffusion limitation); each behaves differently on the vent. Detailed in §3.1. Common causes: ARDS, pneumonia, edema, pulmonary embolism, atelectasis.
  2. Hypercapnic respiratory failure — arterial partial pressure of CO₂ (PaCO₂) > 45 mmHg with acidosis. Pump failure: COPD/asthma, neuromuscular weakness, drug overdose, brainstem injury.
  3. Perioperative / atelectasis-driven hypoxemia — surgical, obese, or abdominal patients lose functional residual capacity under anesthesia and develop shunt-physiology atelectasis. Often resolves with PEEP, recruitment, and a few hours of lung-protective ventilation (§24).
  4. Shock with high work of breathing — the respiratory muscles can consume 25–40% of cardiac output in extremis. Intubation off-loads them so the redirected blood flow can serve the brain, heart, and gut.
  5. Inability to protect the airway — depressed mental status (Glasgow Coma Scale [GCS] ≤ 8); bulbar dysfunction (loss of the cranial-nerve–mediated swallow, cough, and gag reflexes that keep secretions out of the trachea — seen in brainstem stroke, advanced ALS, Guillain–Barré, myasthenic crisis); ongoing hematemesis; expanding airway lesion (angioedema, epiglottitis).
  6. Anticipated decline — flail chest, escalating sepsis, expanding intracranial process, agitated patient with worsening trajectory. Intubate proactively when the trajectory is bad, not after the patient codes.
Physiology noteThe PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio (P/F) is the most-used hypoxemia index because it normalizes for FiO₂. Caveat: P/F is sensitive to PEEP and barometric pressure; the SpO₂/FiO₂ ratio (S/F) is a non-invasive surrogate (S/F ≈ 0.5 × P/F + 100) used in the 2023 Global Definition of ARDS.1
Before you tube — NIV and HFNC are first-line for two specific phenotypesNot every patient on this list needs an endotracheal tube. High-flow nasal cannula (HFNC) is first-line for hypoxemic failure with preserved respiratory drive — the FLORALI trial showed a mortality benefit over standard O₂ in immunocompetent hypoxemic patients.41 Bilevel positive airway pressure (BiPAP) is first-line for acute hypercapnic COPD exacerbation46 and cardiogenic pulmonary edema.47 The decision algorithm — when to trial NIV/HFNC and when to proceed straight to intubation — lives in §34. Detailed NIV/HFNC physiology and settings: §27.
Key takeaways
  • The ventilator does four jobs: oxygenate, ventilate, off-load work of breathing, protect the airway. Every patient on the vent is on it for at least one of these — usually two or three.
  • Six clinical patterns drive most intubations: hypoxemic failure, hypercapnic failure, perioperative atelectasis, shock with high WOB, airway protection, anticipated decline.
  • Most intubations are not for a specific PaO₂/PaCO₂ number — they are for the trajectory and to protect the airway.
  • "Anticipated decline" is the most under-recognized indication — intubate proactively when the trajectory is bad, not after the patient codes.
  • P/F < 100 = severe ARDS. ECMO is considered when lung-protective ventilation cannot maintain oxygenation or pH despite optimization; typical referral triggers include P/F < 80 sustained > 6 hours, pH < 7.20 sustained > 6 hours on optimized vent, or Murray score > 3.0 (§33).
  • Before intubating, consider NIV (BiPAP) for hypercapnic failure and cardiogenic edema; HFNC for hypoxemic failure with preserved drive. Decision algorithm: §34.

2Respiratory physiology essentials

Every ventilator setting acts on physiology. The next 30 pages of West's Respiratory Physiology compressed into a critical-care toolkit — the framework you'll come back to whenever a patient surprises you on the screen.28

2.1 Orientation — the breathing system in one paragraph

Start here. The brain stem fires a signal down the phrenic nerves; the diaphragm contracts; pleural pressure drops; the lung is pulled open passively along with the chest wall; alveolar pressure falls below atmospheric; air flows in down the pressure gradient. At end-inspiration the diaphragm relaxes; the elastic recoil of lung + chest wall pushes air back out; CO₂ produced by metabolism is exhaled with it. Mechanical ventilation reverses the inspiratory step — instead of pulling the lung open from below, it pushes air in from above (positive pressure). The rest of this chapter unpacks each stage of that cycle into the physiology you can act on at the bedside.

Figure 2.1 — Concept map of Part I. The oxygen cascade is the framework. It branches into the A–a gradient (quantifies hypoxemia), V/Q matching (the spatial mechanism), and the CO₂ master equation (the ventilation side). All three feed into the equation of motion, which is parametrized by the four levers you actually turn at the bedside — VT, RR, PEEP, FiO₂.
Part I roadmap — three chapters, one diagramThe figure above is the navigational map for the next three chapters. Chapter 2 (this one) builds the gas-exchange foundation: the cascade itself, the gas laws, the alveolar-gas equation. Chapter 3 dives into A–a gradient and V/Q matching — the diagnostic toolkit for hypoxemia. Chapter 4 dives further into the CO₂ master equation — adds CO₂ transport and the neural control of breathing. Return to this map whenever you lose the thread; every later chapter in the document plugs back into one of these mechanisms.

2.2 Lung volumes and capacities — the alphabet

Volumes you'll see referenced everywhere in vent talk:

Standard adult lung volumes (sitting, healthy)
TermWhat it isTypical value
VT — tidal volumeAir moved per breath~500 mL (6 mL/kg PBW)
IRV — inspiratory reserveExtra you could breathe in above VT~3000 mL
ERV — expiratory reserveExtra you could push out below VT~1100 mL
RV — residual volumeAir left after maximal expiration (can't exhale)~1200 mL
FRC — functional residual capacityERV + RV. Volume at end-tidal expiration. The set point of resting breathing.~2300 mL
IC — inspiratory capacityVT + IRV~3500 mL
VC — vital capacityLargest breath you can take, top to bottom~4600 mL
TLC — total lung capacityVC + RV. Lung at maximal inflation.~5800 mL
FRC is the one to memorize. Disease changes it predictably: ARDS, atelectasis, obesity, pregnancy all shrink FRC (less aerated lung at rest → desat fast). COPD and asthma raise FRC (air trapping → operating volume creeps up the steep part of the P–V curve). PEEP exists to push FRC back above the closing volume of small airways (see §6.1a).

2.3 Surfactant and Laplace's law — why alveoli stay open

Alveoli are tiny bubbles in fluid. Surface tension wants to collapse them. Laplace's law for a sphere is P = 2T/r — pressure inside a bubble is inversely proportional to its radius. A small alveolus would have a higher inward pressure than a big one — so without surfactant, every small alveolus would empty into its larger neighbor and the lung would collapse into a few enormous spaces.

Surfactant (made by Type II pneumocytes; phospholipid + protein) reduces surface tension, and it does so more as the alveolus shrinks. That makes T proportional to r, which cancels the radius dependence in Laplace — every alveolus, big or small, stays open at the same pressure. Stability for free.

What goes wrong in ARDSThe injured lung leaks plasma proteins into alveoli, which inactivate surfactant. Surface tension wins. Alveoli collapse and re-open with each breath (atelectrauma). The lung gets stiff, FRC falls, hypoxemia from shunt sets in. This is why PEEP exists. PEEP holds the alveoli open against the un-opposed surface tension; once they're open you can ventilate them. The whole ARDS-management story — recruitment, decremental PEEP, ΔP, prone — is a continuation of this single physiology.

2.4 The oxygen cascade — why hypoxemia happens at all

Oxygen falls in pressure at every step from the atmosphere to the mitochondrion. Naming the steps lets you point to where a particular patient's hypoxemia is coming from.

Figure 2.2 — The oxygen cascade. Atmospheric PO₂ (160 mmHg) falls to ~150 once humidified, to ~100 in the alveolus once CO₂ is exchanged, to ~95 arterially (the small A–a gradient is normal V/Q heterogeneity), to ~40 in mixed venous blood, and to a critical mitochondrial PO₂ of 5–20 mmHg. Hypoxemia means a derangement at one of the alveolus → arterial → tissue steps.28
At the bedsideThe cascade tells you where a patient's hypoxemia lives. SpO₂ low + high A–a → alveolus-to-blood problem (V/Q mismatch, shunt, diffusion). SpO₂ low + normal A–a → not enough getting into the alveolus (hypoventilation, low FiO₂, altitude). Cyanosis with normal SpO₂ → tissue-extraction problem (sepsis, cyanide, methemoglobinemia).

2.5 The gas laws — every formula in this doc descends from these four
The gas laws that drive ventilator physiology
LawWhat it saysBedside relevance
BoyleP · V = constant (at fixed T)A cuffed ETT traps a fixed gas mass — squeeze the tubing and pressure rises. Pneumothorax: a small leak becomes a tension PTX as positive pressure compresses the trapped air on inspiration.
DaltonThe total pressure of a gas mixture = the sum of its partial pressuresThe foundation of the alveolar gas equation. PAO₂ depends only on what fraction of the inspired mix is O₂ (FiO₂) and how much PaCO₂ has displaced it.
HenryDissolved gas concentration ∝ partial pressure × solubilityO₂'s solubility is tiny (0.003 mL/dL/mmHg) — that's why the dissolved-O₂ term in CaO₂ contributes < 2% of arterial content at a normal Hb. CO₂'s solubility is 24× higher — that's why even small ventilation changes move PaCO₂ fast.
Fick (diffusion)Diffusion rate ∝ (Area × ΔP) / Thickness, scaled by the gas's diffusion coefficient DHealthy alveolar membrane is thin and the area is huge — diffusion is rarely rate-limiting. Disease shrinks area (emphysema), thickens the membrane (fibrosis), or both — and you see this as a fall in DLCO long before resting hypoxemia appears.
Graham (effusion / relative diffusivity)Relative diffusion rate of two gases ∝ 1 / √MWCO₂ has a much higher diffusion coefficient than O₂ (heavier molecule but ~24× more soluble — Henry dominates Graham here) — that's why CO₂ retention almost always reflects ventilation, not diffusion, and why even severe diffusion-limited disease shows hypoxemia long before hypercapnia.
Why this section is hereEvery later equation — alveolar gas, shunt fraction, oxygen content, the equation of motion — is just one or more of these four laws written in clinical units. If the algebra later feels arbitrary, it isn't; it traces back to one of these four lines.

2.6 The alveolar gas equation & A–a gradient

Two equations to know cold:

$$P_{AO_2} \;=\; F_iO_2 \,(P_{atm} - P_{H_2O}) \;-\; \frac{P_aCO_2}{R} \quad (R \approx 0.8)$$
$$\text{A–a gradient} \;=\; P_{AO_2} - P_aO_2 \quad \text{(normal = age/4 + 4 on room air; see below for alternatives)}$$
Variable key — gas-exchange equations
SymbolQuantity
PAO₂Alveolar partial pressure of O₂ (calculated)
PaO₂Arterial partial pressure of O₂ (measured)
PatmAtmospheric pressure (760 mmHg at sea level)
PH₂OWater-vapor pressure at body temperature (47 mmHg)
RRespiratory exchange ratio = VCO₂ / VO₂ ≈ 0.8
A–a gradientAlveolar–arterial oxygen tension difference

An elevated A–a gradient indicates a problem at the alveolar–capillary level (V/Q mismatch, shunt, diffusion impairment). A normal A–a gradient with hypoxemia means hypoventilation or low FiO₂ — the alveolus is fine, it just isn't being filled with O₂.

Worked example — the algebra once, with numbersHealthy adult, room air, sea level, normal PaCO₂ 40, measured PaO₂ 95.
Step 1 — solve for PAO₂: 0.21 × (760 − 47) − 40 / 0.8 = 0.21 × 713 − 50 = 150 − 50 = 100 mmHg.
Step 2 — A–a gradient = PAO₂ − PaO₂ = 100 − 95 = 5. Normal for a young adult.
Now break it. Same patient develops ARDS, intubated on FiO₂ 0.5 with PaCO₂ 60: PAO₂ = 0.5 × 713 − 60 / 0.8 = 357 − 75 = 282. If measured PaO₂ is 80, A–a = 202 — wide. The hypoxemia is at the alveolar–capillary level, not from hypoventilation.
Normal A–a gradient — three formulas that approximate the same thingThe "age/4 + 4" rule used in this book is one of several. Alternatives you'll encounter:
  • 2.5 + (0.21 × age) — Mellengaard's original (1966)43; most physiology textbooks
  • (age + 10) / 4 — common bedside rule; very close to age/4 + 4
  • age/4 + 4 — used in this book; identical to the previous within 0.5 mmHg at any age
All three give ~12–15 mmHg as the upper limit of normal in a healthy 40–60-year-old on room air. The clinical question — is this A–a wide or normal? — almost never hinges on which formula you choose; a gradient > 20–25 in an adult is unambiguously elevated. The interactive widget below uses age/4 + 4 as the threshold callout.
Live alveolar gas equation — drag the variables to see PAO₂ recompute
Open full-screen ↗
Figure 2.3 — The alveolar gas equation, interactive. PAO₂ = FiO₂ × (Patm − PH₂O) − PaCO₂ / R. Drag the four levers to see how each shapes the alveolar PO₂ in real time: FiO₂ (inspired oxygen fraction, 0.21 → 1.0), Patm (atmospheric pressure, 760 mmHg at sea level and less at altitude), PaCO₂ (the patient's measured arterial CO₂), and R (respiratory exchange ratio, ~0.8 in steady state). PH₂O is fixed at 47 mmHg. The formula gives the PO₂ that should be in the alveolus; subtracting the measured PaO₂ in the A–a gradient panel quantifies the oxygen "lost" between alveolus and artery — the diagnostic anchor for hypoxemia.

3Hypoxemia & oxygen physiology

The five physiologic causes of hypoxemia, the spatial logic of V/Q matching, the dichotomy between shunt and low-V/Q, and the oxygen-carrying chemistry of hemoglobin — the toolkit for diagnosing where a hypoxemic patient's problem lives.

Figure 3.1 — What's in this chapter. The diagnostic path through hypoxemia, ordered by bedside utility: which of the five causes is acting (§3.1) → the spatial physiology that explains regional V/Q (§3.2 with HPV in §3.2a) → the shunt-vs-mismatch dichotomy (§3.3) → how much oxygen the blood is actually carrying (§3.4, with the interactive ABG playground in §3.4a) → the saturation curve that determines whether a given PaO₂ is safe (§3.5).

3.1 The five causes of hypoxemia — and how each behaves on the vent

Hypoxemia has only five physiologic causes. Pinning down which one is acting tells you whether more PEEP, more FiO₂, more ventilation, or none of the above will help.

The five causes of hypoxemia
CauseA–aResponds to FiO₂?Classic exampleVent move
HypoventilationNormalYesOpioid OD, neuromuscular failure↑ minute ventilation
Low FiO₂NormalYesAltitude, asphyxiaRestore FiO₂
Diffusion impairment↑YesPulmonary fibrosis, exercise hypoxemia↑ FiO₂; treat underlying
V/Q mismatch (low V/Q)↑Yes (corrects with 100% O₂)COPD, asthma, atelectasis, PE (regional)Bronchodilators, recruit, modest PEEP
Shunt (V/Q = 0)↑↑No (resists 100% O₂)ARDS, lobar pneumonia, intracardiac shunt, pulmonary arteriovenous malformation (AVM)↑ PEEP, recruit, prone
The 100%-O₂ test (clinically rare, conceptually essential)True shunt is the only cause of hypoxemia that does not correct on 100% O₂ — because the venous blood traversing un-ventilated alveoli never sees the inspired gas. This is why ARDS hypoxemia (predominantly shunt) demands recruitment and PEEP, not just more FiO₂.28
Figure 3.1 — Diagnostic tree for the hypoxemic patient. Two branch points: the A–a gradient (normal → not enough getting into the alveolus; widened → problem at the alveolar–capillary interface), then the response to 100% O₂ (corrects → V/Q mismatch; resists → true shunt). The four resulting buckets each demand a different vent strategy.

3.2 V/Q matching and West's three-zone model

West's classic insight (1962): in the upright lung, alveolar pressure (PA), arterial pressure (Pa), and venous pressure (Pv) interact differently at different heights, producing three zones of perfusion.28 Mechanical ventilation — particularly with PEEP — can convert physiologic Zone 2 into pathologic Zone 1 (dead space).

Figure 3.2 — West's three-zone model. Apical alveoli see alveolar pressure greater than capillary pressure (Zone 1, dead space — none in healthy upright lungs but created by hypovolemia or excess PEEP). Mid-lung Zone 2 has waterfall behavior. Basal Zone 3 has continuous flow. Adding PEEP shifts more lung into Zone 1 — the price of recruitment is dead space and right-heart afterload.28

The vertical V/Q ratio in a healthy upright lung is ~3.3 at the apex (high V/Q — wasted ventilation) to ~0.6 at the base (low V/Q — preferentially perfused). Mechanical ventilation, supine positioning, and PEEP all redistribute this gradient.

3.2a · Master concept — Hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction (HPV)

If you remember one concept from this chapter, make it this one. HPV is the single physiologic mechanism that unifies most of the bedside oxygenation decisions you'll make in the ICU — proning, iNO, the COPD oxygen-paradox, cor pulmonale, and high-altitude pulmonary edema all live downstream of it.

When alveolar PO₂ drops in a region of lung, the local pulmonary arterioles constrict, diverting blood away from the poorly-ventilated unit toward better-ventilated lung. This hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction (HPV) — historically called the von Euler-Liljestrand mechanism after the two physiologists who first described it in 1946 — is a uniquely pulmonary reflex (systemic arteries dilate to hypoxia; pulmonary arteries constrict). It is the body's automatic V/Q matching system — and it explains a striking number of clinical observations:

  • Why proning works (§20.5, §32): dorsal lung in supine ARDS is atelectatic and locally hypoxic; HPV diverts blood away to ventral lung. Once prone, the dorsal lung re-aerates → HPV releases → blood returns to the now-better-ventilated dependent regions. V/Q matching improves regardless of any change in vent settings.
  • Why iNO works (§20.7): inhaled nitric oxide reaches only the alveoli that are ventilated, where it diffuses into adjacent capillaries and selectively dilates them — overcoming HPV in good units, redistributing flow toward them, and dropping PVR. NO inhaled into a shunt unit goes nowhere because there's no gas exchange there.
  • Why hyperoxia raises PaCO₂ in COPD (§21.6): three mechanisms contribute, in roughly descending order of magnitude. (1) V/Q redistribution — high FiO₂ raises alveolar PO₂ in poorly-ventilated regions, releasing HPV → blood flows there → those regions become high-V/Q dead space → effective alveolar ventilation drops → PaCO₂ rises. (2) Haldane effect — oxygenated hemoglobin holds less CO₂, so raising SaO₂ shifts CO₂ off Hb into plasma and raises measured PaCO₂ at any given V̇A. (3) A small reduction in minute ventilation from blunted central / peripheral chemoreceptor drive — historically called "loss of hypoxic drive," it is real but contributes only modestly to the rise in PaCO₂ in most patients.
  • Why severe lung disease causes pulmonary hypertension and cor pulmonale: chronic widespread alveolar hypoxia → sustained HPV → vascular remodeling → fixed pulm HTN → RV failure (§25).
  • Why high-altitude pulmonary edema happens: hypoxia-induced HPV is heterogeneous; over-perfused non-constricted regions overpressurize and leak.
The unifying lessonHPV is the single physiologic concept that ties together V/Q matching → proning → iNO → COPD hyperoxia → cor pulmonale → high-altitude edema. When you find yourself thinking about any of these, you're really thinking about HPV.
Where this physiology shows up in the rest of the doc
  • Shunt vs V/Q (§3.3) — categorizes hypoxemia by HPV-responsiveness
  • ARDS phenotypes (§20.1) — focal vs diffuse maps onto regional HPV
  • Prone positioning (§20.5, §32) — V/Q redistribution mechanism
  • iNO and inhaled epoprostenol (§20.7) — selective HPV release in ventilated lung
  • RV-protective ventilation (§25) — PEEP and hyperinflation that raise PVR via HPV-independent mechanisms

3.3 Shunt vs V/Q mismatch — the hypoxemia dichotomy

True (anatomic) shunt — perfusion of alveoli with V/Q = 0. The blood traversing them sees no O₂ at any FiO₂. Hypoxemia resists supplemental O₂. Causes: ARDS, lobar pneumonia, atelectasis, AVM, intracardiac right-to-left shunt.

V/Q mismatch (low-V/Q units) — perfusion of poorly-ventilated alveoli (V/Q < 1, but ≠ 0). Hypoxemia responds well to supplemental O₂ because raising inspired PO₂ overcomes the low ventilation. Causes: COPD, asthma, PE (compensatory), bronchospasm.

The shunt equation (Berggren)45 quantifies the shunt fraction:

$$\frac{\dot{Q}_s}{\dot{Q}_t} \;=\; \frac{C_cO_2 - C_aO_2}{C_cO_2 - C_vO_2}$$

where Cc, Ca, Cv = end-capillary, arterial, mixed-venous O₂ content. Bedside estimate: PaO₂/FiO₂ < 200 implies shunt > ~20%; < 100 implies shunt > ~30%.

To visualize the Berggren equation mechanically: mixed venous blood (CvO₂) enters the pulmonary circulation and splits into two streams. The shunt fraction Qs bypasses gas exchange entirely — it traverses collapsed, flooded, or consolidated alveoli and exits with the same low O₂ content it arrived with. The remaining fraction passes through ventilated alveoli, equilibrates with alveolar gas, and exits as end-pulmonary-capillary blood (CcO₂). These two streams then recombine to produce arterial blood (CaO₂). The numerator of the shunt equation — CcO₂ − CaO₂ — is the oxygen the shunt fraction should have acquired but did not, diluting the oxygenated stream. The denominator — CcO₂ − CvO₂ — is the oxygen each unit of fully-oxygenated blood actually picks up. Their ratio is the shunt fraction Qs/Qt.

Worked example — Berggren with numbersARDS patient on FiO₂ 1.0. ABG: PaO₂ 90, SaO₂ 97%; Hb 12; mixed-venous SvO₂ 65%.
Compute oxygen contents (mL O₂/dL, using 1.34 × Hb × SaO₂ + 0.003 × PaO₂; dissolved-O₂ term included because PAO₂ is very high on 100% O₂):
  • CcO₂ (end-pulmonary-capillary, assume SaO₂ ≈ 100% and PAO₂ ≈ 660 on FiO₂ 1.0): 1.34 × 12 × 1.00 + 0.003 × 660 = 16.08 + 1.98 = 18.1
  • CaO₂ (arterial): 1.34 × 12 × 0.97 + 0.003 × 90 = 15.60 + 0.27 = 15.9
  • CvO₂ (mixed venous): 1.34 × 12 × 0.65 + 0.003 × 40 = 10.45 + 0.12 = 10.6
Plug in: Qs/Qt = (18.1 − 15.9) / (18.1 − 10.6) = 2.2 / 7.5 ≈ 0.29 → 29% shunt. Consistent with severe ARDS (P/F ratio 90 in this case). The patient won't oxygenate by increasing FiO₂ further — needs recruitment, PEEP titration, and prone consideration.
At the bedsideThe shunt-vs-mismatch question becomes a single decision: does the SpO₂ rise meaningfully when I turn FiO₂ from 60% to 100%? If yes → V/Q mismatch dominates → recruit modestly, keep PEEP gentle, treat the obstructive component (bronchodilators, secretion clearance). If no → shunt dominates → recruit aggressively, raise PEEP, prone, paralyze if needed. Don't keep climbing FiO₂ — it does nothing for true shunt and accumulates oxygen toxicity.

3.4 The oxygen content equation — saturation matters more than tension
$$C_aO_2 \;=\; (1.34 \times \text{Hb} \times S_aO_2) \;+\; (0.003 \times P_aO_2)$$

The dissolved-O₂ term (0.003 × PaO₂) is trivial at normal hemoglobin. Saturation × hemoglobin carries 98% of arterial O₂. Implications:

  • Once SpO₂ > 90%, further PaO₂ rises do almost nothing for tissue delivery — but they accumulate oxygen toxicity.
  • A patient with Hb 7 has half the O₂ content of a patient with Hb 14 at the same SpO₂. For tissue O₂ delivery, transfuse before maximizing FiO₂.

The full oxygen delivery equation is DO₂ = CO × ((1.34 × [Hb] × SaO₂) + (PaO₂ × 0.003)). Cardiac output (L/min) multiplies total arterial oxygen content — making it the outermost multiplier that governs how much O₂ actually reaches tissue per minute. Inside the content term, the first product (Hb × SaO₂ × 1.34) is the hemoglobin-bound contribution; it dominates at any normal Hb level. The constant 1.34 is Hüfner's number — the mL of O₂ carried per gram of fully saturated hemoglobin. The second product (PaO₂ × 0.003) is dissolved oxygen, physiologically trivial at normal hemoglobin. The practical consequence is that DO₂ has exactly three independent levers — CO, Hb, and SaO₂ — and the ventilator controls only SaO₂.

At the bedsideThe hypoxic ICU patient who is not improving on the vent has, at most, three levers: CO, Hb, SaO₂. The ventilator only adjusts SaO₂. If SaO₂ ≥ 90% and the patient still has a tissue O₂-debt (lactate, low ScvO₂), the next move is to raise CO (resuscitate, treat sepsis, support the RV) or raise Hb (transfuse if < 7 g/dL with active ischemia or shock) — not to push FiO₂ from 60% to 100% chasing a higher PaO₂.
3.4a Try it — interactive ABG & oxygen physiology playground
P_AO₂
100
alveolar (mmHg)
A–a gradient
5
normal
P/F ratio
452
normal
SaO₂
97.0%
via Severinghaus
CaO₂
18.4
mL O₂/dL
Figure 3.3 — Try it (interactive ABG playground). Drag the four sliders to model any ABG. The marker rides the OHbDC; A–a gradient and P/F update live. Try the presets to see the canonical phenotypes; the "100% O₂ test" preset shows what a non-shunting patient achieves on FiO₂ 1.0 (PaO₂ > 500). Persistent hypoxemia at FiO₂ 1.0 implies true shunt (§3.3) — recruit and PEEP, not more FiO₂.

3.5 The oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve — Bohr effect
cycling left ↔ right shift every 6 s
Figure 3.4 — The oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve. The "edge of the cliff" sits at PaO₂ 60 / SaO₂ 90 — small drops below this cause big saturation drops. P₅₀ (the PaO₂ at 50% saturation) is normally 26.5 mmHg; right shifts (acidosis, hypercapnia, hyperthermia, ↑ 2,3-BPG) raise P₅₀ and unload O₂ at the tissue; left shifts do the opposite.28
3.5a Try it — interactive shift explorer
P₅₀
26.5
no shift
SaO₂ at PaO₂ 60
90.0%
at the cliff
PaO₂ for SaO₂ 90%
60.0
to maintain saturation
Figure 3.5 — Try it (shift explorer). Move the pH, PaCO₂, and temperature sliders to shift the active curve. The amber P₅₀ marker (50% saturation) and red 90%-saturation marker both slide horizontally with the curve — that lateral movement is the right- or left-shift. The static gray dot at P₅₀ = 26.5 mmHg is the reference. Right shift (acidemia, hypercapnia, fever) raises P₅₀, drops SaO₂ at any given PaO₂, and unloads O₂ to tissue; left shift does the opposite.
Why the curve is sigmoidHemoglobin's four binding sites cooperate (positive cooperativity): the first O₂ molecule changes conformation to make subsequent binding easier. The result is a curve that's flat at the top (so PaO₂ can fall from 100 → 70 with little SaO₂ change) and steep in the middle (so once SaO₂ drops below 90%, every further mmHg matters).

4CO₂ transport & control of breathing

The ventilation side of gas exchange — dead space, the master equation PaCO₂ ∝ V̇CO₂ / V̇A, how CO₂ travels in blood, when diffusion limits matter, and the neural circuitry that decides how hard to breathe.

Figure 4.1 — What's in this chapter. The CO₂ master equation has two sides: production (V̇CO₂ — the metabolic side, §4.1a) and elimination (V̇A — alveolar ventilation, §4.1). Their ratio sets PaCO₂. §4.2 unpacks how CO₂ travels in blood (Haldane); §4.3 is when diffusion limitation matters; §4.4 is the neural circuitry that adjusts the equation in real time.

4.1 Dead space, alveolar ventilation, and PaCO₂
$$V_E \;=\; \text{RR} \times V_T$$
$$V_A \;=\; (V_T - V_d) \times \text{RR}$$
$$P_aCO_2 \;\propto\; \frac{VCO_2}{V_A} \quad \text{(master equation of CO}_2\text{ elimination)}$$
Variable key — ventilation equations
SymbolQuantityTypical units
VEMinute ventilationL/min
RRRespiratory ratebreaths/min
VTTidal volumemL or L
VAAlveolar ventilation (only the portion of VE reaching gas-exchanging alveoli)L/min
VdDead-space volume per breath (anatomic + alveolar + apparatus)mL
VCO₂CO₂ production rate by tissuesmL/min
PaCO₂Arterial partial pressure of CO₂mmHg
Figure 4.1a — Where every breath goes. Tidal volume VT splits into VA (alveolar ventilation, the only fraction that drives PaCO₂) and Vd (dead space — ventilated but no gas exchange). Vd has three compartments: anatomic (airways, mostly fixed), alveolar (rises in PE, ↑ PEEP, low CO, hyperinflation), and apparatus (HME, Y-piece, catheter mount, ETT lumen — the compartment you can directly shrink at the bedside). When PaCO₂ won't fall on adequate minute ventilation, the answer is usually in the alveolar or apparatus compartment.

Anatomic dead space ≈ 2 mL/kg (~150 mL adult). Physiologic dead space = anatomic + alveolar; the alveolar component rises in pulmonary embolism (PE), low-output states, high PEEP, hyperinflation, and ARDS.

Apparatus dead space — the bedside lever you can actually adjustDead space added by the circuit itself — HME (heat-and-moisture exchanger), Y-piece, connectors, catheter mount, and the ETT lumen distal to the carina — totals ~ 50–100 mL on most setups and is the only dead-space compartment under direct clinical control. In a 70-kg adult on 6 mL/kg PBW (VT ≈ 360 mL), a single 60-mL HME wastes ~ 17% of every breath as rebreathed gas — V̇A falls, PaCO₂ climbs, and the patient compensates with a higher RR (which then erodes Te and drives auto-PEEP in obstructive disease). Whenever PaCO₂ rises out of proportion to the set V̇E, look at the circuit before changing the vent: switching from an HME to heated humidification, shortening the catheter mount, or removing inline filters typically buys back 30–60 mL of VT per breath. See also §37 on humidification choices.

The Bohr-Enghoff dead-space equation at the bedside:

$$\frac{V_d}{V_T} \;=\; \frac{P_aCO_2 - P_{\bar{e}}CO_2}{P_aCO_2}$$

where PēCO₂ is mixed expired CO₂ (volumetric capnography). Normal Vd/VT is 0.20–0.40; values > 0.6 predict difficulty weaning and worse ARDS outcomes (Nuckton 2002).29 The arterial-to-end-tidal CO₂ gap, P(a−et)CO₂, is a clinical surrogate when full volumetric capnography isn't available.

The PEEP / dead-space tradeoffEvery increment of PEEP recruits some alveoli (good — fewer shunt units) but compresses pulmonary capillaries in already-open zones (bad — more dead space). When PaCO₂ rises after a PEEP increase, you've crossed into over-distension. The ETCO₂ trace is your real-time monitor.
4.1a CO₂ kinetics at the bedside — what changes VCO₂

The PaCO₂ ∝ VCO₂ / VA equation has two levers. Most clinicians focus on minute ventilation (VA); the production side (VCO₂) is just as variable in the ICU and explains many otherwise-puzzling PaCO₂ trends.

Drivers of CO₂ production (VCO₂) at the bedside
DirectionCauseEffect on VCO₂Vent implication
↑↑Fever (every °C above 37 raises VCO₂ ~10–13%)+ 10–25%Need more VE to maintain PaCO₂; cooling drops demand
↑↑Sepsis, SIRS, hyperthyroidism+ 20–50%Sudden rise in PaCO₂ on stable settings often = new sepsis, not vent failure
↑↑Shivering, agitation, work of breathing+ 30–100%Treat the cause; sedation and warming both reduce demand
↑Overfeeding (especially carbohydrate-heavy)+ 5–20%RQ rises → more CO₂ per O₂ consumed; reduce carbohydrate load in difficult-to-wean patients
↑Bicarbonate infusion+ transientHCO₃⁻ + H⁺ → H₂O + CO₂; PaCO₂ briefly rises
↓↓Targeted temperature management (current standard: targeted normothermia 36–37.5 °C per TTM2 202144; historical hypothermia at 33 °C reduced VCO₂ by ~30%)−15–30%Even at the modern normothermia target, suppression of fever and shivering lowers VCO₂ — watch for over-ventilation hypocapnia in patients whose minute ventilation was set during the febrile phase
↓↓Deep sedation, NMB− 10–15%Often reduces ventilator demand by relieving WOB
↓Starvation / underfeeding− 10–20%Rare cause of unexpectedly low PaCO₂
The PaCO₂ rose on the same vent — what changed?If VE is unchanged and PaCO₂ rose, the production side (VCO₂) probably increased: new fever, new sepsis, new agitation, recently fed, just gave bicarb. If VE dropped (asynchrony, missed triggers, sedation), VA is the cause. If neither changed, dead space (Vd/VT) probably did — re-check for PE, hyperinflation, or volume depletion.
Figure 4.1b — PaCO₂ diagnostic flowchart. Three sequential questions partition the cause into a bucket: ↓ delivery (VA), ↑ production (V̇CO₂), or ↑ waste (Vd/VT). The most common ICU answer is bucket 2 — fever, sepsis, shivering, or overfeeding. The bucket diagnosed determines the fix; the fix is rarely "turn up the vent."

4.2 CO₂ transport and the Haldane effect

The big idea first: hemoglobin is not just an O₂ carrier. It is a coupled O₂/CO₂ shuttle. Loading one of the two gases unloads the other — at the tissue, where the body needs O₂ delivered and CO₂ picked up, and at the lung, where the body needs the opposite. The widget below shows this happening in real time.

CO₂ travels in the blood in three forms: dissolved in plasma (~10%), as bicarbonate after carbonic-anhydrase-catalysed hydration in the red blood cell (~70%), and bound to hemoglobin as carbamino compounds (~20%). Two physiological effects, both rooted in the same allosteric switch between hemoglobin's T (taut, deoxygenated) and R (relaxed, oxygenated) conformations, drive the coupling:

  • The Bohr effect — at the tissue, the local rise in CO₂, H⁺, and temperature shifts the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve to the right. H⁺ binds to histidine residues on the β-globin chain, stabilizing the T-state, lowering O₂ affinity, and dumping O₂ exactly where it's needed (active, acidic, warm tissue).
  • The Haldane effect — at the lung, the rise in alveolar PO₂ binds heme iron, flips hemoglobin into the R-state, and lowers its affinity for both CO₂ and H⁺. CO₂ is displaced from carbamino compounds back into plasma (where the bicarbonate equilibrium runs in reverse, generating CO₂ for exhalation) — so oxygenation itself drives CO₂ off the hemoglobin and out the alveolus.

The two effects are mirror images of one another: the same conformational switch that releases O₂ at the tissue (Bohr) also picks up CO₂ there (Haldane in reverse), and the same switch that loads O₂ at the lung (Bohr in reverse) also drops CO₂ there (Haldane). Hemoglobin is therefore not just an O₂ carrier — it's a coupled O₂/CO₂ shuttle, and most of the CO₂-buffering capacity of the blood comes from this coupling.

Clinical consequences. Anemia reduces the Hb-mediated CO₂ buffer and the carbamino sink, so anemic patients tolerate respiratory acidosis less well at the same minute ventilation. Massive transfusion temporarily perturbs both: citrate-induced metabolic alkalosis can mask hypercapnia early, then the restored Hb improves CO₂ carriage. In COPD, raising FiO₂ above the patient's chronic level off-loads CO₂ from carbamino compounds and raises PaCO₂ — the Haldane effect contributes to oxygen-induced hypercapnia, but it is not the dominant mechanism. V/Q redistribution (release of hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction in poorly-ventilated regions → more dead space → less effective alveolar ventilation) accounts for the larger share of the rise in PaCO₂.42 See §3.2a for the full ranking of mechanisms and the bedside implications.

Bohr & Haldane effects

Reciprocal modulation of hemoglobin affinity by O₂, CO₂, H⁺, and bicarbonate during gas exchange

O₂ CO₂ H⁺ HCO₃⁻ Cl⁻ RBC
Ventilation 100%

Oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve

The curve shifts in real time based on tissue PCO₂ and pH. Right shift = more O₂ unloaded; left shift = O₂ held tighter.

Live conditions

Adjust ventilation to see how alveolar CO₂ clearance changes the system.

Tissue PCO₂46 mmHg
Tissue pH7.36
P₅₀ (shifted)28 mmHg
Curve shiftRight
O₂ delivered to tissue↑ Enhanced

Haldane effect (in lung)

O₂ binding pushes CO₂ off hemoglobin

  • O₂ loads onto deoxy-Hb at high alveolar PO₂
  • OxyHb is a weaker base — releases bound H⁺
  • H⁺ recombines with HCO₃⁻ → H₂CO₃ → CO₂ + H₂O
  • Carbamino-CO₂ also dissociates from Hb
  • CO₂ diffuses into alveolus and is exhaled

Clinical: minute ventilation drives CO₂ clearance; the Haldane effect roughly doubles CO₂ excretion vs. a passive system

Bohr effect (in tissue)

CO₂ and H⁺ push O₂ off hemoglobin

  • Tissue CO₂ enters RBC, hydrated by carbonic anhydrase
  • H⁺ binds Hb, stabilizing the deoxy (T) state
  • HCO₃⁻ exits RBC; Cl⁻ enters (chloride / Hamburger shift)
  • Hb affinity for O₂ falls — P₅₀ rises
  • Dissociation curve shifts right; more O₂ released

Clinical: hyperventilation causes alkalosis → left shift → impaired tissue O₂ delivery despite high SaO₂

Coupled chemistry
Hb·4O₂ + 2H⁺ + 2CO₂ ⇌ Hb·(H⁺)₂·(CO₂)₂ + 4O₂
← shift in lungs (Haldane) · shift in tissue (Bohr) →
Figure 4.2 — Bohr and Haldane effects (interactive). Animated gas exchange across the alveolus → capillary → tissue axis. The legend up top maps each particle (O₂, CO₂, H⁺, HCO₃⁻, Cl⁻); the ventilation slider lets you change alveolar CO₂ clearance and watch the live oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve shift right (more tissue PCO₂, lower pH, higher P₅₀, more O₂ released to tissue) or left (less tissue PCO₂, higher pH, lower P₅₀, O₂ held more tightly). The two side panels recap the chemistry: Haldane in the lung — O₂ binding displaces CO₂ off Hb; Bohr in the tissue — CO₂/H⁺ binding displaces O₂ off Hb. Same allosteric switch, mirror effects.
At the bedsideThe Haldane effect is the second mechanism (alongside HPV, §3.2a) behind the COPD patient whose PaCO₂ rises after high-flow O₂: oxygenated hemoglobin off-loads CO₂ into plasma, so a given VA now clears less of it. The fix isn't to drop FiO₂ to dangerously low levels — it's to titrate to SpO₂ 88–92% and watch for the rise. If PaCO₂ climbs anyway, that's a ventilation problem, not an oxygenation one — start non-invasive support (§27).

4.3 Diffusion vs perfusion limitation

Most gases (O₂ in health, N₂O, CO₂) are perfusion-limited — equilibration between alveolar gas and pulmonary capillary blood happens within the first third of capillary transit. Increasing blood flow raises uptake.

In disease, O₂ becomes diffusion-limited: when the alveolar–capillary membrane is thickened (fibrosis, ARDS) or transit time shortened (exercise, hyperdynamic state), capillary PO₂ never equilibrates with alveolar PO₂. The result: exertional hypoxemia in fibrosis, hypoxemia worsened by sepsis-induced high cardiac output in ARDS.28

Figure 4.3 — Diffusion vs perfusion limitation along the alveolar capillary. Each curve traces the partial pressure of one gas as a red blood cell traverses the pulmonary capillary (~0.75 s at rest, ~0.25 s in exercise; the cyan line shows the moving RBC). N₂O equilibrates almost immediately — its uptake is limited only by how much blood passes (perfusion-limited). O₂ in health equilibrates by ~0.25 s — also perfusion-limited. O₂ in fibrosis, ARDS, or during exercise never reaches the alveolar value within transit time — diffusion-limited. CO is the textbook diffusion-limited gas: it binds hemoglobin so avidly that the capillary blood never fills up — DLCO measurement exploits this. After West.28
Why this matters at the bedsideIf a patient becomes hypoxemic only on exertion with a relatively preserved resting PaO₂, suspect a diffusion limitation — fibrosis, pulmonary vascular disease, or early ARDS. The shortened transit time of exercise crosses the threshold where O₂ can't keep up. Conversely, the septic ARDS patient with hyperdynamic cardiac output is paradoxically worse oxygenated because of the same physiology — high CO compresses transit time. Slowing the heart (β-blocker in the right setting) sometimes helps; more often, the lever is recruitment and PEEP to enlarge the diffusing surface.

4.4 Control of breathing

Three sensors and one central pattern generator drive ventilation:

  • Central chemoreceptors (medullary surface) sense CSF pH, which tracks PaCO₂ via free diffusion of CO₂ across the blood-brain barrier. This is the dominant minute-to-minute drive in health.
  • Peripheral chemoreceptors (carotid + aortic bodies) sense PaO₂, PaCO₂, and pH. They become the dominant drive when PaO₂ < 60 mmHg, in chronic CO₂ retention, and when central drive is depressed (sedation, hypothyroidism).
  • Stretch receptors (Hering-Breuer reflex) terminate inspiration at large lung volumes — the Cushing-like reflex behind some asynchrony.

In the ICU, the implications are practical: over-sedation suppresses central drive (RR drops, PaCO₂ rises, dyssynchrony with the vent appears as missed triggers); metabolic acidosis drives a tachypneic respiratory compensation that the vent must accommodate (high-rate alarm misuse causes auto-PEEP); chronic CO₂ retention resets central thresholds — overzealous correction back to normal PaCO₂ causes alkalosis-induced hypoventilation when sedated.

The chronic CO₂ retainer's ventFor a COPD patient with baseline PaCO₂ 60, target PaCO₂ at their baseline, not 40. Acute correction to 40 produces metabolic alkalosis, hypokalemia, weakness, and seizure risk (Owens, Hess).30
Key takeaways
  • The five causes of hypoxemia: hypoventilation, low FiO₂, V/Q mismatch, shunt, diffusion impairment. Only shunt fails to correct on 100% O₂.
  • A–a gradient elevated → alveolar-capillary problem (V/Q, shunt, diffusion). A–a normal with hypoxemia → hypoventilation or low FiO₂.
  • Saturation × hemoglobin carries 98% of arterial O₂ content. Dissolved O₂ (0.003 × PaO₂) is trivial — don't chase PaO₂ > 80 with high FiO₂.
  • "Edge of the cliff": SpO₂ 90% ≈ PaO₂ 60. Below this, small drops in PaO₂ cause big drops in saturation.
  • Bohr: high CO₂/H⁺/T → right-shifts oxy-Hb curve → unloads O₂ to tissues. Haldane: high O₂ → loads CO₂ off Hb in lungs. They are mirror effects of allosteric switching.
  • Three drives: central chemoreceptors (PaCO₂ — dominant), peripheral chemoreceptors (PaO₂ < 60, chronic retention), stretch receptors. Don't normalize PaCO₂ to 40 in a chronic retainer.
Figure 4.4 — The neural control of ventilation. The medullary respiratory centers are the central pattern generator. Inputs: (1) central chemoreceptors on the medullary surface sense CSF H⁺ (which tracks PaCO₂ via free CO₂ diffusion across the blood-brain barrier, then hydration to H₂CO₃ and dissociation) — dominant minute-to-minute drive in health; (2) peripheral chemoreceptors in the carotid and aortic bodies sense PaO₂, PaCO₂, and H⁺ — dominant when PaO₂ < 60, in chronic CO₂ retention, and when central drive is depressed; (3) cerebral cortex for voluntary control; (4) pontine respiratory centers shape the rhythm; (5) pulmonary inputs — stretch, irritant, and J-receptors — provide reflexive feedback (Hering-Breuer, cough, J-reflex tachypnea in pulmonary edema). Output: phrenic and intercostal motor neurons → diaphragm and chest-wall muscles → lung movement.

5PFTs at the bedside — what spirometry tells you about a patient on the vent

You won't perform formal pulmonary function tests (PFTs) on an intubated patient — but every chronic vent patient comes with a chart, every COPD or interstitial lung disease (ILD) patient is set up by their pre-existing physiology, and every set of PFT numbers maps onto a vent strategy. The four PFT signatures every intensivist should recognize at a glance:30

The four PFT patterns
PatternFEV₁FVCFEV₁/FVCTLCDLCOFlow-volume signature
Normal≥ 80% pred≥ 80% pred≥ 0.70NormalNormalTriangular, smooth descent
Obstructive (COPD, asthma)↓↓↓ (less than FEV₁)< 0.70Normal or ↑ (hyperinflation)↓ in emphysema, normal in asthma"Scooped" / coved expiratory limb
Restrictive (fibrosis, neuromuscular, chest wall)↓↓↓≥ 0.70 (often ↑)↓ (the diagnostic finding)↓ in fibrosis; normal in extra-parenchymal restrictionSmall but normally-shaped (miniature triangle)
Mixed↓↓↓↓< 0.70↓VariableBoth scooped and small

5.1 The flow-volume loop — the most teachable PFT
Figure 5.1 — Pulmonary flow-volume loops. Flow (L/s) on the y-axis, volume (L) on the x-axis; expiration above the zero line, inspiration below; loops start at total lung capacity (TLC), expire to residual volume (RV), then inspire back to TLC. Normal — rapid rise to high PEF, smooth concave expiratory limb, symmetric inspiratory limb, normal FVC. Obstructive (asthma, COPD) — decreased PEF, markedly scooped concave expiratory limb from airflow limitation, ↓ FEF₅₀ and ↓ FEV₁, RV increased from air trapping. Restrictive (pulmonary fibrosis, chest-wall disorders) — normal or near-normal peak flow, narrow loop from reduced lung volumes, steep expiratory limb, ↓ TLC and ↓ FVC, FEV₁/FVC normal or ↑. Fixed UAO (tracheal/subglottic stenosis, large tracheal tumor) — plateau on both inspiratory and expiratory limbs, suggesting a fixed obstruction at or below the level of the vocal cords. Variable extrathoracic (vocal-cord dysfunction, laryngeal tumor) — flattened inspiratory limb, normal expiratory limb (obstruction outside the thorax limits flow on inspiration). Variable intrathoracic (tracheomalacia, intrathoracic tumor) — flattened expiratory limb, normal inspiratory limb (obstruction inside the thorax limits flow on expiration). Key point: loop shape distinguishes obstructive (scooped) from restrictive (small but tall) patterns.30

5.2 What each pattern means for the vent
  • Obstructive — long expiratory time constant (τ = R × C). Vent strategy: low rate (8–12), I:E 1:3 to 1:5, accept permissive hypercapnia, watch for auto-PEEP. Set extrinsic PEEP slightly below the measured intrinsic PEEP (commonly 70–85%) so the patient's inspiratory effort only has to overcome a small additional threshold to trigger a breath. Auto-PEEP measurement and the rationale for matched extrinsic PEEP are covered in §17.
  • Restrictive — parenchymal (pulmonary fibrosis, ARDS): small and stiff lungs with reduced DLCO. Vent strategy: low VT (4–6 mL/kg PBW for ARDS; 6 mL/kg for chronic ILD if no acute injury), modest PEEP (lung needs splinting), higher RR to maintain minute ventilation. Watch driving pressure ΔP ≤ 15 cmH₂O (§30).
  • Restrictive — extra-parenchymal (kyphoscoliosis, severe obesity, abdominal hypertension): small lungs with preserved DLCO — the parenchyma is fine, the chest wall or abdomen is the problem. Vent strategy: PEEP often needs to be higher (12–18) to overcome chest-wall stiffness; esophageal manometry helps (§29); ΔP based on transpulmonary pressure, not airway pressure.
  • Neuromuscular — lungs are normal but the pump fails. Vent strategy: higher VT acceptable (6–10 mL/kg) since lung mechanics are intact; rate to match drive; aggressive secretion clearance, cough assist (§43).
  • Mixed — clinical judgment. Identify which axis is acutely deranged (the obstructive component if PIP–Pplat is wide; the restrictive component if Pplat − PEEP is wide) and target that one first.
The "preserved DLCO" tell — parenchymal vs extra-parenchymal restrictionRestrictive pattern with preserved DLCO (typically > 75% predicted) → the parenchyma is intact and the gas-exchange surface is normal; the problem is the chest wall, diaphragm, or abdomen. Causes: kyphoscoliosis, obesity, ascites, abdominal compartment syndrome, neuromuscular weakness. Restrictive pattern with reduced DLCO → the parenchyma itself is destroyed or thickened. Causes: idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, sarcoidosis (advanced), pulmonary edema, ARDS. The distinction changes the entire vent strategy: extra-parenchymal restriction tolerates higher VT and benefits from higher PEEP to splint the chest wall; parenchymal restriction demands lung-protective volumes and PEEP titrated to the lung itself (esophageal manometry, §29).

5.3 GOLD severity for COPD — the number every IM physician needs48

The Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease (GOLD) stages airflow limitation by post-bronchodilator FEV₁ as a percentage of predicted, after obstruction has been confirmed (FEV₁/FVC < 0.70):

GOLD spirometric staging (post-bronchodilator FEV₁, in confirmed obstruction)
StageFEV₁ % predictedSeverityWhat it means at the bedside
GOLD 1≥ 80%MildOften asymptomatic at rest; usually liberates from vent without difficulty
GOLD 250–79%ModerateReduced exercise tolerance; baseline-dependent on ABG; weans with attention to bronchodilators and secretion clearance
GOLD 330–49%SevereChronic CO₂ retention common; high risk of auto-PEEP and weaning failure; consider tracheostomy planning early (§37)
GOLD 4< 30%Very severeOften non-rescuable from acute decompensation without prior advance-care planning; baseline HFNC/BiPAP dependence common

The newer GOLD ABE assessment combines spirometry with symptoms (mMRC, CAT) and exacerbation history to guide outpatient therapy; for vent-management purposes, the FEV₁ stage is what matters because it predicts the time-constant problem (§6.4) and the wean-failure risk.

5.4 Worked example — reading a chart PFT before you set the vent

Clinic PFT from your patient's chart:

Example chart PFT
MeasureValue% predicted
FEV₁1.2 L45%
FVC2.4 L62%
FEV₁/FVC0.50—
TLC7.8 L110%
RV4.6 L175%
DLCO11.5 mL/min/mmHg55%

Read it. FEV₁/FVC < 0.70 → obstruction. FEV₁ 45% pred → GOLD 3 (severe). TLC 110% with RV 175% → hyperinflation (air trapping). DLCO 55% → reduced (parenchymal destruction = emphysema, not pure asthma). This is severe emphysematous COPD.

What it means for the vent.

  • Long expiratory τ → low RR (8–10), long TE (I:E 1:4 or 1:5).
  • Auto-PEEP risk is high — measure it on day 1; set extrinsic PEEP at ~70–85% of intrinsic.
  • Permissive hypercapnia: PaCO₂ 55–70 with pH ≥ 7.20 is acceptable; chasing PaCO₂ to 40 forces high V̇E and worsens hyperinflation.
  • Weaning will be protracted — DLCO 55% predicts difficulty, and prior FEV₁ 45% suggests baseline CO₂ retention. Plan early: SBT criteria (§36), trach discussion at day 5–7 (§37).
  • Don't aim for ARDSnet 6 mL/kg — this isn't ARDS. 6–8 mL/kg PBW with a focus on TE rather than VT is appropriate.

5.5 Mixed pattern — when the chart shows both

Case. 68-year-old former smoker with combined emphysema + interstitial fibrosis (combined pulmonary fibrosis and emphysema, CPFE). Clinic PFT 6 months ago: FEV₁ 1.1 L (38%), FVC 1.8 L (50%), FEV₁/FVC 0.61, TLC 4.9 L (74%), DLCO 7.0 (35%). Now intubated for community-acquired pneumonia and worsening hypoxemia.

Reading. FEV₁/FVC < 0.70 with both FEV₁ and FVC reduced disproportionately to TLC → mixed. TLC < 80% = restrictive component present. Markedly reduced DLCO (35%) → both compartments contributing. The CPFE phenotype is notorious for severe pulmonary hypertension and rapid decompensation.

What to do on the vent.

  • Compromise VT: 6 mL/kg PBW (restrictive lower bound), monitor ΔP and PIP.
  • Compromise RR: start at 14–16 (between obstructive low and restrictive high); accept hypercapnia rather than push faster.
  • PEEP modest (8–10) — high PEEP overdistends emphysematous bullae and worsens dead space; low PEEP derecruits fibrotic regions.
  • Trial of bronchodilators (mostly to address any reversible component); aggressive treatment of the pneumonia.
  • Discuss goals of care early — CPFE acute decompensations have high mortality and survivors usually require home O₂ ± transplant evaluation.

5.6 The single number to remember on rounds

FEV₁/FVC < 0.70 = obstruction. TLC < 80% predicted = restriction. Both = mixed. DLCO splits restriction into parenchymal (reduced) vs extra-parenchymal (preserved). Everything else is detail.

Key takeaways
  • FEV₁/FVC < 0.70 = obstruction; TLC < 80% = restriction; both = mixed pattern.
  • Flow-volume loop shape diagnoses the lesion: scooped expiration = COPD; restrictive = small normal triangle; fixed upper-airway obstruction = box-shaped.
  • Preserved DLCO in restrictive pattern points to chest-wall or neuromuscular disease (good lungs, bad pump); reduced DLCO = parenchymal injury (fibrosis, ARDS). Vent strategies differ.
  • GOLD stage (FEV₁ % predicted in confirmed obstruction) predicts auto-PEEP risk and weaning difficulty: ≥ 80% mild, 50–79% moderate, 30–49% severe, < 30% very severe.
  • Pre-existing PFTs map onto vent strategy: obstructive needs low RR and long exhalation; parenchymal restrictive needs lung-protective VT and modest PEEP; extra-parenchymal restrictive (obesity, ascites) needs higher PEEP to splint the chest wall; neuromuscular needs higher VT with intact lungs.

6Lung mechanics — compliance, resistance, time constants

6.1 The equation of motion
$$P_{aw} \;=\; \frac{V}{C} \;+\; (\dot{V} \times R) \;+\; \text{PEEP}$$
Variable key — equation of motion (and the equations that follow)
SymbolQuantityTypical units
PawAirway pressure (instantaneous)cmH₂O
VLung volume above functional residual capacity (FRC) — i.e., delivered tidal volume at the moment in questionL (or mL)
CRespiratory-system compliance (lung + chest wall)L/cmH₂O (or mL/cmH₂O)
V̇ (V-dot)Inspiratory flow rate at that instantL/sec
RAirway resistance (Raw)cmH₂O/L/sec
PEEPPositive end-expiratory pressure (machine baseline + any auto-PEEP)cmH₂O

Every airway pressure during a breath is the sum of three components — each one a different physical force the ventilator (or the diaphragm) must work against. Memorize how each maps to the bedside; the rest of this chapter is just unpacking these three terms.

The three components of Paw, decomposed
TermWhat it representsHow to read it on the traceGoes up when…
V / C
volume-elastic		 Pressure required to stretch the lung + chest wall by the delivered volume V at compliance C. This is the elastic recoil that builds as the lung fills. Pplat − PEEP after a 0.5–2 s end-inspiratory hold (V̇ = 0, so the resistive term vanishes). Equivalently, VT / (Pplat − PEEP) gives compliance C. Stiff lung (ARDS, edema, fibrosis, atelectasis), stiff chest wall (obesity, ascites, abdominal hypertension), or excessive VT.
V̇ × R
flow-resistive		 Pressure required to push gas through the airways and the ETT at instantaneous flow V̇, against airway resistance R. PIP − Pplat during constant-flow VC inspiration. Disappears at end-inspiratory hold. Equivalently, R = (PIP − Pplat) / V̇. Bronchospasm, airway secretions, kinked / biting / undersized ETT, water in the circuit, mucous plug.
PEEP
baseline		 End-expiratory airway pressure already in the system before the next breath begins. Set PEEP + any auto-PEEP from incomplete exhalation. The trough between breaths on the pressure trace. End-expiratory hold reveals total PEEP; total − set = auto-PEEP (§17). You set it (extrinsic PEEP), or the lung fails to empty (auto-PEEP / dynamic hyperinflation).

Total airway pressure at any moment during a breath = (V/C now) + (V̇ × R now) + (PEEP). When the patient breathes spontaneously, a fourth term — Pmus, the pressure generated by the inspiratory muscles — pulls airway pressure down; the equation becomes Paw = V/C + V̇·R + PEEP − Pmus. Almost every bedside ventilator concept in the rest of this document is one of these four terms in disguise.

How the equation of motion shows up at the bedside
ConceptWhich term of the equationWhere to find it in this doc
Driving pressure (ΔP)Volume-elastic term (V/C) at end-inspiratory hold§16, §30
Resistive pressure (PIP − Pplat)Flow-resistive term (V̇ × R)§6.3, §16
Auto-PEEP / dynamic hyperinflationPEEP term plus residual elastic recoil from incomplete exhalation§17, §21
Mechanical power (MP)Integral of pressure × volume × rate over time§30
Mode choice (VC vs PC)Which variable you fix (V or P) and which becomes dependent§8–§12
AsynchronyPatient effort adds a fourth term (Pmus) — pulls airway pressure down§18
Stress & strain (VILI math)Volume-elastic term decomposed: stress = transpulmonary P, strain = ΔV/FRC§29, §30
Try it — interactive equation-of-motion decomposition

Drag compliance, resistance, VT, and PEEP to see how each of the three terms stacks into airway pressure. The vanishing of the red (resistive) band during the end-inspiratory hold is the visual answer to the "PIP high? Pplat high?" question that diagnoses most pressure alarms (§6.3).

Equation of motion — decomposing every breath
Paw(t) = V(t)/C + V̇(t)·R + PEEP. Drag the three components to see how each colored band stacks into total airway pressure.
PIP
25
peak insp. (cmH₂O)
Pplat
15
end-insp hold (cmH₂O)
ΔP (driving)
10
Pplat − PEEP
Resistive ΔP
10
PIP − Pplat
Elastic (V/C) Resistive (V̇·R) PEEP (baseline) Total Paw
Figure 6.1 — Equation of motion, decomposed. Paw(t) = V(t)/C + V̇(t)·R + PEEP, in real time across one breath. Note that the resistive band (red) collapses to zero during the end-inspiratory hold — that's why Pplat reads only elastic + PEEP, the basis of the bedside two-question algorithm in §6.3.

6.1a Closing capacity vs FRC — why some patients desaturate fast

Closing capacity (CC) is the lung volume at which small airways (those without cartilage to splint them) begin to collapse during exhalation. Below CC, the airway lumen closes and the alveoli behind it can no longer ventilate. FRC is the normal end-expiratory volume. The relationship between these two — not the absolute number of either — predicts who develops atelectasis and shunt.

Why CC moves at all: small airways are kept open by two opposing forces. Radial traction from surrounding alveoli (alveolar attachments to the airway wall pull it open) and airway smooth-muscle tone hold the lumen open; surface tension at the airway lining and extramural pressure from surrounding tissue push it closed. CC rises whenever the radial traction is lost (emphysematous destruction of alveolar attachments) or the closing force grows (mucosal edema, secretions, smooth-muscle hyperresponsiveness). FRC drops whenever the chest-wall recoil outward weakens or the abdominal/intrathoracic pressure pushing inward grows.

Drivers of CC and FRC at the bedside
ConditionFRCCCMechanism
Healthy young, uprightNormalNormal (CC ≪ FRC)Tidal breathing well above closing volume; no atelectasis.
AgingStable until late decades↑ ~ 50 mL per year of lifeLoss of elastin from airway walls and surrounding parenchyma → less radial traction. By age ~ 45 supine, by age ~ 65 upright, CC reaches FRC and tidal breathing dips into the closing zone.
Smoking, chronic bronchitis, early COPDVariable (often ↑ with hyperinflation)↑↑ — earlyMucosal inflammation thickens the airway wall, narrows the lumen, and accelerates the loss of elastin. CC rises long before FEV1 changes — the small-airway disease that smokers develop in their 30s–40s.
Asthma / bronchospasm↑ (hyperinflation)↑↑Smooth-muscle constriction + mucosal edema + mucus plugging close the airways at higher lung volumes.
Obesity↓↓ — up to 50%≈ Normal at baselineThe chest-wall (abdominal compartment) compliance falls; supine diaphragm rides higher; ERV is consumed. CC didn't change but the FRC/CC ratio inverts — tidal breaths now happen inside the closing zone → shunt at rest.
Pregnancy (term)↓ ~ 20% supineNormalGravid uterus elevates the diaphragm; same FRC/CC inversion as obesity.
Supine position alone↓ ~ 20% vs uprightStableLoss of gravity-assisted chest expansion and abdominal contents pressing on the diaphragm.
General anesthesia / sedation / paralysis↓ ~ 20% additionalStable or ↑Loss of inspiratory muscle tone (especially diaphragm) collapses dependent lung. Combined with supine: FRC down ~ 40% from upright awake — happens within minutes of induction.
Acute lung injury / ARDS↓↓↑↑Edema floods alveoli (FRC down); surfactant loss raises closing pressure of small airways (CC up). Both forces converge → widespread atelectasis and shunt.
Cardiogenic pulmonary edema↓↑Interstitial cuffs of edema around airways narrow the lumen and raise CC; alveolar flooding drops FRC.
Abdominal hypertension / ascites↓StableDiaphragm pushed cranially; same mechanical mechanism as obesity.
Trendelenburg position, surgery↓StableGravity pushes the abdominal contents into the diaphragm; reverse the position to recover.

What unifies all of these: anything that raises CC or drops FRC moves tidal breathing closer to the closing zone. Whoever sits closest to (or below) the line desaturates fastest in apnea and benefits most from PEEP that lifts the operating volume back above CC.

Why the obese patient desaturates in 60 secondsFRC has dropped to ~ 30% of predicted; closing capacity is unchanged or higher; almost the entire tidal range is below closing volume. There's almost no oxygen reservoir to draw from during apnea. This is why pre-oxygenation, ramping, and apneic oxygenation are mandatory — and why even brief desaturation occurs minutes after induction in non-obese patients but seconds after in obese patients.
The clinical lever: PEEP raises FRC above CCThat's the single most important physiologic effect of PEEP — not just "open the alveolus," but "lift the operating volume back above closing capacity." This is why PEEP 8–12 helps the obese anesthetized patient even without ARDS, why post-operative atelectasis is treated with CPAP, and why supine position alone causes shunt that PEEP can fix.

6.2 Compliance

Compliance (C) is the volume change you get per unit of pressure applied: C = ΔV / ΔP. It is the inverse of stiffness — high compliance means a soft, easily-distensible lung; low compliance means a stiff one. Two flavors are measured at the bedside:

  • Static compliance (Cstat) = VT / (Pplat − PEEP). Read at end-inspiratory hold (V̇ = 0), so the resistive contribution is gone and only the elastic recoil of lung + chest wall remains. Normal in a healthy intubated adult: 60–100 mL/cmH₂O. ARDS commonly 20–40; severe restrictive disease < 20.
  • Dynamic compliance (Cdyn) = VT / (PIP − PEEP). Includes the resistive overhead, so it's always lower than Cstat. Useful for trending; not for setting strategy. The Cstat − Cdyn gap is a rough resistance index — small gap = low R; widening gap = bronchospasm or secretions developing.
Two compliances that combine — and why this matters

What the ventilator measures (Crs, the respiratory-system compliance) is actually a combination of two distinct mechanical elements — the lung and the chest wall — that the breath has to stretch simultaneously. They add as reciprocals (the same algebra as resistors in parallel or capacitors in series), giving a measured Crs that is always lower than either component alone:

$$\frac{1}{C_{rs}} = \frac{1}{C_L} + \frac{1}{C_{CW}}$$
  • Lung compliance (CL) — the lung tissue + alveolar interface. Falls in ARDS, edema, fibrosis, atelectasis, hemorrhage, tumor, infection. Rises in emphysema (loss of elastic recoil).
  • Chest-wall compliance (CCW) — the rib cage + abdominal wall + diaphragm. Falls in obesity, ascites, abdominal compartment syndrome, kyphoscoliosis, large pleural effusions, chest-wall edema, tense pneumothorax, and circumferential burns.
  • Respiratory-system compliance (Crs) — what the dial shows. A low number can come from a stiff lung, a stiff chest wall, or both — and the management is completely different.
How disease shifts the three compliance components
ConditionCLCCWCrs (measured)
Healthy adult~ 200 mL/cmH₂O~ 200 mL/cmH₂O~ 100 mL/cmH₂O
ARDS (parenchymal injury)↓↓ (40–80)≈ Normal↓↓ (20–40)
ARDS + obesity / abdominal HTN↓↓↓↓↓↓
Emphysema (advanced)↑↑ (loss of recoil)≈ Normal↑ — but with hyperinflation, the operating point is on the flat top of the P-V curve, so functional compliance is low.
Pulmonary fibrosis↓↓↓≈ Normal↓↓
Cardiogenic pulmonary edema↓ (variable)≈ Normal↓
Pure obesity (no lung disease)≈ Normal↓↓↓ — looks like ARDS but the lung is fine.
Late pregnancy, supine≈ Normal↓↓
Pleural effusion (large)≈ Normal↓↓
The lung-vs-chest-wall ambiguity matters at the bedsideA morbidly obese ARDS patient may have Pplat 35 not because the lung is on the verge of barotrauma but because the chest wall is heavy and the abdomen pushes the diaphragm up. Pushing Pplat ≤ 30 by lowering VT may under-ventilate that patient. Esophageal manometry (§29) decomposes Pplat into transpulmonary pressure (the lung sees) and pleural pressure (the chest wall imposes), telling you which compartment to act on.

Specific compliance = Crs / FRC. This normalizes for lung size — small lungs always have lower absolute compliance. Specific compliance is approximately constant (~ 0.05/cmH₂O) across mammals; a baby with Crs = 5 mL/cmH₂O can have perfectly normal specific compliance. Useful when interpreting low absolute numbers in pediatrics or in the "baby lung" of severe ARDS where only ~ 20% of lung is aerated and the absolute compliance reflects that small recruitable volume, not intrinsic lung stiffness.

Track Cstat day to dayThe single most useful trended number on the vent. A falling Cstat with stable resistance is the lung becoming stiffer — evolving ARDS, edema, fibrosis, atelectasis. A rising Cstat is improvement (or, less commonly, derecruitment of overdistended lung after PEEP reduction). Driving pressure (Pplat − PEEP) is Cstat's mirror image: ΔP = VT / Cstat.

6.3 Resistance

Resistance (R) is the pressure required to drive gas flow through the airway tree, per unit of flow: R = ΔPresistive / V̇. In a constant-flow VC breath the resistive pressure is exactly the gap between PIP and Pplat:

$$R_{aw} \;=\; \frac{P_{IP} - P_{plat}}{\dot{V}} \quad \text{(constant-flow VC; }\dot{V}\text{ in L/sec)}$$

Normal airway resistance (Raw) on an intubated adult: < 5 cmH₂O / (L/s). In disease it climbs to 10–20+; in catastrophic bronchospasm or severe mucus plugging, > 30.

Components of resistance — they sit in series

Total inspiratory resistance is the sum of several segments, each a candidate for sudden change at the bedside:

  1. The endotracheal tube (ETT). The biggest single component you can do anything about. Bigger tubes are easier to breathe through — a 6.0 has roughly 2× the resistance of a 7.0; a 7.0 has roughly 1.5× the resistance of an 8.0. If a patient is failing weaning trials with a small tube in place, upsizing the ETT before the next SBT can be the move that gets them off the vent. The physics behind it: resistance scales as ~ 1/radius4 at low flow (Poiseuille) and even more steeply at high flow (where turbulence dominates and the radius-dependence steepens further). Approximate values in adults: 7.0 mm ID ≈ 6 cmH₂O/(L/s); 8.0 mm ≈ 4; 6.0 mm ≈ 12. A bitten, kinked, or secretion-plugged ETT can quadruple this in seconds.
  2. Conducting airways (large bronchi → bronchioles). The dominant component in disease — bronchospasm, mucosal edema, mucus plugging, secretions, dynamic compression in obstructive disease.
  3. Tissue resistance (lung and chest-wall viscoelasticity). Small contribution; usually neglected in acute decisions but accounts for the slow drift on the inspiratory hold (§14.7).
  4. Apparatus / circuit resistance (inline filters, HME, catheter mounts). Small but additive. Worth checking on a "stuck" pressure trace.
How resistance varies with flow and lung volume
  • Flow-dependent: at higher flow rates, gas movement through the airway tree becomes turbulent and R rises (turbulent resistance scales with velocity, not just radius).
