Measuring Raw at 60 L/min gives a different number than at 30 L/min. Standard practice: measure at moderate flow (~ 30–60 L/min ≈ 0.5–1 L/sec).
  • Volume-dependent: small airways are tethered open by radial traction from surrounding alveoli. At higher lung volume, traction is greater and small-airway R falls; at lower lung volume, R rises. This is why end-expiratory R is higher than end-inspiratory R, why severe bronchospasm worsens at low volumes, and why PEEP can transiently reduce small-airway resistance.
  • Phase-dependent: in obstructive disease, expiratory R is much higher than inspiratory R because dynamic compression of small airways during exhalation (positive pleural pressure squeezing the airway from outside) closes them off. This asymmetry is the basis of dynamic hyperinflation and auto-PEEP.
Disease-specific resistance patterns
Raw in common ICU phenotypes
PhenotypeRawMechanism / fix
COPD exacerbation↑↑↑Mucus + bronchospasm + dynamic small-airway collapse on exhalation. Bronchodilators, anticholinergics, steroids; allow long Te.
Acute severe asthma↑↑↑↑Smooth-muscle bronchospasm dominates; mucus plugging and inflammation contribute. Aggressive bronchodilation; permissive hypercapnia; ketamine, magnesium, heliox in refractory cases.
Secretion plug / mucus in ETTSudden ↑↑PIP rises while Pplat stays flat; the gap explodes. Suction, lavage, sometimes replace ETT.
Kinked / bitten ETTSudden ↑↑↑Bite block, sedation, position the head, replace tube.
Water in the circuitVariable, often saw-tooth on flow traceDrain water traps; check humidifier/HME.
ARDS (uncomplicated)Normal to mildly ↑The pathology is in the alveolus, not the airway. If Raw is high in ARDS, look for a complication (bronchospasm, secretions).
Cardiogenic pulmonary edema↑ ("cardiac asthma")Peribronchial cuffing of fluid narrows the lumen; resolves with diuresis and PEEP.
Anaphylaxis↑↑↑Bronchospasm + mucosal edema + upper-airway swelling; epinephrine first-line.
The two-question diagnostic algorithm(1) Is PIP high? (2) Is Pplat high? Yes/No: resistance problem — fix the airway (suction, bronchodilator, replace ETT, treat bronchospasm). Yes/Yes: lung-mechanics problem — ARDS, edema, atelectasis, abdominal hypertension. This dichotomy alone solves most pressure alarms. Do not raise PEEP for a resistive problem.
Inhaled bronchodilator responseA 10–30% drop in Raw after albuterol within 10–15 min confirms reversible bronchospasm. No response argues for fixed obstruction (chronic bronchitis, mucus plug, ETT issue) and changes the management ladder.

6.4 Time constants
$$\tau \;=\; R \times C \quad \text{(time constant, in seconds)}$$

The time constant is the time required for an exponentially-decaying lung unit to empty (or fill) by ~63%. After 1τ the unit has emptied 63%; after 2τ, 86%; after 3τ, 95%; after 5τ, ≥ 99%. The same math governs filling on inspiration. The clinical rule of thumb: full equilibration requires 3–5 τ.

6.4.1 Why time constants matter clinically
  • Auto-PEEP / dynamic hyperinflation — in severe COPD/asthma, R is markedly elevated → τ > 1.5–2 s. If the ventilator's expiratory time (Te) is shorter than 3τ, the lung cannot empty before the next breath; gas trapping and intrinsic PEEP develop. This is the physiologic justification for low respiratory rate (8–10) and prolonged I:E (1:4–1:5) in obstructed patients (Ch 21).
  • Recruitment heterogeneity in ARDS — ARDS lungs have regionally different time constants. Healthy alveoli have short τ (low R, normal C). Atelectatic regions have long τ (need higher pressure for longer to recruit). Overdistended regions have short τ but already at the top of the compliance curve. Single-PEEP solutions are always compromises; this is why personalized titration (best-compliance, esophageal-guided) outperforms one-size-fits-all approaches.
6.4.2 Cycling threshold and inspiratory time — getting the breath length right

Every breath has to end somewhere. How the ventilator decides to end inspiration depends on the mode:

  • Volume control (VC) — inspiration ends when the set VT has been delivered (volume-cycled).
  • Pressure control (PC) — inspiration ends when the set inspiratory time Ti elapses (time-cycled).
  • Pressure support (PSV) — inspiration ends when inspiratory flow falls below a threshold expressed as a percentage of the peak inspiratory flow of that breath (flow-cycled). The default is 25%.

In all three, the goal is for the vent's mechanical Ti to match the patient's neural Ti — how long they actually want inspiration to last. When they don't match you get cycling asynchrony (§18.5–18.8): premature cycling if the vent ends inspiration too early, delayed cycling if too late.

The full bedside playbook for setting and tuning the cycling threshold — including phenotype-specific PSV cycling targets and the PC Ti = 3–5τ rule — depends on concepts (Pmus, neural Ti, double-triggering) that the book hasn't introduced yet. That material lives in §18.6a, in the asynchrony chapter, where the cycling waveforms and recognition logic are already on the table.

6.4.3 Measuring τ at the bedside

Modern ventilators do not display τ directly, but you can compute or visualize it from waveforms and bedside maneuvers:

  1. From the equation — τ (sec) = Raw (cmH₂O/L/sec) × Cstat (L/cmH₂O). With Raw from the PIP–Pplat gradient (resistive pressure / inspiratory flow) and Cstat = VT/(Pplat − PEEP). Example: VT 0.5 L, Pplat 22, PEEP 8, PIP 32, flow 1 L/sec → Raw = 10/1 = 10; Cstat = 0.5/14 ≈ 0.036 L/cmH₂O; τ = 10 × 0.036 = 0.36 sec. Te should be ≥ 1 sec to ensure complete emptying.
  2. From the expiratory flow trace — the time from peak expiratory flow to the point where flow has decayed to 37% of peak (i.e., 1 − 1/e) equals the expiratory time constant. Visually: if expiratory flow is still > 0 at the next breath, τ > available Te — auto-PEEP is occurring (Ch 17).
  3. From an end-expiratory pause maneuver — measures total PEEP. Total PEEP − set PEEP = auto-PEEP. Auto-PEEP > 0 means τ exceeded Te.
  4. From volume re-expansion on disconnect — disconnect the patient from the vent for 5–10 seconds. The amount of additional gas that flows out is the trapped volume. (Useful in catastrophic auto-PEEP / hemodynamic collapse — Ch 19.4.)
The bedside τ checkIf you suspect auto-PEEP: pause the ventilator's flow trace and look at exhalation. Flow should reach zero before the next inspiration (≥ 3τ exhalation time). If it doesn't, you're hyperinflating — lower the rate or VT, lengthen Te, or both. This single visual check substitutes for the math in > 90% of bedside cases.

Try it — interactive time-constant filling. Drag τ and Ti in the figure below to see how the lung fills as a function of how many time constants the vent gives it. The 1τ / 2τ / 3τ rule (63% / 86% / 95%) emerges directly. Use this to calibrate intuition before reading the stress-index section.

VT at end-Ti
63%
of plateau VT
Ti / τ
1.7
target ≥ 3
Status
underfilled
Figure 6.1 — Try it (interactive time-constant filling). Drag τ and Ti to see how filling evolves. The 1τ / 2τ / 3τ rule (63% / 86% / 95%) is visible: at Ti = τ the breath is only two-thirds in. Set τ to 1.4 s (COPD) and Ti to 1 s and see how VT plateaus far below maximum — explaining why short Ti in obstructed patients delivers less than expected. Conversely, in ARDS short τ means even brief Ti achieves near-full equilibration.
6.4.4 Stress index — reading regional mechanics from the pressure trace

The stress index is a quantitative description of the shape of the airway-pressure-vs-time curve during a constant-flow VC inspiration. It tells you whether tidal breathing is occurring on the linear (uniform-compliance) portion of the lung's pressure–volume curve, or whether the breath is being driven into overdistension (above the upper inflection) or is still recruiting alveoli (below the lower inflection). Mathematically, during a passive constant-flow inspiration, airway pressure rises as a power function of time:

$$P_{aw}(t) \;=\; a \cdot t^{\beta} \;+\; c$$
Stress index equation — what each term is
TermWhat it representsDetermined by
Paw(t)Instantaneous airway pressure during the inspiratory ramp (cmH₂O)What the vent measures and the screen plots
tTime elapsed from the start of inspiration (sec)The ramp itself; a fitting variable, not a setting
aScaling coefficient (cmH₂O · s−β) — sets the absolute pressure scale of the rampPatient mechanics (R, C) and the set inspiratory flow; not a knob you turn
βThe stress index itself — the dimensionless exponent that captures how the curve bends across the breathRegional lung mechanics during this breath: recruitment, linear compliance, or overdistension
cConstant offset (cmH₂O) — pressure at the start of the ramp≈ PEEP plus the resistive pressure drop at the moment flow begins

Only β changes shape; a and c set the absolute pressure scale but not the curvature. Three values of β matter at the bedside:

Figure 6.2 — The three shapes of the stress index. A passive, constant-flow VC breath produces a pressure ramp whose shape reveals regional lung mechanics. β < 1 (concave-down): pressure rises quickly at first, then flattens — the curve bulges above the straight reference line; compliance is improving as alveoli recruit, and the lung is cyclically opening with each breath (atelectrauma). β ≈ 1 (linear): pressure rises in a straight line, overlapping the reference; compliance is constant and the breath sits in the safe operating zone. β > 1 (concave-up): pressure rises slowly at first, then accelerates — the curve sags below the reference; compliance is falling as alveoli reach the upper inflection, and the breath is being driven into overdistension. The dashed line in each panel is the straight reference drawn from the start of inspiration to PIP; comparing the actual trace to this reference is the eyeball read of β.
Stress index — visual interpretation
β valuePressure-time shapeWhat's happening in the lungBedside action
β ≈ 1Linear pressure rise (a straight ramp)Compliance is roughly constant during the breath — the lung is operating on the steep, linear portion of its P–V curve. Settings are appropriate.Maintain.
β < 1Concave-down (pressure rises fast initially, then flattens — curve sits above the straight line drawn from start to peak)Compliance is increasing as volume rises — recruitment is ongoing during the breath. The lung is opening alveoli with each tidal cycle (atelectrauma). PEEP is too low.↑ PEEP to hold the alveoli open between breaths instead of recruiting them on each one.
β > 1Concave-up (pressure rises slowly at first, then accelerates — curve sits below the straight line)Compliance is decreasing as volume rises — the breath is being driven into overdistension. Tidal volume or PEEP is too high; the lung is on the upper flat region of its P–V curve.↓ VT first (most direct lever), then consider ↓ PEEP if Pplat remains high.

Reading β by eye on the pressure-time scalar. You don't need the algorithm to use the stress index — the visual is fast and surprisingly reliable.

  1. Pause the pressure-time scalar during a passive constant-flow VC breath (no spontaneous effort).
  2. Mentally draw a straight line from the start of the inspiratory ramp (just above PEEP) to the PIP.
  3. Compare the actual pressure curve to that line: above the line in the middle → β < 1, recruitment ongoing → consider raising PEEP. Below the line in the middle → β > 1, overdistension → drop VT. On the line → β ≈ 1, leave it alone.

Grasso and colleagues (2007) showed that this eyeball method correlates well with the algorithmic β, and that titrating PEEP to keep β in the linear (≈ 1) zone reduces overdistension as well as ICU-physician PEEP titration.40 The stress index is one of the few tools that can be applied with no extra equipment — every modern vent shows the trace.

How it fits in ARDS protective ventilation. Use it alongside, not instead of, the other two anchors: Pplat ≤ 30 as a ceiling, driving pressure ΔP ≤ 15 as the strain proxy. The stress index gives the third dimension — the shape of the breath, which neither ceiling nor driving pressure captures. A patient can have an acceptable Pplat and ΔP and still have β > 1 (overdistension hidden inside an adequate-looking number) or β < 1 (cyclic atelectrauma despite the right pressures).

Three caveats that ruin the stress index(1) The mode must be VC with a square (constant-flow) waveform. Decelerating-ramp VC and PC waveforms are non-linear by design — the resulting curve shape doesn't carry the same meaning. (2) The patient must be passive — any spontaneous inspiratory effort adds a Pmus that distorts the trace and can fake either a β < 1 or β > 1 pattern. (3) The stress index reflects the global lung; in heterogeneous ARDS, regional β values vary, and a global β = 1 can hide pockets of both recruitment and overdistension.

6.5 Work of breathing — the metabolic cost of every breath

Breathing is muscular work. The diaphragm and accessory muscles do mechanical work against the elastic recoil of the lung-and-chest-wall system, the resistance of the conducting airways, and (when present) intrinsic PEEP. Healthy quiet breathing consumes 1–3% of total O₂ delivery. Severe respiratory distress can push that to 20–30% — at which point the muscles compete with the rest of the body for cardiac output and a vicious cycle begins.

Three sources of work, in order of dominance during disease:

  1. Elastic work — pushing volume against compliance (W = ½ × ΔV² / C). High in stiff lungs (ARDS) and high lung volumes (auto-PEEP, hyperinflation).
  2. Resistive work — pushing flow through resistance (W = R × V̇²). High in obstructive disease (asthma, COPD), small ETTs, and biting/secretions.
  3. Threshold work (auto-PEEP) — the patient must drop airway pressure all the way from the auto-PEEP value to below the trigger threshold before any inspiratory effort is registered. This is pure wasted work and is the most common reason a COPD patient on the vent looks tired despite "good" settings.
Why intubation reverses the cycleMechanical ventilation off-loads the respiratory pump. The vent does the elastic and resistive work; the patient's diaphragm rests; oxygen consumption by the breathing muscles falls; cardiac output redistributes to vital organs; lactate clears. This is also why intubation in shock (Type IV failure) can be life-saving even when oxygenation looks "fine" — the metabolic cost of breathing was killing the patient.

Iatrogenic mirror image — mechanical power. The work the patient was doing isn't gone; it's now done by the vent on the lung. Mechanical power (J/min) is the rate at which the ventilator does work on the lung's tissues, and above ~17 J/min it's independently associated with mortality (§30). Off-loading the patient's pump and not destroying their lung in the process is the central tension of every vent strategy.

Key takeaways
  • The equation of motion (Paw = V/C + V̇×R + PEEP) explains every bedside pressure: elastic + resistive + baseline.
  • Compliance (Cstat) and resistance (Raw) decompose PIP into lung and airway problems — high PIP with normal Pplat = resistance; both high = stiff lung.
  • Time constants (τ = R × C) dictate whether the lung empties in time; auto-PEEP occurs when expiratory time < 3τ.
  • Closing capacity vs FRC: when FRC drops below CC, tidal breathing happens inside collapsed airways — PEEP's job is to raise FRC above CC.

7The lung's pressure–volume curve

The lung's mechanical behavior reduces to a single graph — how volume changes with pressure. This chapter unpacks that graph: its anatomy (§7.1), the five pressures that drive each breath across the cycle (§7.2), what resistance adds during a real breath (§7.3), how dynamic hyperinflation deforms it (§7.4), the bedside corollaries (§7.5), and how the curve looks across the four ICU phenotypes that drive most vent decisions (§7.6).

7.1 The static P-V loop — anatomy of the curve
Figure 7.1 — The pressure–volume loop. The inflation (inspiratory) limb climbs from the origin through the lower inflection point — where recruitment is largely complete and the curve steepens into its linear, best-compliance segment — and then through the upper inflection point (the start of overdistension) into the flat "beaking" plateau at peak airway pressure (Paw). The deflation (expiratory) limb returns above the inflation limb because surfactant and viscoelastic forces hold alveoli open at lower pressures than were needed to open them; the shaded area between the limbs is hysteresis — energy dissipated per cycle. The pressure axis decomposes into four operational zones: PEEP, alveolar recruitment & airway resistance, optimal compliance (compliant lung), and alveolar overdistension; tidal breaths belong in the third zone (see §7.5 for the bedside corollaries).

7.2 Mechanics of breathing — five pressures across the cycle

Figure 7.1 collapses a breath into a single P-V trace, but the cycle is actually driven by five distinct pressures, each tracking lung volume differently. Reading the ventilator waveforms in Part III and interpreting esophageal manometry (§29) both start here.

Figure 7.2 — Mechanics of breathing: five pressures across the respiratory cycle. PA (alveolar) oscillates slightly above and below zero to drive airflow. Ppl (pleural) is always negative and becomes most negative at TLC. PL (transpulmonary, PA − Ppl) is the distending pressure of the lung and is greatest at TLC. PCW (chest-wall recoil) is negative below ~70% VC (chest wall pulls outward) and crosses zero near 70%. Paw (airway opening) is positive whenever airways are open. Clinical correlates: loss of negative Ppl (pneumothorax) drops PL and the lung collapses; increased Ppl (obesity, ascites) drops PL and shrinks operating volumes — the physiologic rationale for esophageal manometry (§29).

7.3 From static to dynamic — adding resistance and work

Figure 7.1 is the lung's quasi-static P-V loop — the trace you would record by inflating and then deflating the lung slowly enough that resistive flow effects are negligible. Even with no flow there are still two limbs, separated by the shaded hysteresis area; the lung holds more volume on the way down than on the way up.

During an actual breath, resistance to flow widens both limbs further. The dynamic inspiratory limb sits to the right of the quasi-static inflation curve (resistance adds extra pressure on the way in); the dynamic expiratory limb sits to the left of the quasi-static deflation curve (resistance opposes flow on the way out). The additional area enclosed beyond the quasi-static loop is the resistive work per breath.

Work decomposes into two terms. The diagonal from the loop's origin to its peak — the line the breath would take if the lung had only compliance and no resistance — is the elastic line. The wedge to the left of the dynamic inspiratory limb, bounded above by the tidal-volume horizontal, is the elastic work (energy spent stretching lung and chest wall). Total work per breath = elastic + resistive. The bigger the resistance, the wider the dynamic loop relative to the quasi-static one; the stiffer the lung, the steeper the elastic line and the larger the elastic wedge.

7.4 Dynamic hyperinflation — what auto-PEEP does to the loop

When the time constant exceeds the available expiratory time (severe COPD or asthma — see §17), the lung cannot fully empty before the next breath begins. The dynamic loop moves in two directions on the same axes:

  • Right-shift along the pressure axis. At end-expiration airway pressure is no longer atmospheric — it has been pushed up by the trapped volume to a value called intrinsic PEEP (auto-PEEP). The new starting point of the loop sits in from the y-axis by the magnitude of PEEPi.
  • Up-shift along the volume axis. Tidal breathing no longer starts from resting FRC but from a higher dynamic FRC — the lung is operating on a steeper, less-compliant region of the inflation limb.

Three penalties accumulate from those shifts. The elastic wedge enlarges because every breath now climbs a steeper part of the curve. Both resistive limbs widen because airway obstruction and dynamic small-airway compression are higher. And a new inspiratory threshold load appears — a rectangular column of pure pressure work to the left of the loop's origin that the inspiratory muscles must overcome before any flow begins (work that produces no ventilation).

The total work-of-breathing penalty often doubles or triples compared with the same patient's loop without trapping — the physiologic basis of the air hunger, ineffective triggering, fatigue, and weaning failure characteristic of severe obstructive disease.

7.5 Clinical corollaries
  • Set PEEP so tidal breaths sit on the linear-compliance portion of the curve — above LIP, below UIP. That's the zone where each delivered milliliter costs the least pressure.
  • Hysteresis is why recruitment maneuvers work. The shaded gap between the two limbs is energy lost per cycle to surface tension, viscoelasticity, and recruitment / derecruitment of marginal alveoli. Surfactant is the dominant determinant: it lowers surface tension at small radii, so an alveolus that has popped open can be held open at a lower distending pressure than was required to open it — hence the deflation limb sits to the upper-left of the inflation limb. Clinical consequence: a brief recruitment maneuver (sustained pressure that climbs the inflation limb past the UIP) shifts the operating point onto the deflation limb, where a lower PEEP holds the same volume open. This is the physiologic justification for the "open the lung, then keep it open" approach — recruitment maneuver followed by a decremental PEEP titration finds the minimum PEEP that prevents end-expiratory collapse, which is reliably below the PEEP required to open the lung in the first place.
  • The total respiratory-system P-V curve includes the chest wall. In obesity, ascites, and ARDS with abdominal hypertension the chest-wall component dominates and PEEP requirements rise; airway plateau pressure can overestimate true alveolar stretch — use esophageal manometry to read transpulmonary pressure (§29).
  • For the bedside deep-dive on this curve — the three regions of the inflation limb, the four-mechanism breakdown of hysteresis, why "PEEP = LIP + 2" fails as an algorithm, and disease-specific curve patterns — see §7.7.

7.6 Comparative disease physiology — one table to navigate the four big phenotypes

Almost every ICU patient on a ventilator falls into (or between) four physiologic phenotypes: ARDS, COPD, severe asthma, and cardiogenic pulmonary edema. They share the same vocabulary — compliance, resistance, V/Q, FRC, time constant — but the direction each axis moves is different, and every ventilator decision in Part III follows from these moves. Memorize the column shape, not the rows.

The four phenotypes side-by-side. Arrows show direction of change vs. normal; † = primary lesion driving the rest.
Axis ARDS COPD (emphysema) Severe asthma Cardiogenic edema
Static compliance (Cstat) ↓↓ (stiff lung)† ↑ (loss of recoil)† ↔ to ↓ (hyperinflated, near TLC) ↓ (wet, heavy lung)
Airway resistance (Raw) ↔ to mildly ↑ ↑↑ (expiratory collapse) ↑↑↑ (bronchospasm + mucus)† ↑ (edema in airway walls; "cardiac asthma")
FRC ↓↓ (collapsed/consolidated units) ↑↑ (dynamic hyperinflation, auto-PEEP) ↑↑ (dynamic hyperinflation, auto-PEEP) ↓ (alveolar flooding)
V/Q pattern Shunt dominates (consolidated alveoli) Dead space dominates (destroyed capillary bed) Mixed — mostly low-V/Q from mucus plugging Shunt + low-V/Q (flooded alveoli, vascular congestion)
Recruitable lung Yes (variable; high in early, low in late/fibrotic) No — alveoli are destroyed, not closed Minimal — the obstruction is in the airway, not the alveolus Yes — diuresis + PEEP recruits flooded units
Time constant (τ = R × C) Short (low C dominates) Long (high R + high C compound) Long (high R; C near normal) Short to mid
Dominant mechanism Loss of aerated lung volume → "baby lung" → pressure injury risk Expiratory flow limitation → incomplete emptying → auto-PEEP Acute airway narrowing → expiratory flow limitation → auto-PEEP Hydrostatic/permeability flooding → shunt + reduced compliance
Vent strategy in one line Low VT (4–6 mL/kg PBW), Pplat ≤ 30, PEEP titrated to keep open (§20) Low RR, long TE, accept hypercapnia, watch for auto-PEEP (§17) As COPD plus aggressive bronchodilation; permissive hypercapnia (§17) PEEP unloads the LV preload & afterload; diurese; oxygenate (§29.4)
What kills the patient VILI (volutrauma + atelectrauma + biotrauma) Dynamic hyperinflation → hemodynamic collapse on intubation Same as COPD — induction-related arrest from auto-PEEP Untreated cause (ischemia, AS, MR); secondary VILI if mismanaged
The single most useful column"Recruitable lung." It is the question that decides whether PEEP helps or harms. ARDS and edema are recruitable; COPD and asthma are not. Putting more PEEP into a non-recruitable patient just pushes the operating point up the chest-wall curve — more dead space, more hemodynamic compromise, no oxygenation benefit.
Mixed phenotypes are the rule, not the exceptionThe septic COPDer with pneumonia has both auto-PEEP physiology and recruitable shunt; the ARDS patient with volume overload has both stiffness and edema. The table is a starting point — every patient is graded on each axis, and the strategy follows the dominant mechanism at the moment.

7.7 The static P-V curve in detail — anatomy, hysteresis, inflection points

Building on the static curve anatomy above (Figure 7.1 — LIP, UIP, beaking, and the four pressure zones), this section adds the bedside-deep detail: the three regions of the inflation curve, four hysteresis mechanisms (§7.7.1), why setting PEEP from the LIP fails in practice (§7.7.2), and disease-specific curve patterns (§7.7.3).

The three regions of the static inflation curve
RegionShapeWhat's happening in the lung
Lower flat regionShallow slope: large pressure for little volumeMost alveoli still collapsed; pressure is opening surfactant-deficient lung units one critical-opening pressure at a time
Linear (compliant) regionSteep, near-linear slopeThe bulk of recruitable lung is open; further pressure expands already-open alveoli at uniform compliance
Upper flat region ("the beak")Shallow slope: large pressure for little volumeMost alveoli are at their elastic limit; further pressure produces overdistension, not recruitment
7.7.1 Hysteresis — the four energy-loss mechanisms

Hysteresis is the difference between the inspiratory and expiratory limbs at any given pressure. Lung volume on the way down is always greater than on the way up — energy was deposited during inflation that is not returned during deflation. The four contributors:37

The four mechanisms of pulmonary hysteresis
MechanismWhat it isWhere it dominates
Recruitment / derecruitmentAlveoli "pop" open at one critical pressure and "pop" closed at a lower one — opening pressure exceeds closing pressureAtelectatic / ARDS lung; surfactant deficiency; deep-volume cycling
SurfactantSurfactant lowers surface tension at low alveolar volume more than at high — the lung is "stiffer" at small volumes than at large onesHealthy lung; dramatically exaggerated in surfactant-deficient lung (neonates, ARDS)
Stress relaxation (viscoelasticity)Lung tissue is viscoelastic — strain develops gradually with applied stress and relaxes gradually when stress is removedEvery lung; visible as the slow drift in airway pressure during the inspiratory hold
Gas absorptionCO₂ and O₂ continuously cross the alveolar–capillary membrane; volume in the lung is not conserved during a slow inflationSignificant only in slow maneuvers (≥ 3 s); negligible at normal breath rates

Hysteresis area increases with tidal volume amplitude, with surfactant deficiency, and with the number of recruitable alveoli. A patient whose loop area is shrinking over days is losing recruitable lung — the curve is becoming more uniformly stiff (no more popping open at high pressures).

7.7.2 Setting PEEP from the static curve — the LIP problem

The "PEEP = LIP + 2 cmH₂O" strategy is a useful concept and a poor algorithm:

  • Theoretical foundation: keeping alveolar pressure above critical opening pressure prevents cyclic recruitment / derecruitment, the most lung-injurious form of cycling.
  • Practical reliability is poor: Harris (2005) found that experienced clinicians given the same inflation curve placed the LIP 11 cmH₂O apart from one another. The curve is heterogeneous; the inflection is a transition zone, not a point.
  • ARDS heterogeneity smears the inflection: in real ARDS, alveoli with very different opening pressures are recruited at very different transmural pressures across the curve — the classical S-shape is smoothed into a near-linear transition with no clear "knee." Some collapsed alveoli are still being recruited at 35 cmH₂O while non-dependent alveoli are already overdistended at 25 cmH₂O.38
  • Better strategies have replaced LIP-based titration:
    • Esophageal manometry — set transpulmonary PEEP to keep end-expiratory PL ≥ 0 (§29).
    • Decremental PEEP trial — start high, step down, find best compliance.
    • R/I ratio — quantitative single-breath estimate of lung recruitability before committing PEEP.
    • ARDSnet PEEP/FiO₂ ladder — empirical, reliable, the safest default for routine cases.
7.7.3 Disease-specific patterns on the static curve
How the static curve looks in different lungs
DiseaseLIPUIP / beakCompliance (slope)Hysteresis area
Healthy lungBarely visible (most alveoli already open at FRC)Visible only at very high VTSteep, normalModest
ARDS — focal (lobar, dependent collapse)Prominent — recruitable atelectasis lifts off at ~ 12–15 cmH₂OOften visible at 25–30 cmH₂O — non-dependent regions overdistend before dependent regions are fully recruitedReduced; the linear region is shortLarge (recruitment + surfactant)
ARDS — diffuseSmoothed / indistinctSmoothed / indistinctReduced and uniformly soSmaller than focal — less to recruit
Pulmonary fibrosisNot present (no recruitable lung)Present at low absolute volumesSeverely reduced (steep right-shift)Small (no recruitment)
COPD with hyperinflationPseudo-LIP at PEEPi (the curve "starts" above zero on the x-axis)Often invisible — small VT stays in the linear region of an over-inflated lungInitially flat (already at high volume), can be near-normal in mid-curveVariable; resistive contribution dominates the apparent loop area
Severe asthmaVariable — small-airway closure mimics atelectasisOften visibleVariableVery large (resistive component dominates the apparent dynamic-loop area)
Why ARDS curves disappointThe classical "set PEEP at LIP" approach was developed in oleic-acid animal models with relatively uniform injury. Real ARDS is heterogeneous: gravitationally-dependent alveoli sit collapsed beneath waterlogged tissue, while non-dependent alveoli are normally aerated and easily overdistended. There is no single "ideal" PEEP — only a least-bad compromise. The curve smooths because the population of alveoli is recruiting and overdistending simultaneously across the same pressure range. Treat the LIP as orienting information, not a setpoint.
The dynamic loop trend beats the static loop snapshotFor routine bedside use the dynamic loop is more useful than the static curve because you get one every breath. Track its slope (compliance), its area (work), and its rightward drift (worsening compliance) day-by-day. Reach for the static curve only when a single-occasion PEEP titration matters — e.g., severe ARDS at admission, post-recruitment maneuver assessment, or a refractory hypoxemia case where the easy ladders have failed. See §15 for the dynamic-loop atlas at the bedside.
Key takeaways
  • The P-V curve has a lower inflection point (LIP) where recruitment begins and an upper inflection point (UIP) where overdistension starts — tidal breaths belong between them.
  • Transpulmonary pressure (Paw − Ppl), not airway plateau pressure alone, is what stretches the lung — high Pplat from chest-wall stiffness (obesity, ascites, abdominal hypertension) doesn't mean lung is injured. Use esophageal manometry to read true transpulmonary pressure (§29).
  • Hysteresis is why a recruitment maneuver + decremental PEEP keeps lung open at lower PEEP than was needed to open it. Four mechanisms contribute (recruitment / surfactant / viscoelasticity / gas absorption) — see §7.7.
  • "PEEP = LIP + 2" is a useful concept and a poor algorithm — modern PEEP titration uses esophageal manometry, decremental trials, R/I ratio, or the ARDSnet ladder (§7.7.2).
  • PEEP is only one part of the system; the elastic recoil of both lung and chest wall determines the operating point.
  • Four phenotypes (ARDS, COPD, asthma, cardiogenic edema) explain >90% of ICU vent decisions; "recruitable lung?" is the single most decision-relevant question.
Part II

Mechanics & the ventilator

Equipment fluency: lung mechanics, the P-V curve, how a breath is constructed, the conventional and modern modes, and a defensible day-1 setup that keeps you out of trouble.

8Anatomy of a ventilator breath

Every breath the ventilator delivers — in any mode — is composed of the same four phases: trigger (when the breath starts), inspiration (how gas is delivered), cycle (when inspiration ends), and expiration (passive recoil to baseline). Master this anatomy and every mode in the next four chapters becomes a recombination of the same four parameters.

8.1 The four phases at a glance
The phase, the parameter, the question
PhaseParameterQuestion it answers
TriggerTime, pressure, or flowWhat starts the breath?
InspirationVolume, pressure, or hybrid targetWhat variable does the vent control during the breath?
CycleTime, volume, or flow terminationWhat ends inspiration?
Expiration(Passive — set by lung mechanics + PEEP)What happens between breaths?

Mode names are shorthand for a particular combination of those four answers. Volume control with assist-control: pressure-or-time triggered → volume-targeted → time-cycled → passive expiration. Pressure support: flow-triggered → pressure-targeted → flow-cycled → passive expiration. Recognize the phases and the modes stop being a memorization problem.

8.2 Triggering — how a breath begins

Three trigger types cover every modern vent:

The three trigger mechanisms
TriggerHow it worksPros / cons
TimeVent delivers a breath when the set RR clock elapses, regardless of patient effortReliable in apneic / paralyzed patients. Fights spontaneous breathing — produces dyssynchrony if the patient is awake.
PressureVent senses a drop in airway pressure (e.g., −2 cmH₂O below PEEP) generated by the patient's inspiratory effortSimple, classic. Higher work of breathing than flow-triggering — the patient must overcome a pressure threshold before any flow is delivered.
FlowVent senses a small inspiratory flow drawn from a continuous bias flow loop in the circuitLowest patient effort to trigger. The default on modern vents. Susceptible to auto-triggering from circuit condensation, leaks, cardiac oscillations.

The trigger threshold (sensitivity) is the single most under-set parameter. Too high → ineffective triggering, missed efforts (visible as effort-dips on the pressure trace not followed by a vent breath). Too low → auto-triggering from circuit noise (visible as faster-than-set RR, breaths delivered when the patient is paralyzed or disconnected). Set flow trigger at 1–3 L/min as a starting default; titrate at the bedside (§18).

8.3 Inspiration — the target variable

Whatever variable the operator sets as the target during inspiration is by definition fixed; the others become dependent on the patient's mechanics:

  • Volume control (VC) — operator sets VT and inspiratory flow; pressure is dependent. The pressure rises along the lung's loading curve until VT is delivered. Diagnostic: pressure shape is a window onto compliance and resistance.
  • Pressure control (PC) — operator sets a target pressure (above PEEP) and an inspiratory time; volume is dependent. Flow decelerates exponentially as the lung fills. Diagnostic: VT changes breath-to-breath are a real-time compliance signal.
  • Pressure support (PSV) — like PC but flow-cycled and patient-triggered. Operator sets the support level; the patient drives every other parameter.
  • Hybrid (PRVC, VC+, AutoFlow) — adaptive modes that target a volume but deliver it at the lowest pressure possible. The vent re-tunes the pressure target every breath. (See §12.1.)
The "set vs dependent" ruleIf you only remember one thing about modes: in any mode, exactly one of {volume, pressure} is set, and the other is dependent on lung mechanics. Reading the screen tells you which is which: the set variable is square or constant; the dependent variable changes shape with the lung. This decomposition is the basis of every troubleshooting algorithm in §18.

8.4 Cycling — when inspiration ends

Cycling determines Ti — and getting it wrong is one of the commonest sources of asynchrony.

The three cycling mechanisms
CycleUsed byFailure mode
Volume-cycledPure VC (legacy)If VT reached during a leak, you under-deliver and the patient receives less than set
Time-cycledVC (modern, with set Ti) and PCTi set too long → late-cycle dyssynchrony (patient wants to exhale, vent is still inflating). Ti too short → premature cycling, double-trigger.
Flow-cycledPSV; cycle threshold = % of peak inspiratory flowThreshold too low (e.g., 10%) → vent waits longer for flow to fall, so longer Ti → late cycling in COPD. Too high (e.g., 40%) → vent ends inspiration earlier, so shorter Ti → premature cycling in stiff lung. Default 25% works for most. (Inverse intuition: lower % = longer Ti; higher % = shorter Ti.)

8.5 Expiration — passive but not silent

Expiration is set by lung mechanics, not by the ventilator: the elastic recoil of lung + chest wall pushes air out through the resistance of airway + ETT. The shape is exponential decay with time constant τ = R × C (§6.4).

What the operator does control:

  • PEEP — the end-expiratory pressure floor.
  • Te — indirectly via RR and Ti: Te = (60/RR) − Ti. If Te < 3τ, the lung does not finish exhaling → auto-PEEP and dynamic hyperinflation (§17).
  • Expiratory limb resistance — fixed by the circuit and exhalation valve, but partially manageable (heated humidifier vs HME, larger ETT).

8.6 The breath as one equation — bringing it all together

Every breath obeys the equation of motion:

$$P_\text{vent} + P_\text{mus} \;=\; \frac{V}{C} \;+\; \dot{V} \cdot R \;+\; \text{PEEP}$$

Translation: the total pressure available (the vent's contribution + the patient's effort) does three jobs each breath — overcome elastic recoil (V/C), push gas through resistance (V̇·R), and maintain the end-expiratory pressure floor (PEEP). Every mode is a way of distributing pressure across these three terms:

  • VC: vent delivers a fixed V (so V/C is fixed), at a chosen V̇ (so V̇·R is set). Total pressure rises to whatever it has to.
  • PC: vent delivers a fixed total pressure; V/C and V̇·R adjust to fit, and V (the dependent variable) ends up wherever mechanics put it.
  • PSV: vent and patient share the pressure work; the patient's Pmus contribution makes the breath possible at lower vent pressure than a passive PC breath would need.
Figure 8.1 — Anatomy of a ventilator breath. Every mode in the next four chapters is a different way of answering the same four questions: trigger (what starts it), inspiration (what variable is targeted), cycle (what ends it), and expiration (passive, set by mechanics). The pressure trace here is a single time-cycled, volume-targeted (VC) breath — but the four-phase skeleton applies to every mode.
Reading any mode in 5 secondsWalk up to a vent screen and ask the four questions in order: What's triggering? (set RR vs spontaneous breaths between mandatory) → What's set vs dependent? (look at pressure shape — square = PC; ramp = VC; patient-shaped = PSV) → What's cycling? (look at when inspiration ends — fixed Ti = time-cycled; flow tail-off = flow-cycled) → What's the τ? (does expiratory flow return to zero before the next breath?). That four-question pass diagnoses the mode and screens for asynchrony.
Key takeaways
  • Every breath has four phases: trigger, inspiration, cycle, expiration. Every mode is a recombination of choices for each.
  • Three triggers (time, pressure, flow); flow is the modern default, lowest patient work, but susceptible to auto-triggering from condensation, leaks, cardiac oscillations.
  • Inspiration: exactly one of {volume, pressure} is the set target; the other is dependent on lung mechanics.
  • Three cycle mechanisms (volume, time, flow); time-cycle Ti too short → double-trigger, too long → late-cycle dyssynchrony; flow-cycle threshold default 25% of peak.
  • Expiration is passive — operator controls PEEP and (indirectly) Te; Te < 3τ → auto-PEEP.
  • The equation of motion (Pvent + Pmus = V/C + V̇·R + PEEP) makes mode choice arithmetic: which side does the operator clamp? VC fixes the right side (V is set; pressure is whatever it has to be). PC fixes the vent's contribution to the left side (Pvent is set; volume is dependent). PSV fixes the vent's support level on the left side and lets Pmus share the work — which is why the same setting feels different breath-to-breath as the patient's effort changes.

9Volume control (VC, assist-control VC [A/C-VC])

Volume control is the workhorse mode of every ICU on day one. The operator sets VT, RR, and inspiratory flow; the ventilator guarantees the volume regardless of lung mechanics. The price of that guarantee is a pressure waveform that becomes your real-time mechanics monitor.

9.1 What VC sets and what's dependent
VC mode at a glance
Set by operatorDependent on lung
Volume (VT)✓
Flow rate & pattern✓
Inspiratory time (Ti)derived from VT/flow (or set directly)
RR (mandatory rate)✓
PEEP, FiO₂✓
Airway pressure (PIP, Pplat)✓ — rises along the lung's loading curve

The terms VC and A/C-VC (assist-control volume control) describe the same set/dependent relationship — the difference is that A/C-VC delivers a full set VT on every patient-triggered breath, not just on the time-triggered (mandatory) ones. Most modern ICU vents default to A/C-VC.

9.2 Setting the parameters — what numbers and why
VC starting parameters for the average adult
SettingDefaultRationale
VT6 mL/kg PBWLung-protective. PBW from height; see §13.1.
RR14–18Match expected V̇E for normal metabolism; titrate to PaCO₂.
Peak flow50–60 L/min, ramp patternHigher = shorter Ti = longer Te. Ramp matches patient demand better than square in most situations.
Flow patternDecelerating (ramp)More physiologic; lower peak pressures than square; gentler at end-inspiration.
I:E1:2 to 1:3 default; 1:4–1:5 in obstructive diseaseAllows full exhalation; longer in obstructive lung disease to avoid auto-PEEP.
PEEP5 cmH₂O default; 8–18 in ARDSPer ARDSnet ladder for FiO₂ > 0.4; see §28.
FiO₂1.0 initial, wean rapidly to lowest for SpO₂ 92–96%Avoid hyperoxia toxicity once oxygenation is established.
TriggerFlow trigger 1–3 L/minLowest WOB; readjust if auto-triggering or missed efforts.

9.3 Reading the screen in VC — what each waveform tells you

The diagnostic power of VC is that the pressure trace is the dependent variable — it draws the lung's loading curve in real time:

  • Linear pressure ramp during constant-flow inspiration: optimal compliance, no recruitment, no overdistension. β = 1 stress index. Maintain settings.
  • Concave-up ramp (β < 1): continued recruitment with each volume increment — consider raising PEEP. (See §15.5.)
  • Convex-up ramp (β > 1): overdistension — reduce VT first, then PEEP if Pplat still high.
  • "Scooped" / concave-down inspiratory limb: the patient is generating more flow demand than the set rate — flow starvation. Switch to ramp pattern, increase peak flow, or consider PSV. (See §18.4.)
  • Notch or downward dip mid-inspiration: an effort against the passive breath. Check sedation level and trigger sensitivity.

9.4 When VC is the right choice
  • ARDS — VT guarantee is critical for lung-protective ventilation. The whole ARDSnet protocol is built on VC with VT 4–8 mL/kg PBW.
  • Apneic / paralyzed patients — predictable minute ventilation regardless of effort.
  • Obstructive disease (COPD, asthma) — VT guarantee plus low RR and long Te; pressures are diagnostic of bronchospasm vs gas trapping.
  • Acute pH derangement requiring tight V̇E control — DKA, cerebral injury with target PaCO₂.

9.5 Common pitfalls and how to avoid them
The high-pressure alarm in VCVC's volume guarantee means that a stiff lung or high resistance generates whatever PIP it takes — and trips the alarm. Reflexive instinct: drop VT. Better: read the trace. If PIP is high but Pplat is not, the problem is resistance (bronchospasm, secretions, ETT — fixable). If Pplat is high (≥ 30), the problem is compliance — drop VT by 1 mL/kg or accept a lower SpO₂ target. Don't reflexively switch to PC just to silence the alarm; you trade volume guarantee for variable VT, often worse.
Flow starvation in awake patientsThe square-wave default flow setting (40–60 L/min) is fine in paralyzed patients but is often too low for an awake, distressed patient who wants 80–120 L/min. The pressure trace shows it as a scooped inspiratory limb. Switch to descending ramp and increase peak flow before reaching for sedation.

9.6 VC variants — A/C, SIMV, and adaptive
The VC family
ModeWhat's differentUse case
VC (legacy)Mandatory time-cycled breaths only — no patient triggeringHistorical; rare on modern vents
A/C-VCEvery patient effort triggers a full VT breath; RR is the minimum rateDefault modern setting; works for nearly every patient
SIMV-VCPatient-triggered breaths between mandatory breaths get pressure support, not a full VTMostly historical; outperformed by PSV-based weaning (§11.2)
PRVC / VC+ / AutoFlowAdaptive — targets VT at the lowest pressure possible, breath-by-breathMid-recovery patients with variable mechanics — but be cautious in awake patients with strong drive: the algorithm can drop support inappropriately when the patient pulls more volume on their own. See §12.1 and §12.5.
Key takeaways
  • VC sets VT, flow, RR, PEEP; pressure rises along the lung's loading curve and is your free real-time mechanics monitor.
  • Default starting set: VT 6 mL/kg PBW, RR 14–18, peak flow 50–60 with ramp, I:E 1:2–1:3, PEEP 5 (or per ARDSnet ladder), FiO₂ 1.0 to wean.
  • Linear pressure ramp = optimal; concave-up = recruitment opportunity; convex-up = overdistension; scooped = flow starvation; notch = patient effort.
  • VC is the right mode for ARDS, apneic patients, obstructive disease, and any pH-tight situation. Don't switch to PC just to silence a high-pressure alarm — read Pplat first.
  • A/C-VC is the default; SIMV-VC is largely historical; PRVC / VC+ is the adaptive cousin (§12.1).

10Pressure control (PC, assist-control PC [A/C-PC])

Pressure control is the structural mirror of VC: the operator clamps the pressure plateau (above PEEP) and the inspiratory time; volume becomes dependent on lung mechanics. The advantage is a pressure ceiling against barotrauma; the price is VT that varies breath-to-breath when compliance changes.

Read this chapter as "differences from VC"The four-phase anatomy (trigger, inspiration, cycle, expiration) is the same as Ch 9. The default RR, PEEP, FiO₂, and alarm philosophy are the same as Ch 9. What changes: the operator sets pressure instead of volume, so VT becomes the dependent variable that drifts day-to-day as compliance changes; flow is no longer set but decelerates exponentially as the alveolar gradient closes; and the bedside diagnostic skill shifts from reading the pressure trace to reading the VT trend. Skim the parallels; pay close attention to §10.3 (VT drift as the early-warning signal) and §10.5 (the silent hypoventilation pitfall).

10.1 What PC sets and what's dependent
PC mode at a glance
Set by operatorDependent on lung
Pset (target above PEEP)✓
Ti (inspiratory time)✓
RR (mandatory rate)✓
Rise time / pressure ramp✓ (typically 0.1–0.2 s)
PEEP, FiO₂✓
VT✓ — varies with compliance, resistance, and patient effort
Flow waveform✓ — decelerating ramp from peak to ≈ 0 as the alveolar gradient closes

10.2 Setting the parameters
PC starting parameters for the average adult
SettingDefaultRationale
Pset above PEEPWhatever achieves VT 6 mL/kg PBW — typically 12–15 cmH₂O on initial mechanicsThe target is VT, not a particular pressure. Start with Pset 12–15 above PEEP, observe the delivered VT, then adjust. Stiffer lung needs higher Pset for the same VT; if Pset > 18 is required to reach 6 mL/kg, you're already at high ΔP — consider whether the lung can accept that VT at all.
Ti0.8–1.2 s (adult)Long enough to allow flow to decay near zero (full equilibration); shorter Ti = under-delivery.
Rise time0.10–0.20 sFaster rise = higher peak flow, more turbulent, but reaches plateau faster. Slower rise = smoother but may under-fill.
RR14–18Same as VC default.
PEEP, FiO₂Per ARDSnet ladderSame approach as VC.

10.3 Reading the screen in PC

The signature of a PC breath is a square pressure plateau and a decelerating flow ramp. Watch the volume trace — it tells you the patient's mechanics in real time:

  • Stable square pressure, VT stable breath-to-breath: settings are appropriate; mechanics steady.
  • VT drifting down: compliance is worsening (atelectasis, edema accumulating, pneumothorax) — investigate before VT becomes unsafe.
  • VT drifting up: compliance is improving — recovery, lung opening up, drying out. Consider switching to PSV / starting weaning.
  • Flow doesn't return to near-zero by end of Ti: Ti is too short for the lung's time constant. Lengthen it or increase Pset.
  • Pressure overshoots Pset at start: rise time too fast — slow it down.
  • Patient effort visible as a dip in the pressure plateau: cycle dyssynchrony — the patient wants out before the time-cycle ends. Shorten Ti or switch to PSV.

10.4 When PC is the right choice
  • Bronchopleural fistula or large airway leak — pressure ceiling avoids endless flow into the leak.
  • Severe airway resistance with high PIP in VC — PC limits the peak pressure (resistive component is no longer "free" to climb).
  • Pediatric / neonatal ventilation — pressure-targeted modes are the historic standard.
  • Awake, spontaneously breathing patients with intact drive — PC's decelerating flow matches inspiratory demand better than VC's square or ramp.
  • Recovery from heavy sedation — when the patient is starting to breathe and the goal is to allow more variability without inducing dyssynchrony.

10.5 Common pitfalls and how to avoid them
Variable VT in PC during early ARDSThe decline in compliance during evolving ARDS is silent in PC: the screen looks fine, but VT is creeping down 50–100 mL each shift. The patient hypoventilates without an alarm. Set low VT and low V̇E alarms in PC; they're often forgotten. Or use PRVC / VC+ for volume guarantee with PC's smoother flow profile.
Wrong Ti in obstructive diseaseIn COPD and asthma, the lung's expiratory time constant is long (τ > 1.5 s); a long PC Ti shrinks Te below 3τ → auto-PEEP. Set Ti short (0.6–0.8 s) and accept lower VT in obstruction; rely on a slow RR to compensate.

10.6 PC vs A/C-PC vs SIMV-PC vs PSV — knowing the family
The PC family
ModeWhat's differentUse case
PC (legacy)Mandatory only — no patient triggeringHistorical; not on modern vents
A/C-PCEvery patient effort triggers a full Pset breath; RR is the minimum rateDefault PC mode on modern vents
SIMV-PCMandatory PC breaths + patient-triggered PSV breaths betweenLargely historical; PSV alone usually wins for weaning
PSVPressure-targeted but flow-cycled; no time cycle; patient sets the rateThe weaning mode. See §11.1.
Why every breath looks different from the lastIf VT changes 10–20% breath-to-breath in PC, that's not a bug — it's the mode telling you mechanics. Sit with the trace for a minute. Decreasing VT on a stable patient is the single best early-warning sign of evolving lung pathology, often before P/F changes.
Key takeaways
  • PC sets target pressure (Pset) and Ti; VT is dependent — varies with compliance, resistance, and patient effort.
  • Defaults: Pset 12–15 above PEEP, Ti 0.8–1.2 s, rise time 0.1–0.2 s. Titrate Pset to VT 6 mL/kg PBW.
  • Drifting VT is the diagnostic — down = worsening compliance, up = improving. Set low-VT alarms; they're easy to forget.
  • Flow not returning to near-zero by end of Ti = Ti too short for the lung's τ. In COPD, deliberately keep Ti short to preserve Te.
  • Best for bronchopleural fistula, severe resistance, awake patients with strong drive, recovery from sedation.
  • A/C-PC is the modern default; PRVC (§12.1) gives PC's smooth flow with VC's volume guarantee.

11Pressure support (PSV) and SIMV

PSV and SIMV are the modes you most often use to liberate a patient from the vent. PSV — a flow-cycled, pressure-targeted, patient-triggered mode — gives the patient back the rate, the rhythm, and most of the work, while still amplifying their effort. SIMV is its older cousin, mostly historical at this point but worth knowing how to read.

11.1 Pressure support (PSV)

In PSV, every breath is patient-triggered, pressure-targeted, and flow-cycled. The operator sets only three things: the support level (PS, in cmH₂O above PEEP), the cycle threshold (% of peak inspiratory flow), and PEEP. RR, Ti, and VT are all the patient's.

11.1.1 Setting the support level
PS level — what each setting does
PS levelWhat it meansUse case
PS 5Just enough to overcome ETT resistance (~5 cmH₂O for an 8.0 ETT)Minimal support — used during SBT to test for liberation readiness
PS 8–12Comfortable spontaneous breathing on the ventilatorStable patient who is breathing on their own but not yet ready to extubate
PS 15–20Substantial support; vent doing most of the workRecovering patient, transitioning out of A/C; or tachypnea / dyspnea on lower support
PS > 20Approaching A/C-PC equivalenceThe patient probably needs a controlled mode, not PSV

Adjust PS to achieve a comfortable RR (12–24) and VT (≥ 5 mL/kg PBW) without accessory muscle use. RR > 30 or VT < 4 mL/kg → patient is over-working, raise PS or escalate to A/C.

11.1.2 The cycle threshold — the most under-tuned knob

The cycle threshold is the inspiratory flow (as a percentage of peak) at which the vent terminates the breath. Default is usually 25% of peak. The right value depends on the lung's inspiratory time constant — and the intuition is the opposite of what most learners guess at first (see §18.6a for the full tuning table):

  • Short time constant (stiff lung — ARDS, fibrosis): flow decays fast → at the default 25% the breath cycles early, before the patient is done inhaling. Lower the threshold to 10–15% so flow has to fall further before cycling — extends Ti to match the patient's neural Ti.
  • Long time constant (COPD, asthma): flow decays slowly → at the default 25% the breath cycles late, after the patient already wants to exhale. Raise the threshold to 35–45% so cycling happens sooner — shortens Ti to match neural Ti.
  • Signs you have it wrong: pressure rises above Pset at end-inspiration (delayed cycling — vent is still pushing while the patient pushes out) or double-triggering (premature cycling — patient continues to inhale and triggers a stacked breath).
11.1.3 When PSV is the right mode
  • Spontaneous breathing trial — PSV 5–8 over PEEP 5 is the most common variant; PSV 0 + PEEP 0 (T-piece) is the most rigorous; both are acceptable. See §36.
  • Bridging from A/C to extubation when sedation is lightening and drive is intact.
  • Post-op recovery with intact respiratory drive.
  • Difficulty weaning from PRVC / A/C — PSV often outperforms when the issue is asynchrony.
PSV in central drive failurePSV requires a working respiratory drive. A patient with profound sedation, brainstem injury, or post-cardiac-arrest depressed drive will not trigger reliably and will drop minute ventilation precipitously. Set a backup rate (most modern vents allow PSV with apnea ventilation backup) or use A/C until drive returns.

11.2 SIMV (synchronized intermittent mandatory ventilation)

In SIMV, the ventilator delivers a set number of mandatory (volume- or pressure-targeted) breaths per minute. Between mandatory breaths, the patient can breathe spontaneously, with those efforts amplified by pressure support. The "synchronized" part means the mandatory breaths are timed to coincide with patient efforts when possible.

11.2.1 Why SIMV exists

SIMV was introduced as a weaning mode in the 1970s on the theory that gradually reducing the mandatory rate would re-train the patient's respiratory muscles. The mandatory breaths give a baseline V̇E; the patient's spontaneous breaths between contribute the rest. In principle, the operator titrates down the mandatory rate as the patient takes over.

11.2.2 Why SIMV has been outperformed

Three large RCTs in the 1990s (Brochard 1994, Esteban 1995, Ely 1996) found that SIMV-based weaning prolonged ventilator days compared with PSV-based weaning or daily SBTs. The mechanism: at low SIMV rates, the patient does enormous work on the spontaneous breaths but gets full unloading on the mandatory ones — a confusing pattern of effort that the patient adapts to poorly. PSV provides a more uniform pattern that's easier to wean from.3

11.2.3 Where SIMV still earns its place
  • Pediatric and neonatal ventilation — historical preference, still common.
  • Specific patient phenotypes that fail PSV (rare) — sometimes the predictability of mandatory rate helps.
  • Institutional habit — many ICUs use SIMV-PC because attendings are familiar with it. Outcomes are not better, but not catastrophically worse either when the rest of management is sound.

11.3 PSV vs SIMV vs A/C — choosing for liberation
Mode-choice for the liberation phase
Patient stateBest choiceWhy
Sedated, paralyzed, no driveA/C-VC or A/C-PCVent does all the work; predictable
Light sedation, intact drive, stable mechanicsPSV 8–12Patient sets rate; vent unloads
Daily readiness checkPSV 5–8 / PEEP 5 (most common) or T-piece (most rigorous)Tests liberation readiness; both variants acceptable; see §36
Mid-recovery, drive variablePRVC or PSV with backupAdaptive support during fluctuating effort
Failed PSV with asynchronyBack to A/C (VC or PC)Asynchrony in PSV usually reflects mismatch between patient drive and the cycle/trigger settings — return to a controlled mode to reset rather than chasing a partial solution.
The under-titrated knobThe cycle threshold is the most-overlooked parameter on a modern vent. Default 25% works for most, but in COPD/asthma raise it to 35–45% to terminate the breath before the patient pushes out against it. In stiff lung (ARDS/fibrosis) lower it to 10–15% to extend Ti so the breath isn't cut short before the patient has finished inhaling. Sign you have it wrong: pressure rises above Pset at end-inspiration (cycling too late) or double-triggering (cycling too early). See §18.6a for the full tuning table.
Key takeaways
  • PSV is the workhorse liberation mode — patient-triggered, pressure-targeted, flow-cycled. Three knobs: PS level, cycle threshold, PEEP.
  • PS 5 = SBT-level (just overcomes ETT); PS 8–12 = comfort spontaneous; PS 15–20 = substantial support; PS > 20 → consider A/C instead.
  • Cycle threshold is the most under-titrated knob — raise it (35–45%) in COPD/asthma so the breath ends before the patient pushes out; lower it (10–15%) in stiff lung so the breath isn't cut short. Default 25% works for most. Full tuning table in §18.6a.
  • PSV requires intact respiratory drive — set apnea backup. Sedation, brainstem injury, post-arrest drive depression are reasons to stay in A/C.
  • SIMV underperformed PSV in the 1990s weaning trials; it persists out of habit, especially in pediatric ICU. Outcomes are not dramatically worse with sound overall management, but PSV is the more efficient weaning path.
  • For SBT, use PSV 5–8 over PEEP 5 (most common variant) or PSV 0 + PEEP 0 (T-piece, most rigorous); both are acceptable. Default test for liberation readiness (§36).

12Adaptive modes & APRV

12.1 Pressure-regulated volume control (PRVC) / VC+ / Autoflow

Closed-loop hybrids: pressure-controlled breaths whose inspiratory pressure is adjusted breath-to-breath to deliver a set target tidal volume. Combines the comfort of PC with a VT guarantee. Caveat: in a strongly breathing patient, the algorithm can drop the support inappropriately, prolonging weaning.

12.2 Airway pressure release ventilation (APRV)

A high continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) at Phigh, interrupted by brief, periodic releases to Plow. Spontaneous breathing is encouraged throughout. Settings:

  • Phigh: 25–30 cmH₂O (titrate to Pplat < 30 if measured during a hold).
  • Plow: 0 cmH₂O (avoids exhaling against pressure but de-recruitment is prevented by the short Tlow).
  • Thigh: 4–6 s (time at Phigh).
  • Tlow: 0.4–0.8 s (release time); titrate so expiratory flow terminates at ~75% of peak.

Used as a rescue or alternative in moderate-severe ARDS at experienced centers; randomized data are mixed, with some signal for improved oxygenation and shorter ventilator days.4

Figure 12.1 — APRV pressure and flow tracings. The pressure scalar shows sustained Phigh (25–30 cmH₂O) interrupted by brief releases toward Plow set = 0, lasting Tlow (0.4–0.8 s). Crucially, airway pressure never reaches the set Plow — Tlow is deliberately shorter than the lung's expiratory time constant, so exhalation is truncated and a substantial end-expiratory pressure (typically 5–10 cmH₂O) remains. This residual is the intrinsic PEEP that maintains recruitment. The patient breathes spontaneously throughout — spontaneous efforts appear as small pressure deflections superimposed on the Phigh plateau. Tlow is titrated so that expiratory flow terminates at ~75% of peak expiratory flow rate — the trick that makes APRV work.

12.3 Neurally adjusted ventilatory assist (NAVA) and proportional assist ventilation (PAV+)

Proportional modes that scale support to either the diaphragm electrical signal (NAVA) or the patient's instantaneous effort (PAV+).

  • NAVA: a specialized nasogastric catheter detects diaphragm electromyographic signal (Edi). The ventilator delivers pressure proportional to Edi — the patient controls timing AND magnitude. Asynchrony rates drop dramatically; trigger latency near-zero (no pneumatic delay). Best for difficult-to-wean patients with significant asynchrony.
  • PAV+: continuously measures patient compliance and resistance and provides a percentage of the work the patient is "asking for." Like NAVA, asynchrony improves; unlike NAVA, no special catheter required.
  • Limitations: requires cooperative patient; not for deeply sedated/paralyzed; learning curve for staff; institution-dependent availability; outcomes data limited.

12.4 Mode selection — bedside cookbook
Mode selection by clinical scenario
Patient / scenarioFirst choiceWhy
Newly intubated, undifferentiatedVC-A/CGuarantees VE, simple, easy to read pressures
ARDSVC-A/CLung-protective targets (Pplat, ΔP) easiest to track
Severe asthma/COPDVC-A/CTight RR + Ti control to manage auto-PEEP
Awake / cooperative, day 2+PSVPatient comfort, less sedation, prep for SBT
Active patient with stiff lungs (worried about Pplat spikes)PC-A/CPressure ceiling protects from spikes
Significant asynchrony despite optimizationNAVA or PAV+ (if available)Patient-driven timing eliminates trigger asynchrony
Refractory ARDS, prone unavailableAPRV (consider)Spontaneous breathing + sustained recruitment
Post-op rapid wean, neurosurgeryVC-A/C → PSVStandard, predictable
Status epilepticus on continuous propofol/midazolamVC-A/CApneic patient, need full mandatory ventilation
RV failure / massive PEVC-A/C with low PEEPRV-protective; tight CO₂ control

12.5 Common mode-related controversies
  • VC vs PC for ARDS: no mortality difference; pick whichever you read more fluently. PC offers a pressure ceiling but VC offers volume guarantee.
  • SIMV for weaning: largely abandoned. Multiple RCTs show prolonged liberation vs PSV-based approach.
  • APRV in ARDS: the Zhou trial (2017) suggested benefit (more vent-free days, lower mortality), but methodologic concerns and conflicting subsequent trials. Use as rescue at experienced centers, not first-line.4
  • Adaptive modes (PRVC) for routine use: marketed as "best of both worlds" but the algorithm can drop support inappropriately in awake patients with high drive (sensing the volume target, then reducing pressure → patient pulls harder → more dyssynchrony). Use cautiously.
The "two modes" ruleMost ICU patients can be managed in two modes: VC-A/C for the controlled phase and PSV for liberation. Mastering these two modes well beats partial knowledge of six. Add APRV, NAVA, and PAV+ as your institution and experience allow.
Key takeaways
  • PRVC / adaptive modes offer hybrid comfort but can drop support inappropriately in awake patients with high drive — use cautiously.
  • APRV (high CPAP with brief releases) maintains spontaneous breathing throughout and has shown signal for improved oxygenation in refractory ARDS at experienced centers.
  • NAVA (diaphragm EMG-triggered) and PAV+ (proportional to effort) eliminate trigger asynchrony and P-SILI but require infrastructure and cooperative patients.
  • Mode selection: VC-A/C for controlled phases; PSV for liberation; APRV/NAVA/PAV+ as rescue at experienced centers.

13Initial settings & the lung-protective bundle

Most vent disasters happen in the first hour after intubation, when settings are wrong by default and no one is watching the trace. This chapter is the day-1 setup checklist for the average adult — and the four lung-protective targets you'll be measured against.

13.1 The day-1 setup checklist

For an intubated adult of average mechanics, on a modern ICU vent, with no specific disease modifier (see chapters 18–24 for ARDS, COPD, etc.):

The four big questions a vent setting answersHow much volume each breath delivers (VT), how often (RR), how much pressure to keep alveoli open at end-exhale (PEEP), and how much oxygen to mix into the inspired gas (FiO₂). Every other knob in the table below is a variation on these four.
Day-1 default settings — average adult
SettingDefaultRationale / range
ModeA/C-VC (volume control with assist-control)Predictable VT, predictable V̇E; pressure trace as mechanics monitor.
VT6 mL/kg PBWRange 4–8 mL/kg PBW; PBW from height (Devine formula). See bedside calc in cheatsheet.
RR14–18Match expected V̇E for normal metabolism (~6–8 L/min); titrate to PaCO₂ on first ABG.
FiO₂1.0 immediately, wean to lowest for SpO₂ 92–96% within 30–60 minAvoid hyperoxia toxicity; once oxygenation is established, drop FiO₂ before raising PEEP.
PEEP5 cmH₂O default; per ARDSnet ladder if FiO₂ > 0.4See §28 for titration.
Flow / pattern50–60 L/min, descending rampFaster flow = shorter Ti = longer Te. Ramp matches demand better than square.
I:E1:2 to 1:31:4–1:5 in obstructive disease.
TriggerFlow trigger 1–3 L/minRe-tune for ineffective triggers or auto-triggering.
AlarmsHigh PIP 40, high Pplat 32, low V̇E 70% of set, high V̇E 150% of set, apnea 20 sSet on every patient, every time — don't accept defaults. High-PIP guards against sudden bronchospasm / kinked or biting ETT; high-V̇E guards against unrecognized agitation or fever.
Cuff20–30 cmH₂OManometer-verified; below 20 → micro-aspiration; above 30 → mucosal ischemia.
HOB30–45°Reduces aspiration / VAP risk.
The first ABG and what to do with itGet an ABG at 30 min. If pH is low (acidemia from hypercarbia), raise the RR by 2–4 breaths/min. If pH is high (alkalemia from over-ventilation), drop the RR. Don't reflexively change VT on the basis of the ABG alone — that's a lung-protection setting, not a pH lever.

13.2 Why those numbers? The four mechanisms of ventilator-induced lung injury (VILI)

The day-1 settings above are not arbitrary; they're calibrated to avoid the four ways a ventilator damages a lung:

VILI mechanisms and the bundle that addresses each
MechanismWhat it isBundle target
VolutraumaOver-stretch from large VT (high regional strain in heterogeneous lung)VT ≤ 8 mL/kg PBW; ARDS ≤ 6
BarotraumaHigh alveolar pressure causing rupture (pneumothorax, pneumomediastinum)Pplat ≤ 30 cmH₂O
AtelectraumaCyclic recruitment-derecruitment of unstable alveoli at low lung volumePEEP titrated to keep alveoli open; ΔP ≤ 15 cmH₂O
BiotraumaInflammatory cytokine release from mechanical stretch — propagates to multi-organ injuryThe combined effect of low VT + low ΔP + appropriate PEEP
P-SILI (patient self-inflicted lung injury)Vigorous spontaneous effort on top of vent settings — pendelluft, high regional strain even on "protective" settingsSedation; consider neuromuscular blockade in severe ARDS (§33)

13.3 The four lung-protective targets — your daily report card

Every patient on the vent should have these four numbers checked on rounds, every day:

The lung-protective bundle
TargetThresholdWhat "out of range" means
VT / PBW≤ 8 mL/kg (ARDS ≤ 6)Volutrauma risk; drop VT
Pplat≤ 30 cmH₂OBarotrauma risk; drop VT by 1 mL/kg, then consider PEEP
ΔP (Pplat − PEEP)≤ 15 cmH₂O (Amato 2015)Strongest VILI predictor; drop VT first, then careful PEEP
Mechanical power≤ 17 J/minMortality begins to climb above this threshold (Serpa Neto 201817). Composite of VT, RR, ΔP, and PEEP — reduce RR or VT before raising PEEP.
The bundle is one number, not fourThe four targets are not independent; they all derive from the equation of motion (§6.1). A high ΔP almost always implies high VT for the lung's compliance. A high MP almost always implies one of {high VT, high RR, high ΔP}. The bedside skill is recognizing which of the four is the rate-limiting metric on this patient and adjusting that one — the others usually fall into line.

13.4 First 24 hours — what to monitor and re-check
  • 30 min: ABG (titrate RR for pH, FiO₂ for SpO₂). End-inspiratory hold to confirm Pplat and ΔP within range.
  • 1 h: Re-check trigger (any auto-triggering or missed efforts?). Check cuff pressure.
  • 2 h: CXR (ETT position 3–5 cm above carina, NGT through diaphragm).
  • 4 h: Re-evaluate mode — is the patient still appropriate for A/C, or is sedation lightening and drive returning (consider PRVC or PSV)?
  • 6 h: Repeat ABG; reassess if any settings changed.
  • 12–24 h: Daily readiness screen for SBT (§36) starts.

13.5 When to deviate from the defaults

Specific patient phenotypes need specific deviations. The defaults above are wrong for:

  • ARDS — VT 4–6 mL/kg PBW, higher PEEP per LOVS table, prone if P/F ≤ 150 (§20).
  • Obstructive (COPD, asthma) — low RR (8–12), long Te, accept hypercapnia, watch for auto-PEEP (§21).
  • Neuro / raised ICP — target PaCO₂ 30–35 (mild hypocapnia), avoid PEEP > 10 if possible (§23).
  • Cardiogenic edema — start with PEEP 8–10 (preload + afterload reduction is therapeutic), expect rapid wean as the heart unloads (§22).
  • Obesity / pregnancy / abdominal hypertension — chest-wall compliance drops; use esophageal manometry to read true transpulmonary pressure rather than airway Pplat (§29).
  • Bronchopleural fistula — switch to PC, accept lower VT, minimize PEEP.
  • Neuromuscular respiratory failure (Guillain–Barré, ALS, myasthenic crisis, high cervical SCI) — lungs are mechanically normal but the pump fails. VT can be higher (6–10 mL/kg PBW) without VILI risk; rate to match drive; aggressive secretion clearance and cough-assist (§43). Watch for early tracheostomy planning if liberation will be prolonged (§26).
  • Post-NIV bridge — patient who failed BiPAP and was intubated. Start with the FiO₂ and PEEP they were on at NIV failure; expect rapid liberation back to NIV if the underlying cause (COPD exacerbation, cardiogenic edema) responds to treatment. Don't over-settle settings — the goal is back to NIV within 24–48 h (§27).
The hyperoxia trapFiO₂ 1.0 is the right initial setting but the wrong setting at hour 4. Hyperoxia (PaO₂ > 200) is independently associated with worse outcomes in post-cardiac-arrest, post-stroke, and septic shock patients. Wean FiO₂ aggressively to the lowest level that holds SpO₂ 92–96% (88–92% in chronic CO₂ retainers).
Key takeaways
  • Day-1 default for an average adult: A/C-VC, VT 6 mL/kg PBW, RR 14–18, PEEP 5 (or per ARDSnet ladder), FiO₂ 1.0 to wean, flow 50–60 with ramp, I:E 1:2–1:3.
  • Set the alarms on every patient: high Pplat 32, low V̇E 70% of set, apnea 20 s. Don't accept the defaults the prior team left.
  • The four VILI mechanisms (volutrauma, barotrauma, atelectrauma, biotrauma) plus P-SILI map onto the four lung-protective targets: VT/PBW ≤ 8, Pplat ≤ 30, ΔP ≤ 15, MP ≤ 17.
  • The targets are not independent — they all derive from the equation of motion. The bedside skill is identifying which metric is rate-limiting and adjusting that one.
  • 30-min ABG titrates RR for pH and FiO₂ for SpO₂. End-inspiratory hold confirms Pplat / ΔP.
  • Wean FiO₂ aggressively after the first hour — hyperoxia is harmful in post-arrest, post-stroke, sepsis.
  • Eight phenotypes need different defaults: ARDS, COPD/asthma, neuro/ICP, cardiogenic edema, obesity/pregnancy/IAH, BPF, neuromuscular failure, NIV bridge.
Part III

Reading the ventilator

The information density at the bedside is on the screen, not in the chart. Six waveforms and three numbers will tell you almost everything you need.

14Waveform scalars — the three traces (and the fourth)

Modern ventilators put four traces on the screen — pressure-time, flow-time, volume-time, and capnography — and the majority of bedside vent diagnoses are made by reading them. Each shape is its own diagnosis; the trick is to scan them in a fixed order and let pattern recognition do the work.

Live waveform simulator — manipulate the equation of motion in real time
Open full-screen ↗
Live simulator. Sweep traces driven by Paw(t) = V(t)/C + Flow(t)·R + PEEP. Pick a mode (VC, PC, PRVC, PS, SIMV, CPAP, APRV) and a scenario (ARDS, COPD, auto-PEEP, ineffective triggering, double-triggering, reverse-triggering, premature/delayed cycling) and watch the shapes change. Drag Compliance and Resistance to feel how the slope of the pressure trace splits into elastic vs resistive components. Use Inspiratory Hold to read Pplat and compute static compliance; use Expiratory Hold to unmask intrinsic PEEP. The simulator's traces are the same physiology this chapter walks through in static form — the figures below are snapshots of what you'll see here.

14.1 Reading the three scalars in order

Develop a top-to-bottom eye discipline. The scalars are stacked because each one answers a different question and the answers feed each other.

What each scalar tells you, in scanning order
TraceQuestion it answersThe four things you read
Pressure-timeHow much force is moving the lung — and where it goes (resistive vs elastic)?PIP · shape during inspiration · Pplat (with hold) · PEEP baseline
Flow-timeHow is gas being delivered, and is exhalation complete?Peak flow · decel pattern · expiratory tail · return to zero before next breath
Volume-timeWhat was actually delivered and exhaled?VT peak · plateau (with hold) · return to baseline · drift across breaths
CapnographyIs metabolism, circulation, and ventilation all coupled?Phase II upslope · Phase III plateau · ETCO₂ value · baseline return to zero
Figure 14.1 — The three scalar traces synchronized. A single VC breath with an end-inspiratory hold. Pressure rises linearly during constant-flow inspiration (the slope reflects compliance + resistance), drops to Pplat when flow stops at the hold (eliminating the resistive component), then decays exponentially through expiration. Flow is the set variable — square positive during inspiration, zero during the hold, then a sharp negative peak at exhalation that decays to zero with the lung's time constant. Volume ramps linearly with flow during inspiration, plateaus during the hold, and decays through exhalation. The three traces share a time axis — read them together.

14.2 Pressure-time — anatomy of the upper trace

The pressure-time waveform is the most diagnostic of the three because it decomposes vertically into the three additive components of airway pressure:

The three components of airway pressure visible on the trace
ComponentWhere it sits on the traceIncreases when…
PEEPBaseline before and after the breathYou set it; auto-PEEP raises measured baseline above set value
Resistive (PIP − Pplat)The "spike" between PIP and the plateauBronchospasm, secretions, kinked ETT, high flow
Elastic / driving (Pplat − PEEP = ΔP)The plateau height above PEEPStiff lung (ARDS, fibrosis, atelectasis), high VT, chest-wall stiffness

Three practical readings of the pressure trace:

  • Concave-up inspiratory ramp (constant flow): the lung is filling against an exponentially increasing elastic load — continued recruitment is happening. Stress index β < 1. Suggests the patient may benefit from more PEEP. (See §15.5.)
  • Convex-up inspiratory ramp: the lung is being inflated past its compliance peak — overdistension. Stress index β > 1. Reduce VT or PEEP.
  • Notch or dip mid-inspiration: a patient effort against a passive-mode breath — the patient is "biting" the trace. Either reduce sedation, switch to a more synchronous mode, or check trigger sensitivity (§18).
The end-inspiratory hold trickTo read Pplat reliably, set a 0.5–1 s hold on a passive (non-triggered) breath. Without the hold, what looks like a "plateau" on the screen still has residual flow contributing to airway pressure, and you'll over-read the elastic component. Most modern vents do this with a single button labeled "Insp hold" or "Pplat."

14.3 Flow-time — the breath archetype

The flow-time scalar instantly identifies the mode and reveals expiratory pathology. Its shape during inspiration tells you what the ventilator is doing; its shape during expiration tells you what the lung is doing.

Inspiratory flow patterns by mode
PatternMode signatureWhat it means
Square (constant)VC with constant-flow waveformSet flow is delivered at the rate you specified; pressure rises along the lung's loading curve
Decelerating rampPC, PSV, or VC with descending-ramp waveformFlow starts at peak, decays as the alveolar–airway pressure gradient closes
Sinusoidal / patient-shapedPSV with patient-controlled timingPatient effort drives the rise and fall; usually mirrors a biological breath
Variable, irregularAdaptive modes (PRVC, NAVA, PAV+)The vent is adjusting target with each breath

Three diagnostic patterns visible on the expiratory side of the flow scalar:

  • Scooped (concave) inspiratory limb (in VC mode): the patient is pulling more flow than the vent is set to deliver — flow starvation. Switch to descending ramp or increase peak flow (§18.4).
  • Saw-tooth on expiration: secretions oscillating in the airway / ETT. Suction. Don't ignore — secretions both raise resistance and increase asynchrony.
  • Expiratory flow not returning to zero before the next breath: the lung has not finished exhaling. Auto-PEEP / dynamic hyperinflation. Drop the rate, lengthen Te, or shorten Ti (§17).

14.4 Volume-time — leak and trapping detection

The volume-time trace is the simplest of the three but answers two questions nothing else does:

  • Did the patient exhale what you delivered? If the volume trace doesn't return to baseline (zero), there's a leak — circuit, cuff, or bronchopleural fistula. The size of the gap is the leak volume per breath.
