  • Is the lung volume drifting up over multiple breaths? The end-expiratory baseline rises with each breath when air-trapping is severe — visible only on volume (the flow trace shows it as failure to return to zero, but volume integrates the trapping over time).

In low-VT ventilation (ARDS), watch the volume trace closely — a 50 mL leak that's tolerable at VT 600 is 10% of an ARDS VT of 350.

14.5 Capnography — the fourth waveform you should always have

Continuous waveform end-tidal CO₂ is the single most under-used screen at the bedside. It couples ventilation, perfusion, and metabolism into one trace, and the four-phase anatomy diagnoses problems no other monitor can.

The four phases of a normal capnogram
PhaseWhat it isWhat it should look like
Phase IAnatomic dead-space exhalation (no CO₂ from alveoli yet)Flat, near-zero baseline
Phase IIMixed dead-space + alveolar gas (the upstroke)Steep, near-vertical rise
Phase IIIAlveolar plateau — CO₂ from gas-exchanging alveoliSlight upslope (0–3°), reflecting V/Q heterogeneity
Phase IVInspiratory drop to baselineVertical drop back to 0

Two angles to know:

  • α-angle (Phase II → III): normal < 100°. Wider angles flatten the plateau and suggest obstructive disease (V/Q heterogeneity blunts the transition).
  • β-angle (Phase III → I): normal 90°. Increased angle suggests rebreathing — exhausted CO₂ absorber or ventilator circuit leak from inspiratory limb.

14.6 Six classic capnograph patterns — and what to do
Capnograph pattern atlas
PatternCauseAction
"Shark fin" — sloped Phase II merging into upsloped plateauObstructive disease (asthma, COPD); kinked ETTTreat bronchospasm; check ETT patency; suction
Curare cleft — small notch in mid-Phase IIIReturning patient effort during paralysis recoveryIf unwanted, redose NMB; if recovery is the goal, prepare for spontaneous trial
Sudden drop to zeroDisconnection, dislodged ETT, esophageal intubation, cardiac arrestImmediate physical exam, breath sounds, capnogram check; pulse / rhythm assessment
Rising baseline (Phase 0 > 0)Rebreathing — exhausted CO₂ absorber, inadequate fresh-gas flowReplace absorber; increase fresh-gas flow; check valves
Cardiac oscillations on plateauSmall VT at slow RR exposes heart-beat synchronous oscillations of mediastinumBenign artifact; consider if it interferes with cycle-off in PSV
"Camel-hump" — two peaksLateral position with unilateral disease (one lung empties before the other)Reposition; investigate unilateral pathology
Capnography in cardiac arrestThe single most actionable use of waveform capnography is during CPR. ETCO₂ < 10 mmHg after 20 minutes of high-quality compressions predicts no ROSC; a sudden rise to 35–40 mmHg is the most sensitive sign of return of spontaneous circulation — often before pulse is palpable. End every code with a capnogram check.
ETCO₂ ≠ PaCO₂ in dead spaceThe arterial-to-end-tidal gradient (P(a−et)CO₂) is normally 2–5 mmHg. In ARDS it can widen to 10–15; in pulmonary embolism, > 20. Trend ETCO₂ on stable settings, but verify PaCO₂ with an ABG in any patient with high dead space (see §4.1).
Live capnography library — pick a pattern or adjust the physiology directly
Open full-screen ↗
Figure 14.2 — Capnography waveform library (interactive). Live sweep of the six classical capnogram shapes — normal, obstructive ("shark-fin"), curare cleft, cardiogenic oscillations, rebreathing, and sudden drop to zero. Pick a pattern to load characteristic physiology, then drag the sliders (respiratory rate, peak ETCO₂, Phase III slope, baseline / β-angle, I:E, heart rate) to recreate the bedside trace. Scanning order: Phase III plateau height (ETCO₂ value) → Phase II steepness → baseline return to zero → trend across breaths.

14.7 The hold maneuvers — what they actually measure and their limits

Two single-button maneuvers on every modern ventilator make the most diagnostic readings on the screen possible. The inspiratory hold reveals true alveolar pressure (Pplat); the expiratory hold reveals total end-expiratory pressure (and so intrinsic PEEP). Both close both valves simultaneously, abolishing flow and allowing pressures inside the lung to equilibrate with the manometer in the circuit. Both are valid only in a passive patient — any spontaneous effort (or trigger attempt) ruins the reading.

The two hold maneuvers at a glance
Inspiratory holdExpiratory hold
TimingEnd-inspiration, both valves closedEnd-expiration, both valves closed
Pressure readPplat (≈ alveolar pressure at end-inspiration)Total PEEP (set PEEP + intrinsic PEEP)
What it abolishesThe resistive component (no flow → no resistive drop)Continuous expiratory flow (small-airway pressure equilibrates upstream)
Recommended duration0.5–2 s (CICM/Harris recommend 2 s for full equilibration)0.5–1 s
Patient state requiredPassive (no inspiratory effort during hold)Passive (no active expiration or trigger attempt during hold)
14.7.1 Reading the inspiratory hold — what Pplat is, and isn't

When you press "Insp hold" at end-inspiration, both valves close and inspiratory flow stops. Within ~ 50 ms the resistive pressure drop disappears, exposing the elastic alveolar pressure. The trace falls from PIP to a plateau — that is Pplat.

  • Why a 2-second hold is better than 0.5: during the hold the airway pressure usually drifts down for the first second or two before settling. That drift is real physiology — it reflects (a) tissue stress relaxation (lung tissue is viscoelastic and slowly accommodates the new volume) and (b) pendelluft, the redistribution of gas between lung units with different time constants (long-τ alveoli are still filling from neighbors while short-τ alveoli are emptying into them). A 0.5-second hold captures the early "relaxed" pressure but may miss the final equilibration; a full 2-second hold gives the true Pplat.
  • Pplat & intrinsic PEEP: a reliably-measured Pplat is also the cleanest representation of the highest regional intrinsic PEEP. The high inspiratory pressure splints all small airways open, so the alveolar pressure across the entire lung equilibrates with the circuit — including alveoli served by airways that were collapsed at end-expiration. In severe heterogeneous obstruction, Pplat (after a 2-s hold) is therefore often a better indicator of the worst regional auto-PEEP than the expiratory hold reading is. Counter-intuitive but practically important.
  • Mode caveat: in pressure-control modes a "Pplat" displayed during steady inspiration is not a true plateau — there is still ongoing flow late in the breath, especially in stiff lungs. Use an explicit hold maneuver, not the displayed end-inspiratory pressure, when the answer matters (driving pressure, ARDS PEEP titration).
14.7.2 Reading the expiratory hold — and why it under-reads

The expiratory hold pauses the breath at end-expiration: both valves close, no further breath is delivered, and circuit pressure rises to meet whatever is sitting in the alveoli. The rising plateau on the trace gives total PEEP; subtracting the set PEEP (the dial value) yields intrinsic PEEP.

  • Dynamic airway closure underestimates true PEEPi. In obstructive disease (especially asthma and severe COPD) many small airways collapse during expiration. The alveoli behind those collapsed airways are sealed off — their pressure cannot equilibrate with the circuit during a brief hold. Only lung units served by patent airways contribute to the reading, so the expiratory-hold value is a floor on the true regional PEEPi, not a ceiling.36
  • Active patient ruins the reading. Any expiratory abdominal effort raises pressure artifactually high; any inspiratory trigger attempt drops it. In a non-paralyzed, distressed patient the number is essentially uninterpretable. Sedate, ensure passive breathing, then hold.
  • Prolonged holds (some vents allow up to 5 s) help in severe obstruction by giving slow lung units time to equilibrate, but a hold longer than ~ 3 s often triggers the patient unless they are deeply sedated or paralyzed.
Figure 14.3 — The two hold maneuvers compared. Left: inspiratory hold. The trace falls from PIP to Pplat as the resistive component is abolished, then drifts gently down for ~ 1–2 s as tissue stress-relaxation and pendelluft equilibrate. A 2-s hold gives the true plateau; a 0.5-s reading captures the early "relaxed" pressure and may miss late equilibration. Right: expiratory hold. Circuit pressure rises from set PEEP to total PEEP. The gap is intrinsic PEEP (PEEPi). Dynamic small-airway closure means the reading equilibrates only with alveoli served by patent airways — in heterogeneous obstructive disease the true regional PEEPi may be substantially higher than the screen value.
The "Pplat reads PEEPi too" trickIn heterogeneous obstruction (severe asthma, end-stage COPD), the expiratory hold often under-reads PEEPi because collapsed small airways isolate alveoli from the circuit. A long inspiratory hold (2 s, paralyzed) splints those small airways open, and the resulting Pplat is often a more reliable representation of the worst regional intrinsic pressure. Use both maneuvers together — they answer different (and partially overlapping) questions.
Trust your hold only if the patient is passiveAn active inspiratory effort during an inspiratory hold pulls Pplat artifactually low; an active expiratory effort during an expiratory hold pushes total PEEP artifactually high. Either way, the number is meaningless. Confirm passive breathing (no flow deflections, no Pmus on the esophageal trace, RASS deep enough) before trusting any plateau or auto-PEEP measurement that drives a clinical decision.

14.8 PRVC and adaptive-mode waveforms — what to look for

Adaptive modes (PRVC, AutoFlow, Volume-Guarantee, AVAPS, ASV, NAVA, PAV+) all use breath-by-breath feedback to adjust either the pressure target or the inspiratory time. Their waveforms look like pressure-control breaths — decelerating flow, square pressure — but with a few signatures worth recognizing on rounds.

  • PRVC (and AutoFlow, VC+, Volume Guarantee): the inspiratory pressure target changes from breath to breath as the lung's compliance changes. On the screen the pressure ceiling drifts up or down by ~ 1–3 cmH₂O between breaths in a stable patient — this is normal and reflects the algorithm tracking compliance. A sharp jump in pressure target (≥ 5 cmH₂O between consecutive breaths) means a sudden drop in compliance: secretions, kink, mucous plug, evolving ARDS, abdominal compartment hypertension. Investigate, don't just let the algorithm chase it.
  • NAVA: pressure trace shape is patient-driven, so it varies breath-to-breath. The unique signature is the Edi (diaphragm electrical activity) trace: a triangular spike at each breath. Loss of the Edi spike during NAVA = either over-assistance, sedation excess, or catheter dislodgement — check the Edi tracing first, the airway pressure trace second.
  • PAV+: continuously varies pressure support in proportion to estimated patient effort. Look for a stable percent of work assistance (the "PAV gain" %) and watch the dynamic loop area as a free work-of-breathing trend.
  • APRV: not strictly adaptive but worth recognizing — two pressure plateaus (P-high, P-low) with brief releases at P-low. Spontaneous breaths superimposed on either plateau show as small flow excursions — these contribute to minute ventilation and should not be suppressed unless oxygenation is failing.
Adaptive modes can mask deteriorating mechanicsThe whole point of PRVC is to deliver the set VT regardless of compliance — meaning a quietly-stiffening lung produces a quietly-rising pressure with no alarm until the upper limit is hit. Look at the pressure trend, not just the volume. A morning pressure target of 18 cmH₂O drifting to 28 over 12 hours at the same VT is the signature of evolving ARDS, even though "the volume is on target."
Key takeaways
  • Read pressure → flow → volume → capnography in that order, top to bottom on the screen — and always at the bedside on rounds.
  • Pressure-time decomposes vertically into PEEP + resistive (PIP − Pplat) + elastic (Pplat − PEEP). Read those three numbers as a stack.
  • Concave inspiratory pressure ramp (β < 1) suggests further recruitment is possible; convex (β > 1) suggests overdistension. §15.5.
  • Flow-time identifies mode (square = VC, decel = PC/PSV) and exhalation pathology (saw-tooth = secretions; no return to zero = auto-PEEP).
  • Volume-time catches what nothing else does: leak (no return to baseline) and progressive air-trapping (drift up over breaths).
  • Capnography couples metabolism + circulation + ventilation. Six pattern shapes, four phases, two angles. Sudden drop to 0 = disconnection / arrest; sudden rise during compressions = ROSC.
  • ETCO₂ underestimates PaCO₂ in proportion to dead space — verify with ABG in ARDS, PE, low-CO states.
  • Inspiratory hold (2 s) gives true Pplat — and in obstruction it often estimates regional PEEPi better than the expiratory hold, because the high inspiratory pressure splints small airways open.36
  • Expiratory hold reads total PEEP only in alveoli served by patent airways — in heterogeneous obstruction the true regional PEEPi can be substantially higher than the screen number. Both holds are valid only in a passive patient.
  • Adaptive modes (PRVC, AutoFlow) change pressure breath-to-breath as compliance changes — read the pressure trend, not just the displayed VT. A drifting pressure target is the early sign of evolving ARDS.

15Pressure-volume and flow-volume loops

Where the scalars in §14 plot pressure, flow, and volume against time, the loops plot them against each other — and that change of axes makes new mechanical features visible. Two loops are worth the screen real estate at the bedside: pressure-volume (recruitment, overdistension, hysteresis) and flow-volume (obstruction, leaks, secretions).

15.1 The pressure-volume loop — dynamic vs static

P-V loops come in two flavors that look similar but answer different questions:

Dynamic vs static P-V loops
Dynamic P-V loopStatic (low-flow) P-V curve
How drawnReal-time during any breathSlow inflation (5–10 L/min) or stepwise inflation hold
Slope reflectsCompliance and resistance combinedPure compliance (resistive contribution removed by slow flow)
Best forQuick visual scan of compliance trends, asynchrony, leaksLocating LIP and UIP for PEEP titration (§28)
Bedside availabilityAlways on, every breathSpecial maneuver (most modern vents have a low-flow mode)

The dynamic loop is what you look at every day; the static curve is a research-grade tool that occasionally surfaces at the bedside for hard ARDS PEEP titration.

15.2 Reading the dynamic P-V loop — five bedside features

The dynamic loop, oriented with pressure on the x-axis and volume on the y, is traversed counter-clockwise: inspiration is the upper limb (volume rises with pressure), expiration is the lower limb (volume falls back to zero).

Five features visible on the dynamic P-V loop
FeatureWhat it looks likeWhat it means
Slope (overall)The line from PEEP/0 to peak pointDynamic compliance ≈ VT / (PIP − PEEP). Steeper = better compliance.
Bird-beak at the topLoop flattens horizontally near peak — pressure rises with no further volume gainOverdistension. Reduce VT; consider lowering PEEP.
Inflection knee at the bottomInitial slope is shallow, then steepens after a "kink"Recruitment happening above the knee — set PEEP at or just above this point (LIP).
Hysteresis areaInspiratory limb above expiratory limb; enclosed areaResistive work + recruitment energy. Large hysteresis with bird-beak = wasted work, mostly heat.
Rightward shiftLoop moves to the right at the same VT over hours/daysWorsening compliance — atelectasis, edema, fibrotic progression.
The square-wave appearance trapPressure-control breaths produce a rectangular P-V loop because pressure is the set variable (it's held at Pset across the entire inspiration). This isn't pathologic — it's just the mode. Compliance is read from the height (VT) divided by ΔP (Pset − PEEP). Don't confuse a PC-mode rectangle with VC-mode bird-beak.

15.3 Flow-volume loops on the vent — how they differ from outpatient PFTs

Flow on the y-axis, volume on the x-axis; inspiration above the zero line, expiration below; loop traversed counter-clockwise. The shape language is identical to outpatient spirometry — the classical six-pattern atlas is in §5.1, and those signatures still appear on a vented patient, scaled down to tidal volume rather than vital capacity.

Two reading differences matter at the bedside:

  • Effort-independent shape. The expiratory limb is drawn by passive recoil, not forced effort. The "scooped" COPD curve is therefore amplified when the patient stops fighting the vent, and a bronchodilator response shows up as visible un-scooping of the expiratory limb between snapshots — a real-time response monitor outpatient PFTs can't offer.
  • The loop should close at zero. If the expiratory limb doesn't return to the volume baseline before the next inspiration begins, the loop hangs open in mid-air — the signature of auto-PEEP (§17). A different opening — a horizontal gap between the start and end of the loop on the volume axis — points to a circuit or cuff leak: gas in did not equal gas out.

What to scan for at the bedside (saw-tooth secretions, scooped expiratory limb, late inspiratory peak, expiratory notch) is in §15.4.

15.4 Bedside features of the flow-volume loop
  • Concave (scooped) expiratory limb — obstructive disease. The earlier and steeper the scoop, the more severe the obstruction. Acute change → bronchospasm or new mucus plug.
  • Saw-tooth on the expiratory limb — secretions in the airway/ETT. Suction the patient.
  • Expiratory limb not returning to zero before the next breath — auto-PEEP. The flow-volume loop closes in mid-air; the next inspiration starts from a non-zero baseline (§17).
  • Loop area shifted right at the same VT — increased dead space or compliance change.
  • Inspiratory limb shows late peak — patient effort superimposed on the mandatory breath; consider PSV or trigger sensitivity adjustment.
  • Notch in the expiratory limb — early reverse flow, often from cardiac oscillations or asynchrony.

15.5 Stress index — overdistension or recruitment from the pressure-time scalar

During constant-flow VC ventilation, the inspiratory pressure-time curve is mathematically described by:

$$P_{aw}(t) \;=\; a \cdot t^{\beta} \;+\; c$$

The shape coefficient β is the stress index:

Stress index — visual interpretation
β valueCurve shapeLung stateAction
β = 1Linear pressure riseOptimal compliance throughout the breath — neither recruitment nor overdistensionMaintain settings
β < 1Concave-up (curves toward the volume axis)Compliance improves as volume rises — continued recruitment↑ PEEP — hold the alveoli open
β > 1Convex-up (curves away from the volume axis)Compliance worsens as volume rises — overdistension↓ VT first, then consider ↓ PEEP if Pplat still high
The eyeball stress indexYou don't need software to read β. With a constant-flow VC breath in front of you, mentally draw a straight line from the start of the inspiratory ramp to the peak. If the actual curve sits below the line in the middle (bows down toward the volume axis), β < 1 — recruitment. If the curve sits above the line, β > 1 — overdistension. The visual is fast and surprisingly reliable; published validation shows good correlation with the algorithmic stress index in ARDS (Grasso 2007).

15.6 Loops and asynchrony — what to recognize

Several asynchrony patterns are easier to see on the loops than on the scalars:

  • Open / unclosed P-V loop: expiratory limb doesn't meet the inspiratory start point — leak around the cuff or in the circuit.
  • Figure-8 shape on the P-V loop: the loop crosses itself — usually a vigorous patient effort during a passive breath. Check sedation and trigger.
  • Double-loop on the flow-volume tracing: two stacked breaths with no expiration between — breath stacking / double trigger (§18.3.3).
  • Dimples or notches on the inspiratory limb of the F-V loop: missed triggers or trigger oscillations — verify trigger sensitivity.
The single most useful loop habit on roundsGlance at the dynamic P-V loop on every patient, every day. The slope is your free, real-time compliance trend; the area is your free work-of-breathing surrogate. Two days of "the loop is shifting right and getting fatter" is the earliest sign of evolving ARDS or volume overload — long before P/F or X-ray catches up.
Don't titrate PEEP from the dynamic loop aloneThe dynamic loop confounds compliance with resistance, and the apparent "knee" is a function of flow as much as of recruitment. For real LIP/UIP-guided PEEP titration use a static low-flow inflation (or, even better, esophageal manometry — see §29). Use the dynamic loop for trend, the static curve for setpoint.

15.7 The static P-V curve in detail — moved to §7.7

The deep-dive on the static P-V curve — three regions of the inflation limb, four hysteresis mechanisms, the LIP titration problem, and disease-specific patterns — now lives with the rest of the P-V curve material in §7.7. Click through for the tables and analysis; this chapter stays focused on bedside loops that update in real time.

15.8 Pathology-specific patterns on the dynamic loops — a quick atlas

For day-to-day rounds the dynamic P-V and F-V loops carry a small but high-yield pattern library. Pair these with the scalar findings in §14 to triangulate any acute change.

Pathology-specific loop signatures
DiagnosisDynamic P-V loop signatureFlow-volume loop signature
Bronchospasm (acute)Loop unchanged in shape; dynamic compliance preserved; PIP ↑ but Pplat normal — gap visible as horizontal stretchConcave (scooped) expiratory limb; reduced peak expiratory flow; expiratory limb may not return to zero (auto-PEEP)39
Secretions in the airway/ETTSubtle loop irregularity; PIP rises while Pplat unchangedSaw-tooth oscillations on the expiratory limb — usually pathognomonic; suction
Mucous plug / partial ETT obstructionPIP rises sharply between breaths; the loop "moves right" without changing slopeTruncated peak expiratory flow; expiratory limb scooped
Worsening compliance (ARDS, edema)Loop shifts right; slope decreases (loop fattens, leans further from vertical); bird-beak appears at high VTLoop area shrinks; both limbs shorter
Air leak (cuff or circuit)Open loop — expiratory limb does not return to inspiratory start point; baseline missingExpired flow integrates to less than inspired; loop area asymmetric
Bronchopleural fistulaAs above, with a continuous leak that does not vary with airway pressurePersistent asymmetry; cycle-by-cycle change in tidal volume
Patient effort (flow starvation)Concave (scalloped) inspiratory limb of the P-V loop — pressure dips as the patient pulls more flow than the vent is set to deliverInspiratory limb shows a late peak superimposed on the mandatory breath
Asynchrony — double triggerTwo stacked loops with no return to zero betweenTwo stacked breaths; expiratory limb interrupted by a second inspiration
Variable extra-thoracic obstructionNot characteristic on P-V loopFlattened inspiratory limb; expiratory limb preserved (vocal-cord paralysis, goiter)
Variable intra-thoracic obstructionNot characteristic on P-V loopFlattened expiratory limb; inspiratory limb preserved (tracheomalacia, mediastinal mass)
Fixed central-airway obstructionNot characteristic on P-V loop"Box-shaped" loop — both limbs flattened (tracheal stenosis, tumor)
The morning loop habitOne minute, every patient: compare today's dynamic P-V loop to yesterday's screen capture (most ICU vents save them in the trends menu). Three things to ask: Has the loop shifted right? (worsening compliance — investigate). Has it grown a beak? (VT too large for current lung — drop VT, then PEEP if Pplat still high). Is the area growing without slope changing? (rising resistive work — bronchospasm, secretions). The loop trend often beats the chest X-ray by 12–24 hours.
Key takeaways
  • Dynamic P-V loop = real-time, every breath, every mode. Slope = dynamic compliance; area = resistive + recruitment work; bird-beak = overdistension; knee = LIP. Static curve = research-grade PEEP-titration tool.
  • Flow-volume loop atlas — six pattern shapes cover almost every diagnostic question: normal, obstructive (scooped), restrictive (small), fixed upper-airway (box), variable extrathoracic (insp clip), variable intrathoracic (exp clip).
  • Saw-tooth on the F-V expiratory limb = secretions. Failure to return to zero = auto-PEEP. Open P-V loop = leak.
  • Stress index β: concave (β < 1) means more PEEP could help; convex (β > 1) means drop VT; linear (β = 1) means leave it alone. You can read it by eye on a constant-flow VC breath.
  • Loops are most useful as a daily-trend tool and an asynchrony-detection screen — not a sole basis for PEEP setting.
  • Static P-V curve = three regions (lower flat / linear / beak) and two inflections (LIP / UIP). Hysteresis has four mechanisms: recruitment, surfactant, stress-relaxation, gas absorption.37
  • "PEEP = LIP + 2" is conceptually correct but practically unreliable — clinicians vary by ~ 11 cmH₂O on the same curve.38 Use esophageal manometry, decremental PEEP trial, R/I ratio, or the ARDSnet ladder for a real titration.
  • Bronchospasm: high PIP, normal Pplat, scooped expiratory limb, low PEFR. Restrictive lung: small loop, rapid insp-flow drop, rapid recoil exp flow.39

16The three pressures: PIP, Pplat, driving pressure

To measure them: in VC mode with a square flow pattern, perform a 0.5-second end-inspiratory hold. Read PIP at end of insufflation, Pplat after the hold, PEEP from baseline.

QuantityWhat it representsTargets
PIP (peak airway)Resistive + elastic pressure during flowTracks ETT/airway integrity
Pplat (plateau)Elastic pressure (lung + chest-wall)≤ 30 cmH₂O
ΔP (driving pressure) = Pplat − PEEPCyclic strain on the lung≤ 15 cmH₂O — the single mechanic best correlated with ARDS mortality5
PIP − PplatResistive pressure (flow × Raw)< 5 indicates clean airway
The two-question diagnostic algorithm1. Is PIP high? 2. Is Pplat high?   If yes/yes → lung mechanics (ARDS, edema, atelectasis, abdominal hypertension). If yes/no → resistance (ETT, bronchospasm, biting, secretions). This dichotomy alone solves most pressure alarms.

16.1 The three pressures and the two-question algorithm — at a glance

The text and the table above are the §16.1 summary — every pressure alarm in the ICU can be triaged with the two-question dichotomy and the three numbers (PIP, Pplat, ΔP). The rest of this chapter expands the calculation with a bedside tool (§16.2) and the four-axis mortality bundle (§16.3, deferred to the lung-protective discussion in §13.3).

16.2 Bedside calculator — driving pressure, compliance, mechanical power

Plug in your numbers and get all three:

mL cmH₂O cmH₂O cmH₂O /min
Why this matters at the bedsideThree numbers — VT, Pplat, PEEP — generate the four mortality-correlated mechanics (ΔP, Cstat, mechanical power, resistive ΔP). Track them on every set of vent rounds, every mode change, and every clinical worsening.
Key takeaways
  • PIP is resistive + elastic + PEEP; Pplat is elastic + PEEP (flow = 0); ΔP (Pplat − PEEP) is cyclic strain — three numbers collapse to one: the two-question algorithm.
  • Is PIP high? Is Pplat high? Yes/yes = lung mechanics (ARDS, edema, atelectasis). Yes/no = resistance (ETT, bronchospasm, biting).
  • ΔP is the variable most strongly correlated with ARDS mortality (Amato 2015) — hazard ratio 0.4 per 7 cmH₂O. Target ≤ 15 (lower is better).
  • Mechanical power (MP) = 0.098 × RR × VT × (PIP − ½ΔP) in J/min; > 17 associated with mortality — reduces via ↓RR (most impactful), ↓VT, ↓ΔP, ↓peak flow.

17Auto-PEEP — detection and management

Auto-PEEP (intrinsic PEEP, PEEPi) is alveolar pressure at end-expiration that exceeds the set PEEP, caused by incomplete emptying. It does three things, all bad: (a) increases the patient's work of triggering — they have to overcome the trapped pressure before the vent even senses an effort; (b) compromises hemodynamics from raised intrathoracic pressure squeezing venous return; (c) makes the displayed mechanics (PIP, Pplat, ΔP) under-read because the manometer baseline is wrong. For any COPD/asthma/obstructive patient on the vent — and for any patient with set RR or VT outpacing what their lung can exhale — auto-PEEP is one of the highest-yield numbers to check on rounds.

17.1 How to detect auto-PEEP at the bedside
  • Look at the flow-time trace: expiratory flow does not return to zero before the next breath.
  • Perform an end-expiratory hold: close both valves for 0.5–1 s; airway pressure equilibrates with alveolar pressure. Total PEEP − set PEEP = auto-PEEP.
  • Cross-check with an end-inspiratory hold: in heterogeneous obstructive disease the inspiratory hold (with a 2-s pause and a passive patient) splints small airways open and is often a more accurate representation of the worst regional intrinsic PEEP than the expiratory hold reading. See §14.7.
Why the expiratory hold under-readsThe expiratory hold equilibrates only with alveoli served by patent airways. In severe obstruction, dynamic small-airway closure isolates many alveoli from the manometer, so the displayed total PEEP is a floor on the true regional PEEPi.36 An active patient also breaks the reading: any inspiratory effort drops the trace artifactually, any forced exhalation pushes it artifactually high. Both holds are valid only in a passive patient.

To see auto-PEEP develop in real time across three synchronized scalars, open the §14 waveform simulator and select the "auto-PEEP" scenario — the pressure baseline climbs breath-over-breath, the expiratory flow tail never returns to zero, and end-expiratory volume escalates with each cycle.

17.2 Worked example — unmasking auto-PEEP

Scenario. 64M, severe COPD exacerbation, intubated 8 hours ago. On A/C-VC, VT 420 mL, RR 22, set PEEP 5, FiO₂ 0.40. Latest ABG: pH 7.18, PaCO₂ 78, PaO₂ 92. Rounds finding: hemodynamically borderline (MAP 62 on a small norepinephrine drip), increasingly agitated, you suspect the vent is part of the problem.

Step 1 — read the flow trace. Pause the flow-time scalar. The expiratory tail is still ~ 8 L/min downward at the moment of the next inspiration — flow never returns to zero. Auto-PEEP is present.

Step 2 — measure it. Briefly suppress patient effort (push 50 mcg fentanyl + brief propofol bolus) so the holds are valid. Perform an end-expiratory hold for 1 s.

  • Reading: total PEEP = 17 cmH₂O.
  • Set PEEP = 5.
  • Auto-PEEP = 17 − 5 = 12 cmH₂O. Substantial.

Step 3 — re-interpret the mechanics. Displayed Pplat 32 looks like it's at the lung-protective ceiling. But the true end-inspiratory alveolar pressure is what matters, and the true elastic ΔP is (Pplat − total PEEP) = 32 − 17 = 15, not (Pplat − set PEEP) = 27. The number was hiding gas trapping, not safety.

Step 4 — fix it in priority order.

  1. Drop RR from 22 → 12. Permissive hypercapnia (pH was already 7.18 — accept ≥ 7.20 with bicarb if needed). Single most effective lever.
  2. Increase peak inspiratory flow (60 → 80 L/min) so Ti shortens and Te lengthens — gives the lung more time to empty between breaths.
  3. Treat bronchospasm. Albuterol/ipratropium MDI through the circuit, IV methylprednisolone, consider IV magnesium 2 g.
  4. Match extrinsic PEEP to ~75–80% of total PEEP. Set PEEP 5 → 12 (or 13). The patient's inspiratory effort no longer has to overcome 12 cmH₂O of trapped pressure before the vent sees the effort — work of triggering plummets. (Note: this only works in obstructive physiology; do not raise PEEP in patients without auto-PEEP "to be on the safe side" — it overdistends.)
  5. Re-measure after 5 minutes. If total PEEP has fallen but auto-PEEP persists, repeat the cycle.
The two-line bedside checkEvery COPD or asthma patient on the vent gets this on rounds: (1) does expiratory flow reach zero before the next breath? (2) what's the end-expiratory hold reading? Two minutes, every shift. The number you'll catch climbing is the one that kills the patient on hour 12 when the bedside RN gives "just a little more rate" to fix a rising PaCO₂.

17.3 Induction collapse — the auto-PEEP killer

The single most lethal moment in obstructive disease is the few seconds after intubation. The mechanism:

  1. Pre-intubation, the patient is breathing fast and shallow, generating their own auto-PEEP from incomplete exhalation. They tolerate it because they're awake, sympathetically activated, and their chest wall is doing some of the work.
  2. Induction drugs (propofol, etomidate, ketamine) drop sympathetic tone and abolish spontaneous breathing.
  3. The bag-valve-mask operator squeezes too fast — typical RR 20+ on a bag during pre-oxygenation. Each breath stacks on the prior. Total PEEP climbs to 20–30 cmH₂O in seconds.
  4. Intrathoracic pressure spikes → venous return to the right heart drops → cardiac output crashes → PEA arrest within 60–120 seconds of intubation.
If the patient codes immediately after intubation in an obstructive picture — disconnect the circuit.This is one of the very few times in critical care when removing the vent from a hypoxic patient is the right move. Detach the circuit at the ETT, let the chest passively decompress for 20–30 seconds, then resume bagging at RR 6–8, slow flow, long expiratory pause. Most patients recover hemodynamics within 1–2 minutes once the trapping resolves. Pulse comes back without epinephrine, without compressions, without anything except releasing the trapped pressure. The mnemonic is the DOPES reverse-check for sudden post-intubation deterioration: Dislodged ETT · Obstructed ETT · Pneumothorax · Equipment failure · Stacked breaths (auto-PEEP). When DOPE fails, the S is usually the answer in obstructive patients.

17.4 Matched-PEEP decision flow

The matched-PEEP strategy (set extrinsic PEEP to ~75–80% of measured total PEEP) only applies when the patient is triggering against trapped pressure. Walk through the three questions in order:

When (and when not) to match PEEP to measured auto-PEEP
QuestionYes → actionNo → action
Is auto-PEEP present? (Flow doesn't return to zero, or end-expiratory hold shows total > set PEEP.) Go to question 2. Don't raise PEEP — overdistends without benefit.
Is the patient triggering breaths? (PSV, or A/C with patient-triggered breaths above the set rate.) Go to question 3 — matched PEEP will reduce trigger work. Patient is fully passive — matched PEEP doesn't help trigger work. Instead address the cause: ↓ RR, ↑ Te, treat bronchospasm.
Is hemodynamics borderline? (Vasopressor-requiring, raising PEEP risks worsening it.) Match carefully: extrinsic PEEP at 50–60% of total first, watch MAP. If tolerated, advance to 75–80%. Match to 75–80% of total PEEP directly; recheck auto-PEEP in 5 min.

The physiology of matched PEEP. The patient's inspiratory effort has to drop alveolar pressure below the trigger threshold to start a breath. With set PEEP 5 and auto-PEEP 12, the alveolus sits at 17; the patient must generate −2 below trigger, i.e., bring alveolar pressure from 17 → 3 → a 14 cmH₂O effort just to start a breath. Raising extrinsic PEEP to 10 means the alveolus sits at 17 (unchanged — extrinsic and intrinsic are not additive when the airway is dynamically obstructed; they overlap), but the trigger threshold is now relative to set PEEP 10, so the patient only has to drop alveolar pressure 7 cmH₂O to trigger. Work of triggering halves.

Why extrinsic and intrinsic PEEP overlap, not addIn flow-limited obstructive disease, the small airways collapse dynamically on exhalation. Once collapsed, alveolar pressure is decoupled from airway opening pressure — the alveolus is at the auto-PEEP level regardless of what extrinsic PEEP you apply, up to the point where extrinsic PEEP exceeds the closing pressure of the collapsed airway. Above that crossover (typically ~ 80% of total PEEP), further extrinsic PEEP starts to add and you risk overdistension. Hence the 75–80% rule.

17.5 The complete fix-list

In rough priority order from most effective to most adjunctive:

  1. ↓ RR — single most effective lever; accept permissive hypercapnia (pH ≥ 7.20).
  2. ↓ VT — less air to exhale per breath; couples with ↓ RR for additive effect on minute ventilation.
  3. ↑ peak inspiratory flow (VC) or ↓ Ti (PC) — shortens inspiration to lengthen expiration.
  4. Treat the bronchospasm: aggressive β-agonist + ipratropium + steroid; in severe asthma add IV magnesium, ketamine, heliox (60:40 He:O₂), and consider neuromuscular blockade.
  5. Match ~75–80% of total PEEP if the patient is triggering against auto-PEEP (improves trigger work — see §17.4).
  6. If hemodynamic collapse: disconnect the circuit and let the chest passively empty for 20–30 s, then resume bagging slowly (§17.3).
Key takeaways
  • Auto-PEEP (intrinsic PEEP) occurs when expiratory flow doesn't return to zero before the next breath — causes increased work of triggering, hemodynamic compromise, underestimation of mechanics.
  • Detection: look at flow-time trace (no return to zero), perform end-expiratory hold (total PEEP − set PEEP = auto-PEEP). In severe heterogeneous obstruction, the inspiratory hold can be more representative of worst regional PEEPi.
  • Fix (in order): ↓RR (most effective), ↓VT, lengthen expiratory time, treat bronchospasm, match ~75–80% of total PEEP if patient is triggering.
  • Post-intubation collapse in an obstructive patient = stacked breaths until proven otherwise. Disconnect the circuit, let the chest decompress, resume slow bagging. The "S" of DOPES.
  • The bedside τ check: pause the flow trace at exhalation — it should reach zero before the next breath (≥ 3τ). If not, you're trapping gas.

18Triggering & patient-ventilator asynchrony

Triggering is the moment the ventilator detects patient effort and starts a breath. It is the single most clinically consequential interface between the patient and the machine — a bad trigger setup creates work of breathing, distress, sleep loss, P-SILI, prolonged liberation, and increased mortality. Asynchrony with an asynchrony index > 10% is independently associated with mortality.624

Patient–ventilator dyssynchrony explorer — live waveforms for the eight common patterns
Open full-screen ↗
Figure 18.1 — Patient–ventilator dyssynchrony explorer. Interactive overview of the eight common dyssynchrony patterns. Select a pattern to see live pressure and flow waveforms with annotated diagnostic features, the underlying mechanism, and corrective actions. For each pattern described in §18.3–§18.8 below, select the matching pattern in this simulator to see live waveforms.

18.1 The three triggering systems
SystemHow it worksSensitivity rangeTrade-off
Pressure triggerVent senses a drop in circuit pressure below PEEP from patient effort−0.5 to −2 cmH₂O (default ~ −1 to −2)Slowest (~ 50 ms latency); higher patient work; can be foiled by leaks
Flow triggerA small "bias flow" runs through the circuit; patient effort decreases that flow → vent triggers1–3 L/min (default ~ 2 L/min)Faster, lower work; sensitive to circuit motion / water; modern default
Neural trigger (NAVA)Diaphragm electromyogram (EMG) via a dedicated nasogastric catheter (Edi signal)Edi threshold 0.5 µV above baselineFastest, no pneumatic latency; physiologic; requires the EMG catheter and proprietary platform

18.2 Setting sensitivity at the bedside
  • Default flow trigger 1–3 L/min; pressure trigger 1–2 cmH₂O. Modern vents almost always default to flow.
  • Too sensitive (e.g., flow < 1 L/min, pressure < 0.5 cmH₂O) → auto-triggering (false breaths from circuit motion, water, cardiogenic oscillations).
  • Not sensitive enough (e.g., flow > 4–5 L/min, pressure > 3 cmH₂O) → ineffective triggering + ↑ work of breathing.
  • If switching from flow to pressure for diagnostic reasons (e.g., leak), expect higher work and slightly slower response.
  • Always check the trigger setting at every mode change and any time the patient seems to be "fighting the vent."
The trigger work of breathingThe patient must do the work to drop airway pressure (or bias flow) below threshold before the vent assists. In an obstructive patient with auto-PEEP, the diaphragm has to first overcome the auto-PEEP gradient, then reach the trigger threshold — sometimes adding 5–10 cmH₂O of inspiratory effort per breath. That's an enormous and entirely fixable load.

18.3 The five trigger-related abnormalities

Memorize these — they cover > 95% of dyssynchrony cases:

18.3.1 Ineffective triggering (wasted effort) — the most common

Patient effort generates a small dip in pressure or flow that does not cross the trigger threshold; no breath is delivered.

  • Mechanism: usually auto-PEEP — the patient must lower alveolar pressure below the set PEEP plus the auto-PEEP, then further to reach trigger threshold. May also be: insensitive trigger, weak respiratory drive (over-sedation), leak, or pressure-support set too high (over-assistance leads to apnea / late effort / wasted attempts).
  • Recognition: small notch in pressure-time scalar or upward deflection in flow-time scalar between vent breaths, with no delivered breath. The classic "missed effort."
  • Implications: dramatically ↑ work of breathing, sleep fragmentation, distress, prolonged liberation, mortality.
  • Management (in order):
    1. Reduce auto-PEEP (the highest-yield intervention): ↓ RR, ↓ VT, lengthen expiratory time (↑ peak flow in VC, or ↓ Ti in PC), treat bronchospasm, suction.
    2. Match extrinsic PEEP to ~ 75–80% of total PEEP (counterbalance) — reduces trigger work without increasing dynamic hyperinflation.
    3. Increase trigger sensitivity (lower threshold) — but only if ineffective trigger is from sensitivity, not from auto-PEEP.
    4. Lighten over-sedation if the patient is hypopneic.
    5. Reduce excessive PSV / VT if over-assistance is the cause.
18.3.2 Auto-triggering (false triggering)

Vent delivers a breath that the patient did not initiate.

  • Mechanism: water in tubing, large air leak (cuff or circuit), cardiogenic oscillations (high stroke volume in a thin-walled chest), motion artifact, oversensitive trigger.
  • Recognition: breath delivered while patient is apneic / paralyzed / disconnected; rate higher than expected; often regular cardiac-pattern triggering at HR.
  • Implications: respiratory alkalosis, sleep disruption, barotrauma risk, false weaning data.
  • Management: empty water traps; check ETT cuff and circuit for leak; raise trigger threshold (less sensitive); switch flow → pressure trigger if cardiogenic; stabilize the circuit.
18.3.3 Double triggering (breath stacking)

Two ventilator breaths back-to-back with no exhalation between, producing a single very large effective tidal volume.

  • Mechanism: the patient's neural inspiratory time exceeds the vent's set Ti (or set VT); when the vent cycles off, the patient's continuing effort triggers a second breath. Classic in awake ARDS on a 6-mL/kg low-VT setting; also in pain, anxiety, fever, light sedation.
  • Recognition: two pressure peaks back-to-back with no return to PEEP; volume curve shows stacked rise; alarms for high pressure may sound.
  • Implications: delivered VT can double — e.g., 12 mL/kg PBW in an ARDS patient — directly causing volutrauma and P-SILI. Strongly correlated with mortality.24
  • Management (escalating):
    1. Match the set Ti to the patient's neural Ti (e.g., extend from 0.7 → 1.0 s).
    2. If VT can be increased without violating Pplat ≤ 30 / ΔP ≤ 15, do so.
    3. Switch from VC to PC (decelerating flow more comfortable; pressure ceiling).
    4. Treat pain (fentanyl) and anxiety; deepen sedation to RASS −2 to −3.
    5. If severe ARDS with persistent stacking despite optimization → NMB (cisatracurium).
18.3.4 Reverse triggering (entrainment)

Diaphragm contracts after a passive ventilator breath; the respiratory center synchronizes ("entrains") to the machine rhythm. Increasingly recognized — found in > 50% of deeply sedated ARDS patients on continuous monitoring.

  • Mechanism: brainstem entrainment in deeply sedated but not paralyzed patients; the lung-stretch from the vent breath provokes a delayed reflex inspiration.
  • Recognition: small effort visible on flow or pressure scalar after the start of the machine breath (different from ineffective trigger, which is between breaths). Esophageal manometry confirms. May "drag" a second breath (reverse-triggered double-trigger), causing pendelluft (gas redistribution within the lung) and regional volutrauma.
  • Implications: P-SILI, asymmetric stress in heterogeneous ARDS lungs, complicates mechanics interpretation.
  • Management: often paradoxical — lightening sedation may eliminate it (so the patient breathes spontaneously rather than entrains); alternatively, deepening sedation with NMB. Evidence is evolving; treat case-by-case.
18.3.5 Trigger delay

Time between patient effort and vent delivery exceeds 100 ms (target < 50 ms).

  • Mechanism: insensitive trigger, high airway resistance, high circuit resistance, auto-PEEP, very low respiratory drive.
  • Recognition: visible offset on simultaneous diaphragm-electrical/airway-pressure tracings; patient distress and "out-of-phase" appearance.
  • Implications: ↑ inspiratory work, fatigue, weaning failure.
  • Management: switch pressure → flow trigger; lower trigger threshold; reduce auto-PEEP; suction; consider NAVA in refractory cases.

18.4 Flow asynchrony ("flow starvation")

The set ventilator flow rate is lower than the patient's desired flow rate. The patient generates additional negative inspiratory pressure to "pull" more flow — visible as concave deflections on the pressure-time scalar.

  • Recognition: concave ("scooped") inspiratory pressure trace; sometimes flow trace shows a peak below the patient's perceived demand.
  • Causes: inadequate set flow (most common in VC with square waveform), high respiratory drive (pain, dyspnea, fever, delirium), small ETT.
  • Fix: ↑ peak flow in VC (e.g., 60 → 80 L/min); switch square → decelerating flow; ↑ inspiratory pressure in PC/PSV; treat the underlying drive (analgesia, anxiolysis, treat fever).

18.5 Cycle dyssynchrony — premature cycling

The ventilator ends inspiration before the patient does. The airway pressure trace is pulled downward at end-inspiration, and reversal of expiratory flow occurs as the patient continues to inhale.

  • Causes: set Ti too short (PC, VC); cycle threshold set too high in PSV (e.g., 40% in a stiff lung that wants 15%); high expiratory time constant (COPD).
  • Risk: often progresses to double-triggering (the unsatisfied effort triggers a second breath).
  • Fix: ↑ Ti in PC; ↑ VT in VC; lower the expiratory cycling threshold in PSV (e.g., 25% → 10–15% in COPD).

18.6 Cycle dyssynchrony — delayed cycling

The ventilator stays in inspiration after the patient wants to exhale. The patient exhales against an inflating breath, producing a terminal pressure spike and increased expiratory flow when the vent finally cycles.

  • Causes: set Ti too long (PC, VC); cycle threshold set too low in PSV (e.g., 10% in a stiff lung wants 30–40%); long inspiratory time constant (ARDS).
  • Fix: ↓ Ti in PC; raise the expiratory cycling threshold in PSV (e.g., 25% → 35–40% in stiff ARDS lungs); switch from time-cycled to flow-cycled mode.
18.6a Tuning the cycling threshold — by phenotype

Both premature and delayed cycling have one common bedside lever in PSV mode: the expiratory cycling threshold, the percentage of peak inspiratory flow at which the ventilator decides the breath is over. The intuition is the opposite of what most learners guess at first:

  • Raising the cycle threshold (e.g., 25 → 40%) — vent terminates inspiration when flow has only dropped to 40% of peak. That happens earlier in the breath → shorter Ti.
  • Lowering the cycle threshold (e.g., 25 → 10%) — vent waits until flow is down to 10% of peak. That happens later → longer Ti.

Now match it to the time constant (§6.4):

Cycling-threshold tuning by phenotype
PhenotypeτFlow decay during PSVDefault 25% givesPatient's neural TiMismatchFix
COPD / asthma (high R) Long (1–2 s) Slow — flow takes a long time to drop to 25% Long vent Ti Short (dyspneic, wants to exhale soon) Delayed cycling: patient pushes against still-flowing inspiration ↑ threshold to 35–45% — terminate sooner
ARDS / restrictive (low C) Short (0.3–0.6 s) Fast — flow drops rapidly to 25% Short vent Ti Long (high drive, wants more volume) Premature cycling: patient continues to inhale after the vent has cycled ↓ threshold to 10–15% — extend Ti
Normal mechanics ~ 0.5–0.8 s Moderate Matched vent Ti Matched neural Ti Synchronous Leave at 25%

For PC mode, you set Ti directly (typical 0.7–1.2 s in adults). The benchmark is full pressure equilibration into the alveoli — about 3–5 τ. Two failure modes:

  • Ti too short (< 3τ) — alveolar pressure never reaches the set inspiratory pressure; delivered VT is smaller than predicted. Common in obstruction (long τ) when the operator picks a generic 0.8 s.
  • Ti too long (> 5τ) — equilibration was already complete; the extra time adds no volume but eats into expiratory time Te, raising the risk of auto-PEEP. Common when the operator confuses long Ti for "more lung recruitment."

Practical defaults: ARDS (short τ ≈ 0.4 s) → Ti ≈ 0.7–0.8 s gives full equilibration with room to spare. COPD/asthma (long τ ≈ 1.5 s) → 3τ would mean Ti 4.5 s, which is impossible without crushing Te. The clinical compromise is Ti ~ 0.8 s plus a slow rate (RR 8–12) and long Te (I:E 1:4 or 1:5) — accept partial equilibration on each breath and protect the exhalation window.

The flow-cycling rule of thumbIf the patient looks like they're "fighting the end of the breath" — pulling against ongoing flow, double-triggering, or appearing to start exhalation while the screen still shows inspiration — your cycling threshold is wrong. Long τ patient: raise the threshold. Short τ patient: lower it. The change is usually in 5% increments and the response is visible within a few breaths.

18.7 Late-cycle dyssynchrony — pressure control vs volume control

The pattern of late-cycle dyssynchrony differs by mode. In pressure control, the patient's neural inspiratory time can be longer than the vent's set Ti; pressure remains constant but the patient's effort continues. In volume control, the vent continues delivering volume even after the patient wants to exhale, producing pressure elevation as the patient pushes against the still-inflating breath.

18.8 Early-cycle dyssynchrony — detail by mode

Early termination of the vent breath while the patient is still inhaling. Expressed differently in three settings: VC with decelerating flow, VC with square (constant) flow, and PC.

18.9 The asynchrony index — quantifying the burden
$$\text{AI} \;=\; \frac{\text{asynchronous breaths}}{\text{total breaths}} \times 100\%$$
  • AI is calculated over a short observation window (typically 100 breaths or 15 minutes).
  • AI > 10% is the threshold associated with prolonged ventilation, longer ICU stay, and higher in-hospital and 1-year mortality.24
  • Ineffective triggering specifically: > 10 events/hour predicts weaning failure.6
  • Most ICUs do not measure AI routinely — but eyeballing the screen for > 10% wasted/double/late efforts during a 1-minute observation is a powerful clinical surrogate.
18.9.1 Worked example — calculating AI at the bedside in one minute

Scenario: 64M with COPD exacerbation on PSV 12/PEEP 8. You watch the pressure-time scalar for 60 seconds and record:

  • Total breaths attempted (counting both vent-delivered breaths and visible patient efforts that didn't trigger): 22
  • Vent-delivered breaths: 16 (pressure rises to PSV plateau)
  • Failed efforts (small pressure dips that don't reach trigger): 5 — ineffective triggering
  • Double-trigger events (back-to-back pressure peaks without exhalation): 1

Calculation:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Asynchronous breaths} &= 5\,(\text{ineffective}) + 1\,(\text{double}) = 6 \\ \text{Total breaths attempted} &= 22 \\ \text{AI} &= \frac{6}{22} \times 100 = \mathbf{27\%} \end{aligned}$$

Interpretation: AI 27% is well above the 10% mortality threshold. Ineffective triggering is the dominant pattern (5 of 6 events) — almost certainly auto-PEEP given COPD physiology. Confirm with end-expiratory pause; treat by lowering RR/VT, lengthening Te, and applying extrinsic PEEP at ~ 75–80% of measured auto-PEEP. Recheck AI in 15–30 min after each adjustment; the goal is < 10%.

The 60-second AI assessmentYou don't need a fancy monitor. Set a stopwatch, watch one minute of the pressure waveform, count vent breaths and add visible failed efforts and stacked breaths to the denominator. AI > 10% in any single minute predicts the same outcomes as the formal 100-breath / 15-min window in research studies.

18.10 Bedside management algorithm — putting it together
  1. Recognize — pause at the bedside and watch one minute of three waveforms (P-time, F-time, V-time). Eyeball asynchrony rate.
  2. Classify — is the issue at trigger, flow, or cycle? (Most asynchrony is trigger-related.)
  3. Diagnose the mechanism — auto-PEEP? Sedation level? Mode mismatch? Leak? Pain / fever? Underlying disease?
  4. Fix the patient first — analgesia, anxiolysis, treat underlying disease (bronchospasm, fever, agitation).
  5. Fix the ventilator — sensitivity, mode, VT, Ti, peak flow, PEEP, cycling threshold.
  6. Reassess after each change — never make multiple ventilator changes simultaneously.
  7. If refractory in severe ARDS — consider NMB (cisatracurium) or NAVA / PAV+ at experienced centers.
Three rules of thumb(1) The most common asynchrony is ineffective triggering from auto-PEEP — fix the auto-PEEP, not the trigger. (2) The most dangerous asynchrony is double-triggering in ARDS — fix it before the patient barotraumatizes themselves. (3) The most under-diagnosed asynchrony is reverse triggering — esophageal manometry confirms when the diagnosis matters.
Key takeaways
  • Triggering is the single most clinically consequential ventilator interface — bad trigger setup creates work, distress, sleep loss, P-SILI, and increased mortality.
  • Three systems: pressure trigger (~50 ms latency, foiled by leaks), flow trigger (~2 L/min, faster, modern default), neural trigger (NAVA, zero latency, requires EMG catheter).
  • Five major asynchrony types: ineffective triggering (auto-PEEP patient can't reach threshold), auto-triggering (circuit water/leak), double-triggering (patient effort exceeds vent cycle), flow starvation, reverse triggering.
  • The most common is ineffective triggering from auto-PEEP — fix the auto-PEEP, not the trigger sensitivity.

19POCUS in the ventilated patient

Point-of-care ultrasound has moved from "nice to have" to "expected" in the modern ICU. Six focused exams answer most ventilator-related bedside questions in under 10 minutes — diaphragm function, lung consolidation/edema, pneumothorax, pleural effusion, fluid responsiveness, and cardiac structure/function. Each is fast, learnable, and changes management more often than the test it replaces.

19.1 Lung ultrasound — the BLUE protocol & the four signs

The Bedside Lung Ultrasound in Emergency (BLUE) protocol gives a 90%+ accurate diagnosis of acute respiratory failure in 3 minutes by examining 6 zones (3 per hemithorax). Four findings carry most of the information:

The four lung ultrasound signs
SignWhat you seeMeans
A-linesHorizontal repeating reverberation lines below the pleura, equal spacingNormal aerated lung — air below pleura reflects ultrasound. (A-profile = bilateral A-lines = exclude pulmonary edema; suggests COPD/asthma exacerbation, PE, or PTX.)
B-linesVertical "comet-tail" hyperechoic lines from pleura to bottom of screen, move with respirationInterstitial syndrome. ≥ 3 B-lines per intercostal space = pathologic. Bilateral diffuse B-lines (B-profile) = cardiogenic edema or ARDS. Focal B-lines = pneumonia, contusion, atelectasis.
Lung slidingShimmering / sliding motion at the pleural line with respirationPleura is moving against chest wall — excludes pneumothorax at that spot. Loss of sliding (with A-lines) → suspect PTX.
Lung pointThe exact location where lung sliding stops as you move probe laterally100% specific for pneumothorax — marks the edge of collapsed lung against intact lung.
Pneumothorax workflowSliding present at any rib space → no PTX there. Sliding absent + A-lines → suspect PTX. Lung point confirms. M-mode "seashore sign" (sliding) vs "barcode/stratosphere sign" (no sliding) supports. Bedside US has > 95% sensitivity for PTX in supine patients vs CXR ~50%.

19.2 The BLUE-protocol algorithm for acute respiratory failure
BLUE-protocol diagnoses by lung profile
ProfileFindingsDiagnosis (90%+ accuracy)
A-profileBilateral A-lines + sliding+ DVT on lower-extremity scan → PE; otherwise → COPD/asthma exacerbation
B-profileBilateral diffuse B-lines + slidingCardiogenic pulmonary edema
A/B or B' profileAsymmetric B-lines, ± consolidationPneumonia / ARDS
C-profileAnterior consolidationPneumonia
A' profileA-lines + absent sliding (+ lung point)Pneumothorax

19.3 Diaphragm ultrasound — thickening fraction (TF) and excursion

Diaphragm ultrasound at the zone of apposition (mid-axillary line, 8th–10th intercostal space, B-mode) measures diaphragm thickness at end-expiration and end-inspiration:

$$\text{TF} \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{end-insp}} - T_{\text{end-exp}}}{T_{\text{end-exp}}} \times 100\%$$
  • TF > 30%: normal diaphragm function. Predicts SBT success and successful extubation.
  • TF 15–30%: borderline; weaning may be slow.
  • TF < 15%: diaphragm dysfunction — paralysis, severe weakness, or excessive ventilator support that has unloaded the diaphragm. Strongly predicts extubation failure.35

Diaphragm excursion (M-mode at subcostal view) is an alternative measure: > 1.0–1.5 cm during quiet breathing is normal; < 1.0 cm in spontaneous breathing predicts weaning failure. Excursion is easier to obtain but excursion-based criteria are less specific than TF for diaphragm function (because excursion is influenced by lung volume, position, and effort).

Why TF beats excursion in vented patientsExcursion can be entirely passive (the vent is doing the work). TF reflects active muscular contraction of the diaphragm itself — a direct measure of what the patient's pump can do.
Image pendingStatic B-mode ultrasound demonstrating diaphragm thickness measurement at end-expiration vs end-inspiration is in commission — see Radiopaedia (CC-BY-NC-SA), the ASE Critical Care Echo atlas, or your own anonymized clinical clips. The technical procedure: high-frequency linear probe (8–12 MHz) at the mid-axillary line, 8th–10th intercostal space; locate the diaphragm as the echogenic line sandwiched between the pleural and peritoneal lines; measure perpendicular thickness at end-expiration (Texp) and end-deep-inspiration (Tinsp); compute TF.

19.4 Pleural effusion quantification

Effusion appears as anechoic (black) collection at the dependent base, with the diaphragm and atelectatic lung visible above. Quantification methods:

  • Balik formula: estimated volume (mL) = inter-pleural distance (mm at end-expiration) × 20.
  • Effusion > 50 mm depth at the lung base = ~1000 mL — drainage usually high-yield for oxygenation.
  • "Plankton sign" (echogenic particles floating) suggests exudate or hemothorax; static anechoic suggests transudate.

Bedside drainage with US guidance is far safer than landmark-based; reduces PTX rate from ~6% to ~1%.

Image pendingStatic ultrasound image demonstrating the Balik measurement (supine patient, posterior axillary line, inter-pleural distance at end-expiration) is in commission. Sources to consider: Radiopaedia, the original Balik 2006 paper (Intensive Care Med), or your own anonymized clinical clips. The probe convention: curvilinear or phased-array probe in long axis between two ribs at the dependent posterior axillary line; identify diaphragm, lung above, and effusion as anechoic black space between; measure maximum perpendicular distance between visceral and parietal pleura at end-expiration.

19.5 IVC ultrasound for fluid responsiveness — caveats

Inferior vena cava (IVC) measured 2 cm distal to the cavoatrial junction in subxiphoid view, M-mode through the long axis:

  • Spontaneously breathing: IVC inspiratory collapse > 50% suggests fluid responsiveness; < 25% suggests not.
  • Mechanically ventilated, passive: "distensibility index" — IVCmax − IVCmin / IVCmin × 100. > 18% suggests fluid-responsive.
  • Caveats: any spontaneous effort, arrhythmia, RV failure, abdominal hypertension, or open chest invalidates these. False positives in pulmonary HTN are common.
The IVC fallacyIVC variability has poor positive predictive value when used in isolation. Pair with passive leg raise (PLR) test — a more reliable bedside maneuver: lift legs 45° for 1 minute and look for ≥ 10% rise in stroke volume / cardiac output (echo or pulse contour). PLR works regardless of vent mode or rhythm.

19.6 Focused cardiac ultrasound — the four views every intensivist should know
  • Parasternal long axis (PLAX): LV size and gross function, septal motion, pericardial effusion, mitral/aortic valves.
  • Parasternal short axis (PSAX) at papillary level: LV global function, RV size relative to LV, septal flattening (RV pressure overload — "D-sign").
  • Apical four-chamber (A4C): RV/LV size and function, McConnell's sign (RV free-wall hypokinesis with apical sparing — classic for acute PE), tricuspid regurgitation jet for PASP.
  • Subxiphoid: pericardial effusion, IVC, tamponade physiology.

Five questions cardiac POCUS answers in < 5 minutes:

  1. Is the LV severely depressed? → consider inotrope, treat as cardiogenic shock.
  2. Is the RV dilated/dysfunctional? → think PE, RV-protective vent, avoid fluids.
  3. Is there a pericardial effusion or tamponade? → drain.
  4. Is the IVC plethoric and non-collapsible? → not fluid-responsive.
  5. Is there severe valvular disease? → context for shock or edema.

19.7 The "vent-POCUS bundle" — what to scan when
POCUS triggers in the vented patient
Clinical questionScanWhat changes
New hypoxiaLung US (PTX, B-lines, consolidation, effusion)Differentiates ARDS from edema from PTX from atelectasis at the bedside in 3 min
New hypotensionCardiac (LV/RV/pericardial) + IVC + lung USRUSH/EFAST framework — distinguishes obstructive vs cardiogenic vs distributive vs hypovolemic
Pre-fluid bolusIVC + PLR + lung USConfirms responsiveness AND tolerance (B-lines pre-bolus = high risk of pulm edema)
SBT failureDiaphragm TF + B-line score + cardiac LVDiagnoses pump failure vs lung overload vs cardiogenic SBT failure
Pre-extubationDiaphragm TF + cuff leak + lung USTF < 15% predicts re-intubation; B-lines suggest occult edema needing diuresis first
Refractory ARDSCardiac (RV size) + lung distributionRV strain mandates RV-protective adjustments; focal vs diffuse pattern guides PEEP strategy

19.8 Bedside takeaways
  • POCUS is faster, more sensitive, and more specific than CXR for PTX, pleural effusion, and B-line edema.
  • Diaphragm TF predicts extubation success better than RSBI alone.
  • IVC alone overpromises and underdelivers — pair with PLR or stroke-volume response.
  • Five focused exams (lung, diaphragm, cardiac, IVC, abdominal-pelvic) cover > 90% of ICU bedside questions and should be re-checked daily on rounds for unstable patients.
  • Image-acquisition fluency requires > 50 supervised exams per modality. Free training: ASE, ACEP, CHEST, EFOMI/EFSUMB courses.
The POCUS rule of threeIf you're going to make a major management change (intubate, paralyze, push pressors, give 2 L), scan first. Three views, three minutes, three potential diagnoses changed.

19.9 Fluid responsiveness — the framework

The question "does this patient need fluid?" hinges on where they sit on the Frank-Starling curve. Static indicators (CVP, PAWP, LVEDP) tell you filling pressure, not preload. A patient with CVP 15 may be on the flat part of the curve — additional fluid will raise pressure but not increase cardiac output. Dynamic indicators ask the right question instead: "Will the next bolus increase stroke volume?"

The physiology: positive-pressure breaths cyclically reduce preload (intrathoracic pressure squeezes the great veins, reducing LV end-diastolic volume). On the steep part of the Starling curve, stroke volume oscillates with the cycle. On the flat part, it doesn't. Dynamic tests exploit this.

One sentence: Static filling pressures are dead. Ask instead: "Does the ventilator breath make stroke volume dance?" If yes — fluid responsive.

19.10 Pulse pressure variation (PPV) & stroke volume variation (SVV)

Physiology. A positive-pressure breath ↓ preload → ↓ SV → ↓ pulse pressure. The amplitude of cyclic pulse-pressure swing across a breath, normalized to mean pulse pressure, is PPV.

$$\text{PPV} \;=\; \frac{PP_{\max} - PP_{\min}}{(PP_{\max} + PP_{\min})/2} \times 100\%$$
$$\text{SVV} \;=\; \frac{SV_{\max} - SV_{\min}}{SV_{\text{mean}}} \times 100\%$$

Threshold. PPV > 13% (or SVV > 10–12%) predicts fluid responsiveness with sens/spec ~ 85% — the workhorse dynamic test in the controlled-mode ICU patient.

Critical caveats — PPV/SVV is unreliable when:

  • Spontaneous effort. Patient-triggered breaths replace machine-driven cyclic preload swings.
  • Arrhythmia (especially Afib). Beat-to-beat variability swamps the ventilator-induced signal.
  • Tidal volume < 8 mL/kg PBW. Cyclic pressure swing too small to generate measurable PPV.
  • RV failure. RV preload-limited; cyclic SV change is dampened.
  • Open chest, intra-abdominal hypertension, low chest-wall compliance. Pleural pressure transmission unpredictable.

Tidal-volume challenge. If VT is the limiting factor, briefly increase from 6 → 8 mL/kg PBW for 1 minute. If PPV rises ≥ 3.5%, the patient is genuinely responsive.

Common mistakeTrusting PPV > 13% in a patient breathing spontaneously, in fast Afib, or on VT 5 mL/kg. The test is valid only under controlled-mode, regular-rhythm, VT ≥ 8 conditions.

19.11 Passive leg raise (PLR) — the universal bedside test

Mechanism. PLR is a reversible autotransfusion: lifting the legs from supine displaces ~ 250–300 mL of venous blood centrally, transiently raising preload without committing fluid.

Method. Start semi-recumbent at 45°; lower torso to flat; lift both legs to 45° for exactly 1 minute (no longer). Measure SV or CO immediately before and during the maneuver.

Measurement. Echo LVOT VTI, arterial pulse-contour analysis, or non-invasive CO monitor. Threshold: SV/CO ↑ ≥ 10% during PLR = fluid responsive.

The test that always worksPLR is independent of ventilation mode, rhythm, and tidal volume because it doesn't rely on ventilator mechanics — it relies on physiology. Spontaneous breathing? Works. Afib? Works. VT 5 mL/kg? Still works.

Limitations. Significant leg edema, recent vascular surgery, orthopedic fixation, severe agitation. Otherwise feasible in nearly every ventilated patient.

19.12 End-expiratory occlusion test (EEO)

Hold expiration for 15 seconds → no positive-pressure breaths → cyclic preload reduction abolished → preload increases acutely. Threshold: SV/CO ↑ ≥ 5% = fluid responsive.

Method. Measure baseline SV/CO; occlude expiratory port (or hit "expiratory hold") for 15 s; release and measure again immediately.

Use when: PLR contraindicated; quick test on a stable patient with reasonable oxygenation reserve. Avoid when: marginal SpO₂ or high work of breathing — 15 s without a breath may not be tolerated.

19.13 Mini-fluid challenge

If PPV/SVV, PLR, and EEO are all inconclusive or unavailable: 100 mL crystalloid IV over 1 min, recheck SV/CO at 5 min. Rise ≥ 6% = responsive. Smaller and safer than the traditional 500 mL bolus while still informative.

19.14 Putting it together — fluid responsiveness algorithm
  1. Conditions right for PPV/SVV? Regular rhythm, VT ≥ 8, no spontaneous effort, no RV failure or IAH.
    • Yes → PPV (cutoff 13%) or SVV (cutoff 12%).
    • No → step 2.
  2. PLR feasible? Legs available, no vascular/orthopedic contraindication, SV/CO measurement available.
    • Yes → PLR (cutoff +10% SV/CO).
    • No → step 3.
  3. EEO tolerable? Patient stable enough for 15-s breath hold.
    • Yes → EEO (cutoff +5% SV/CO).
    • No → step 4.
  4. Mini-bolus 100 mL over 1 min; reassess at 5 min (cutoff +6%).

Once you've identified responsiveness, then decide volume. A 250–500 mL bolus is a reasonable starting point; reassess at 30 min before giving more.

Key takeaways
  • BLUE protocol: A-lines (normal aerated lung), B-lines (≥3 per space = interstitial syndrome), lung sliding (present = exclude PTX), lung point (100% specific for PTX).
  • Diaphragm thickening fraction (TF) > 30% predicts SBT and extubation success; < 15% strongly predicts failure; more specific than excursion alone.
  • IVC variability has poor positive predictive value in isolation — pair with passive leg raise (PLR) for reliable fluid-responsiveness assessment.
  • Focused cardiac POCUS (PLAX, PSAX, A4C, subxiphoid) answers five critical questions about LV, RV, pericardium, and tamponade within 5 minutes.
  • Static filling pressures (CVP, PAWP) do not predict fluid responsiveness — abandon them as primary tools.
  • PPV > 13% / SVV > 10–12% predict responsiveness only under strict conditions: controlled mode, regular rhythm, VT ≥ 8 mL/kg PBW, no spontaneous effort.
  • PLR is the universal test — works regardless of mode, rhythm, or VT because it relies on physiology, not mechanics.
  • Algorithm: PPV/SVV (if conditions met) → PLR → EEO → mini-bolus.
Part IV

Disease-specific strategies

The same vent in a different patient becomes a different tool. Eight archetypes cover > 95% of ICU work.

20ARDS

ARDS is the prototypical critical-care vent challenge. Mortality has been driven down by lung-protective ventilation, prone positioning, conservative fluid balance, and avoiding ventilator-induced injury — not by any specific pharmacology. The strategy is mechanical, and the strategy is the patient's outcome.

20.1 Definition & phenotypes

Berlin criteria (2012): acute (< 1 wk) bilateral opacities not fully explained by cardiac failure, with hypoxemia on PEEP ≥ 5: mild (P/F 200–300), moderate (100–200), severe (< 100).7

Global Definition of ARDS (2023): expands to include patients on HFNC ≥ 30 L/min and uses SpO₂/FiO₂ ratio when blood gas not available; recognizes ARDS in resource-limited settings.1

Pathophysiology — diffuse alveolar damage (DAD). Three sequential phases: exudative (0–7 d, capillary leak + neutrophil influx + hyaline membranes), proliferative (7–21 d, type-II pneumocyte hyperplasia, fibroblast activation), fibrotic (after ~3 wk, collagen deposition; the cause of long-term restrictive deficit).30 The exudative phase explains the shunt physiology and edema; the proliferative phase explains why the lung becomes harder to recruit; the fibrotic phase explains chronic morbidity.

ARDS phenotypes that change vent strategy
PhenotypeCXR/CTRecruitabilityPEEP strategy
Focal / lobar (e.g., pneumonia, aspiration)Lobar consolidation, asymmetricLow — recruiting maneuvers can overdistend healthy lungLower PEEP (8–12); prone early; consider ECMO for shunt
Diffuse / non-focal (e.g., sepsis, pancreatitis, transfusion)Bilateral homogeneous ground-glassHigh — most respond to PEEP escalationHigher PEEP (12–18) per ALVEOLI/LOVS; recruitment maneuvers reasonable
Hyper-inflammatory subphenotype (latent class analysis [LCA] cluster)VariableVariableHigher mortality; some signal for higher PEEP and conservative fluids
Hypo-inflammatory subphenotypeVariableVariableLower mortality; standard ARDSnet
Why phenotype mattersThe same FiO₂ × PEEP ladder applied to a focal-pneumonia ARDS patient as to a diffuse-sepsis ARDS patient overdistends the healthy lung in the first and underdistends the recruitable lung in the second. Always pair the ladder with the bedside CT or CXR pattern and the response to a single PEEP step (ΔSpO₂, ΔPplat, ΔCstat).33

20.2 Mechanical-ventilation bundle
  1. VT 4–6 mL/kg PBW; lower if Pplat > 30. The ARMA trial (ARDS Network low-tidal-volume study) showed 22% relative mortality reduction.8
  2. Pplat ≤ 30 cmH₂O; driving pressure ≤ 15 cmH₂O.5
  3. PEEP per FiO₂ ladder (low- vs high-PEEP table — see below). High-PEEP strategy benefits moderate-severe ARDS (P/F < 200) per the LOV-eD (Lung Open Ventilation – early Decremental) subgroup analyses.9
  4. Conservative oxygenation: SpO₂ 88–95%; PaO₂ 55–80.
  5. Permissive hypercapnia: pH ≥ 7.20 acceptable; ↑ RR, then accept.
  6. Prone position ≥ 16 h/day for P/F < 150 (PROSEVA trial).10
  7. Neuromuscular blockade (NMB) only if persistent ventilator dyssynchrony / high driving pressure despite sedation; routine NMB no longer recommended (the ROSE trial was negative).11
  8. Conservative fluid strategy (FACTT — Fluid and Catheter Treatment Trial) — even fluid balance once shock resolved.12
  9. Veno-venous extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (VV-ECMO) for refractory hypoxemia (P/F < 80 with optimized vent + prone) at experienced centers (EOLIA trial / post-hoc Bayesian re-analysis).13

20.3 PEEP / FiO₂ tables
Low-PEEP / high-FiO₂ ladder— ARMA, ARDSNet8
StepFiO₂PEEP (cmH₂O)
10.35
20.45
30.48
40.58
50.510
60.610
70.710
80.712
90.714
100.814
110.914
120.916
130.918
141.018–24
Default day-1 strategy. Useful when the patient is recruitable but you do not have evidence of severe ARDS.
High-PEEP / low-FiO₂ ladder— ALVEOLI / LOV-eD9
StepFiO₂PEEP (cmH₂O)
10.35
20.38
30.310
40.312
50.314
60.414
70.416
80.516
90.518
100.5–0.820
110.822
120.922
131.022
141.022–24
Use in moderate-to-severe ARDS (P/F < 200) and clearly recruitable phenotype — meta-analyses show benefit only in this subgroup.
Figure 20.1. The two practical PEEP / FiO₂ ladders used in adult ARDS. Each row is one step. Read across: at each step, the FiO₂ (amber) and PEEP (blue) are paired together. Pick the step that matches the patient's current FiO₂; advance down the ladder (more support) until SpO₂ is 88–95% and the patient tolerates the step; back up the ladder when oxygenation improves and you can reduce. After every step, re-check Pplat (≤ 30) and driving pressure (≤ 15) — a higher PEEP that worsens these numbers indicates over-recruitment, not adequate support. Step combinations shown are the original ARMA / ALVEOLI / LOV-eD protocols; they are guides, not prescriptions, and should be adapted to the individual patient using best-compliance, esophageal-guided, or response-based titration when available.
P-SILIPatient self-inflicted lung injury — large spontaneous tidal volumes / pendelluft / reverse triggering can mimic the vent's barotrauma and progress mild ARDS to moderate-severe. Recognize on the pressure scalar and esophageal manometry; sedate, paralyze, or lower support as appropriate.14

20.4 The Owens-style ARDS rescue ladder

When P/F continues to fall on optimized lung-protective ventilation, escalate in this order. Each step is a discrete decision; do not skip and do not stack everything at once — you'll lose the ability to attribute response.27

Stepwise rescue when standard ARDSnet is failing
StepThreshold to considerActionWhat success looks like
1. Re-confirm the basicsAny worseningVerify Pplat, ΔP, PEEP, VT 4–6 mL/kg PBW, VE matched to needs; rule out pneumothorax / mucous plug / circuit leak / migrated ETTOften "rescue" is detection of a fixable mechanical problem
2. Optimize sedation, treat dys-synchronyVisible asynchrony, P-SILI riskDeepen sedation; if persistent, NMB bolus to assessLower ΔP, smoother waveforms
3. PEEP titrationP/F 100–200, recruitable phenotypeHigh-PEEP table; or decremental PEEP trial; or esophageal manometry-guided (§29)↑ P/F > 20 mmHg without ↑ ΔP
4. Prone positioningP/F < 150 on FiO₂ ≥ 0.6 and PEEP ≥ 5≥ 16 h/day per PROSEVA10; continue ≥ 4 days or until P/F > 150 supineP/F up by ≥ 20 mmHg in 1–4 h; "responder" rate ~70–80%
5. NMBPersistent dys-synchrony or ΔP > 15 despite sedationCisatracurium 15-mg bolus then 37.5 mg/h × 48 h, OR rocuronium / vecuronium per institution; daily TOF monitoring; routine NMB no longer recommended (ROSE)11Lower ΔP, smoother waveforms, ↑ P/F
6. Inhaled pulmonary vasodilatorP/F < 100 with refractory hypoxemia, or RV dysfunctionInhaled nitric oxide (iNO) 5–40 ppm (typical start 20 ppm), or inhaled epoprostenol 10–50 ng/kg/min (typical 30–50); titrate to response↑ P/F (~20 mmHg), bridge to definitive therapy; no mortality benefit as monotherapy
7. ECMO consultP/F < 80 despite optimized vent + prone, or pH < 7.20 with PaCO₂ > 60 despite RR 35 and Pplat at limitVV-ECMO at experienced center per EOLIA criteria13; transfer early — outcomes worse with delayAllows ultra-protective vent (VT 3–4 mL/kg) while lung heals
Owens' single best move"If you've moved a step on the ladder and 4 hours later the patient hasn't responded, undo it before adding the next step." Stacking interventions makes it impossible to know what's helping and what's hurting. The ladder is not a list to add together — it's a sequence with re-evaluation between rungs.27

20.5 Prone positioning — the protocol
  1. Pre-prone: hold tube feeds 1 h prior, secure ETT and lines, eye protection, padding (forehead, chin, shoulders, knees, dorsal feet), 5-person team minimum.
  2. Maneuver: sheet-roll technique; rotate 180°; position arms in "swimmer's" pose; reposition every 2 h to prevent pressure injury.
  3. Vent adjustments: often PEEP can be reduced 2–4 cmH₂O after first 1–2 h as oxygenation improves; recheck Pplat and ΔP at 30 min, 1 h, 4 h.
  4. Duration: 16+ h/day (PROSEVA used 17 ± 3 h); supinate AM for procedures, lines, CT, then re-prone.
  5. Stopping criteria: P/F > 150 supine on PEEP ≤ 10 and FiO₂ ≤ 0.6 sustained ≥ 4 h; OR no response after 4 sessions; OR life-threatening complication (extubation, arrest in prone).
The proning catastropheThe two emergencies during prone are unplanned extubation and cardiac arrest. Pre-position a "supinate immediately" plan with the team; mark the bed-rail emergency role. Have a pre-prepared replacement ETT and difficult-airway cart at bedside.

20.6 NMB in ARDS — practical use
  • Cisatracurium (Hofmann elimination, no organ dependence) is the historic first-line: 15–20 mg bolus, then 37.5–75 mg/h infusion. Reassess at 24 h, daily after.
  • Rocuronium (cheaper, hepatic clearance) acceptable; less ideal in liver failure.
  • Monitor with train-of-four (TOF) at the orbicularis oculi or adductor pollicis; target 1–2 of 4 twitches.
  • Adjuncts always: deep sedation (RASS −5), eye care (lubrication + tape), DVT prophylaxis, daily reassessment for discontinuation.
  • Risks: ICU-acquired weakness (myopathy especially with concurrent corticosteroids), nerve injury from positioning.

20.7 Inhaled pulmonary vasodilators
  • iNO 5–40 ppm (typical start 20 ppm; titrate to oxygenation response then wean by 5 ppm steps) — selective pulmonary vasodilator improves V/Q matching by preferentially dilating vessels in ventilated lung regions. Methemoglobinemia (monitor MetHb if > 40 ppm or prolonged use) & rebound pulmonary HTN on abrupt withdrawal are the major risks. Cost is significant.
  • Inhaled epoprostenol 10–50 ng/kg/min (typical start 30–50; titrate q1h) — similar physiology, much cheaper, no methemoglobinemia. Sticky on circuits; change vent circuit q24 h.
  • Decision: start one if P/F < 100 refractory; titrate to oxygenation; wean within 48–72 h to avoid rebound. Document failure to respond and de-escalate.
  • Caveat: no mortality benefit in pooled trials; bridge therapy only.
