Proning of awake, non-intubated, hypoxemic patients gained widespread use in the COVID era. Modest evidence that it delays/avoids intubation in cooperative patients with isolated hypoxemic respiratory failure. Practical: have patients prone for 1–2 h cycles ≥ 8 h/day; effective in ~50% who can tolerate it. Less effective if work of breathing is high.

32.8 Common complications
  • Pressure injury (face, chest, knees) — mitigated by padding and head repositioning
  • Brachial plexus injury — careful arm positioning ("swimmer's pose")
  • Endotracheal tube migration / unplanned extubation — re-secure, check depth pre/post
  • Vascular line dislodgement — secure at multiple points
  • Facial edema — usually transient; subsides hours after returning supine
  • Conjunctival injury / corneal abrasions — taped closure + lubricant
The "16-hour failure" — a misnomerIf a patient fails to improve in the first prone session, don't abandon — the V/Q matching benefit may take 24–72 h to manifest. PROSEVA used 17 ± 3 h × 4–7 days; many responders are slow.
Key takeaways
  • Prone positioning in moderate-severe ARDS (P/F < 150): PROSEVA showed 28-day mortality 16% prone vs 33% supine (HR 0.39, p < 0.001) — one of the largest treatment effects ever demonstrated.
  • Mechanisms: improved V/Q matching (ventilation anterior, perfusion now to anterior too), recruitment of dorsal lung, mechanical homogenization (less focal overdistension), improved RV function (↓ PVR).
  • Pre-proning: secure ETT & lines, eye protection, padding (forehead, chin, shoulders, elbows, hips, knees, dorsal feet), deep sedation (RASS −5), 5-person team, pre-suction.
  • Stopping: P/F > 150 supine on PEEP ≤ 10 & FiO₂ ≤ 0.6 (≥ 4 h), or no response after 4 sessions, or life-threatening complication; awake proning in cooperative patients delays/avoids intubation ~50%.

33NMB and ECMO

Two adjunct therapies that share the same indication — refractory ARDS — and the same risk-benefit equation: meaningful gain when used at the right severity and timing, harm when overused or delayed.

33.1 Neuromuscular blockade in ARDS

Mechanism of benefit: eliminates patient-vent dyssynchrony (especially double-triggering and reverse triggering); ensures lung-protective tidal volumes; reduces oxygen consumption (paralysis ↓ VO₂ by 10–15%); may reduce P-SILI.

The trial trajectory:

  • ACURASYS (2010): 340 severe ARDS patients (P/F < 150). Cisatracurium 15-mg bolus + 37.5 mg/h × 48 h vs sedation alone. 90-day mortality 32% vs 41% (HR 0.68, p = 0.04). Established early NMB in severe ARDS.18
  • ROSE (2019): 1006 moderate-severe ARDS patients. Continuous NMB × 48 h with deep sedation vs lighter sedation without routine NMB. No mortality benefit. Stopped early for futility. Showed cardiac events more common in the NMB group.11

Current synthesis: NMB is not routine. Reserve for:

  • Refractory dyssynchrony despite optimized sedation
  • Persistent driving pressure > 15 despite VT/PEEP optimization
  • Severe ARDS while establishing prone/ECMO
  • Ventilator-induced lung injury concerns from large patient effort

33.2 NMB practical use (see also Ch 35)
  • Cisatracurium first-line: 15-mg bolus + 37.5 mg/h, max 48–72 h. Hofmann elimination (organ-independent).
  • Daily TOF at orbicularis oculi or adductor pollicis; target 1–2/4 twitches.
  • Always with deep sedation (RASS −5; verify no awareness), eye care, DVT prophylaxis, daily reassessment to discontinue.
  • Avoid concurrent steroids if possible — risk of ICU-acquired weakness rises sharply.

33.3 VV-ECMO — when to refer

EOLIA criteria (2018):13

  • P/F < 50 mmHg for > 3 h, OR
  • P/F < 80 mmHg for > 6 h, OR
  • pH < 7.25 with PaCO₂ ≥ 60 mmHg for > 6 h
  • Despite optimized vent (RR 35, Pplat ≤ 32, PEEP ≥ 10, NMB, prone trial)
  • Reversible cause; < 7 days of high-pressure ventilation; no major contraindications

Trial result: EOLIA was statistically neutral (frequentist, p = 0.09) but stopped early for crossover. Bayesian re-analysis (2018) suggested 96% posterior probability of mortality benefit. Current consensus: refer early when criteria approached.

33.4 ECMO physiology and the "ultra-protective vent"

VV-ECMO oxygenates and removes CO₂ extrapulmonarily — the lung can "rest." Settings during ECMO:

Typical vent settings on VV-ECMO
ParameterSetting
ModePC-A/C or VC-A/C with deep sedation ± NMB
VT3–4 mL/kg PBW (ultra-protective)
RR4–10 (CO₂ removal is via the membrane)
PEEP10–15 (maintain recruitment)
FiO₂0.30–0.50 (oxygenation is via the membrane)
Pplat target< 25
ΔP target< 12

Most patients spend 5–14 days on ECMO. Liberation: gradually reduce ECMO sweep gas while increasing native vent settings. Decannulation when patient tolerates near-normal vent settings off ECMO support for 4–6 hours.

33.5 ECMO contraindications
  • Absolute: irreversible underlying lung disease without transplant candidacy, severe brain injury (anoxic, hemorrhagic), advanced metastatic malignancy, multi-organ failure with poor prognosis
  • Relative: age > 70, BMI > 50 (institution-dependent), high-pressure ventilation > 7 days (worse outcomes), uncontrolled bleeding, immunocompromise
  • Always discuss with the family pre-cannulation — goals of care, expected duration, complication risks, decannulation timing

33.6 Major complications of ECMO
  • Bleeding (~30%): GI, intracranial, surgical sites. Anticoagulation balance is delicate.
  • Thrombosis: circuit clotting, line thrombosis, stroke (5–10%).
  • Cannulation injury: vascular damage, malposition.
  • Hemolysis: from circuit; monitor LDH, free Hb.
  • Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia: switch to bivalirudin if HIT confirmed.
  • Differential hypoxia ("North-South syndrome") — primarily VA-ECMO; the VV-ECMO version is less common.
  • Right-heart strain from cannula impingement on the IVC/RA.
Key takeaways
  • NMB in ARDS: ACURASYS (2010) showed benefit (cisatracurium 15 mg bolus + 37.5 mg/h × 48 h, 90-day mortality 32% vs 41%); ROSE (2019) showed no routine benefit, stopped for futility.
  • Current use: NMB is NOT routine — reserve for refractory dyssynchrony, persistent ΔP > 15, severe ARDS on prone/ECMO, P-SILI concerns; always with deep sedation (RASS −5), TOF monitoring, eye care, DVT prophylaxis.
  • VV-ECMO criteria: P/F < 50 for > 3 h, OR < 80 for > 6 h, OR pH < 7.25 with PaCO₂ ≥ 60 for > 6 h, despite optimized vent; reversible cause, < 7 days of high-pressure vent.
  • Settings on ECMO: ultra-protective vent (VT 3–4 mL/kg PBW, RR 4–10, PEEP 10–15, FiO₂ 0.30–0.50) — lung rests while oxygenation/CO₂ removal are via circuit.
Part VI

Procedural skills & pharmacology

The procedure that gets the patient on the vent and the drugs that keep them safe there.

34Intubation & airway management

Every patient on a vent was intubated at some point. The intubation itself is often the highest-risk procedure in their entire ICU course — peri-intubation hypotension, hypoxemia, and aspiration kill more patients than the disease that brought them in. The ventilator strategy that follows is downstream of how well you set up that single procedure.

34.1 Should I intubate? — the decision algorithm

Before any RSI checklist comes the question whether to intubate at all. Three questions, asked in order, cover virtually every adult acute respiratory failure case. The third is the one most often skipped.

Figure 34.1 — The intubation decision algorithm. Three questions in order. Q1 (airway protection) is a yes/no gate; "no" mandates intubation without a trial. Q2 (which physiology is failing) determines the first-line non-invasive option: HFNC for hypoxemic failure (FLORALI; ROX rule at 12 h), BiPAP for hypercapnic failure (Plant 2000; HACOR rule at 1 h), CPAP or BiPAP for cardiogenic pulmonary edema. Q3 (trajectory) is the most under-recognized — even when Q1 and Q2 favor a trial, a deteriorating patient gets the tube now rather than coding during the trial.
The trajectory question in one sentence"If this patient sits on NIV for the next 90 minutes, are they going to look the same, better, or worse?" If the honest answer is "worse," intubate now. Most preventable peri-intubation arrests happen because the team waited for a number to confirm what the trajectory had already told them.

34.2 The pre-intubation 7 P's

An adapted RSI checklist that every team should run before pushing drugs:

The 7 P's of intubation prep
PActionDetail
PreparationEquipment, drugs, monitoring, IV access, suction, BVM, EtCO₂Two functional IVs minimum. Suction set up and tested. Pre-drawn drugs labeled. Difficult-airway cart at bedside.
Preoxygenation3–5 min of high-FiO₂ breathingNRB at 15 L/min + nasal cannula at 15 L/min ("apneic oxygenation"); HFNC 60 L/min FiO₂ 1.0; or NIV (BiPAP 10/5, FiO₂ 1.0) if hypoxemic. Goal: SpO₂ > 95% before induction.
Pre-treatmentOptimize hemodynamics + treat reactive airways before induction500 mL crystalloid bolus if hypotensive; push-dose pressor (phenylephrine 50–200 mcg or epinephrine 5–20 mcg) ready; albuterol nebulized in asthma/COPD; lidocaine 1.5 mg/kg only for ↑ ICP (controversial).
Paralysis with inductionInduction agent + neuromuscular blocker, simultaneous pushSee Ch 34.2 for selection. Standard: ketamine 1.5–2 mg/kg + rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg.
Position & protectionSniffing position, ear-to-sternal-notch alignmentRamp obese patients with blankets. Cricoid pressure (Sellick) is no longer routinely recommended — controversial; release if view poor.
Placement & proofPass tube; confirm with EtCO₂ and bilateral breath soundsSustained capnography waveform = gold standard. CXR confirms depth (tip 2–4 cm above carina); auscultation alone is unreliable.
Post-intubation managementSedation infusion, ventilator settings, anticipate hypotensionSee §34.6. Do NOT leave patient with bolus sedation only — start propofol/fentanyl drips immediately.

34.3 Induction agent selection — match the agent to the physiology
RSI induction agents
AgentDoseOnset / DurationBest forAvoid in
Etomidate0.2–0.3 mg/kg IV (typical 0.3)30 s / 5–10 minHemodynamically unstable, head trauma (↓ ICP, neutral BP)Septic shock (transient adrenal suppression — though clinical impact debated); seizures
Ketamine1–2 mg/kg IV (typical 1.5–2; reduce to 0.5–1 in shock)30 s / 10–15 minSevere asthma (bronchodilation), shock (sympathomimetic ↑ HR/BP), trauma, awake intubationSevere HTN, recent MI; relative caution in head injury (older concern about ICP — now considered safe)
Propofol1.5–2.5 mg/kg IV (reduce 25–50% if hypotensive or elderly)30 s / 5–10 minHemodynamically stable, status epilepticus, ↑ ICP (↓ CMRO₂)Hypotension, severe LV failure (myocardial depression + vasodilation)
Midazolam0.1–0.3 mg/kg IV (typical 0.2)2–3 min / 30+ minRarely first-line for RSI; useful adjunctSlow onset; deep / prolonged sedation; hemodynamic instability
Neuromuscular blockers for RSI
AgentDoseOnset / DurationNotes
Rocuronium1.0–1.2 mg/kg IV (high-dose RSI; standard intubating dose 0.6–0.9 mg/kg)45–60 s / 45–70 minNon-depolarizing. Reversible with sugammadex 16 mg/kg. Preferred in most situations now (no contraindications like sux).
Succinylcholine1.0–1.5 mg/kg IV (typical 1.5)30–45 s / 5–10 minDepolarizing. Faster offset if airway fails. Contraindicated: hyperkalemia, burns > 24 h, crush injury, denervation, neuromuscular disease, malignant hyperthermia history. Causes K⁺ rise of ~0.5 mEq/L.
Vecuronium0.08–0.12 mg/kg IV (typical 0.1)2–3 min / 25–40 minSlower onset; rarely used for RSI today.
The "two-bag" ruleFor any unstable patient: have one bag of fluid running and one bag of pressor drawn before you push induction. Anticipate that BP will drop. Phenylephrine push-dose 50–200 mcg, epinephrine push-dose 5–20 mcg, or norepinephrine drip up.

34.4 The difficult airway — predict, plan, fail safely

Predictors (LEMON):

  • Look externally — facial trauma, beard, large tongue, short neck, dental disease
  • Evaluate 3-3-2 — 3 fingerbreadths mouth opening; 3 fingerbreadths chin to hyoid; 2 fingerbreadths hyoid to thyroid notch
  • Mallampati class III–IV (only soft palate / hard palate visible)
  • Obstruction / obesity — angioedema, mass, abscess, BMI > 35
  • Neck mobility limited — c-collar, ankylosing spondylitis, RA

Difficult-airway plan (have all three ready before any anticipated difficult intubation):

  1. Plan A: Direct or video laryngoscopy with optimal positioning + bougie
  2. Plan B: Supraglottic airway (laryngeal mask, second-generation supraglottic device with gastric channel) — temporary oxygenation/ventilation while regrouping
  3. Plan C: Awake fiberoptic intubation if predictable in advance
  4. Plan D ("can't intubate, can't oxygenate" — CICO): Surgical cricothyrotomy. Don't delay. If supraglottic device fails to oxygenate and patient is desaturating < 80% on max efforts, cut.
The "just one more attempt" trapEach failed laryngoscopy attempt causes airway trauma, edema, bleeding, and desaturation — making the next attempt harder. Three attempts maximum by the same operator before escalating (different operator, different blade, video, supraglottic, surgical). Multiple-attempt intubation is independently associated with worse outcomes.

34.5 Post-intubation hypotension — anticipate, don't react

Hypotension within 10 minutes of intubation occurs in ~30% of ED/ICU intubations and is associated with mortality. Three mechanisms — usually concurrent:

  1. Loss of sympathetic tone from induction agents (especially propofol) — vasoplegia, ↓ preload
  2. Loss of negative-pressure inspiration — switching from spontaneous (which augments venous return) to positive-pressure ventilation drops preload
  3. Underlying disease — sepsis, hypovolemia, RV failure (especially massive PE — see §25.4)

In an obstructive patient (severe asthma, COPD exacerbation), the post-intubation collapse pattern is specifically stacked breaths from over-bagging — see §17.3 for the "disconnect the circuit" rescue and the DOPES mnemonic.

Pre-intubation: shock index (HR/SBP) > 0.8 predicts post-intubation hypotension. Pre-treat with crystalloid bolus + push-dose pressor.

Post-intubation immediately: if BP drops, give 500 mL crystalloid bolus, push-dose pressor (phenylephrine 100 mcg or epinephrine 10 mcg), and start a vasopressor drip if persistent. Sedation choice matters — switch from propofol to a less-vasoplegic regimen (fentanyl + dexmedetomidine, or low-dose ketamine drip in shock).

34.6 Initial post-intubation ventilator orders — the 30-second cookbook
Default initial vent settings (general adult, no specific phenotype yet)
SettingDefault
ModeVC-A/C
VT6–8 mL/kg PBW (start 6 if any concern for ARDS)
RR12–16 (titrate to PaCO₂ 35–45 — or to patient's chronic baseline)
PEEP5
FiO₂1.0 initially → wean to SpO₂ 92–96% (88–92% in COPD)
SedationPropofol 25–50 mcg/kg/min titrated to RASS −2; fentanyl 25–100 mcg/h
AdjunctsHOB 30°, OG/NG tube, in-line suction, EtCO₂ continuous, ABG at 30 min, CXR for ETT position

Then within the first hour: re-assess Pplat, driving pressure, P/F ratio, hemodynamics. Adjust to phenotype (ARDS bundle, obstructive recipe, etc.). See Part IV chapters for disease-specific tuning.

34.7 Humidification & the ventilator circuit — HME vs heated humidifier

The upper airway normally heats and humidifies inspired gas to 37 °C and 100% relative humidity (44 mg H₂O/L) before it reaches the carina — the isothermic saturation boundary (ISB). An ETT bypasses this entire system, delivering cold, dry compressed gas directly to the lower airway. Without humidification, the mucociliary escalator slows within minutes, secretions thicken and tenacious within hours, mucus plugging accelerates, and tracheobronchitis follows. Humidification is mandatory on every vent — only the method varies.

Humidification options for the ventilated adult
FeatureHME (passive — heat-and-moisture exchanger)Heated humidifier (active)
MechanismCaptures heat & moisture on exhalation; returns it on the next breath. Hygroscopic media (paper, polyurethane, calcium chloride).Heated water reservoir + heated wire circuit; servo-controlled to deliver 37 °C, 100% RH at the Y-piece.
Output~25–32 mg H₂O/L (sub-physiologic)~33–44 mg H₂O/L (physiologic)
Dead space+ 30–95 mL apparatus dead space — meaningful in ARDS at low VT (a 50 mL HME on a 360 mL VT is 14% wasted ventilation)Minimal added dead space
Resistance+ 1–4 cmH₂O/L/sec — slightly raises work of breathing during PSV / SBTNone added
Bias flow / leakAcceptableAcceptable
Cost / complexityLow; single-use; change q24–48 h or sooner if soiledHigher; reusable circuit; needs distilled water; risk of circuit condensation pooling
Best forShort ventilation (< 7 days), most general ICU patients, transport, MRIARDS / low VT protocols (avoids dead-space penalty), neonatal / pediatric, prolonged ventilation, thick secretions, hypothermia
HME contraindications — switch to heated humidification
  • Hypothermia (HME cannot warm cold body gas; heated humidifier helps rewarm)
  • Thick / bloody secretions (HME clogs; reduces flow and ventilation)
  • Hemoptysis or pulmonary hemorrhage (HME blocked by clot)
  • Large air leaks (large BPF, cuff leak) (most exhaled gas bypasses the HME → no moisture capture)
  • Low minute ventilation (e.g., severe ARDS at VT 4 mL/kg, RR 20) — the HME's dead-space cost overwhelms its convenience
  • Continuous nebulization (HME filters out the aerosol; remove HME during nebulization regardless — see §44)
  • Very high tidal volumes > 1 L (exceeds HME capacity → poor humidification)
The dead-space cost is real in ARDSAn adult ARDSnet patient at 6 mL/kg PBW (e.g., 360 mL VT) on a typical 60 mL HME is wasting 17% of every breath on apparatus dead space — the V̇A drops, PaCO₂ rises, and RR climbs to compensate. Switch to heated humidification once an ARDSnet protocol begins; revisit the choice if you re-protocolize a patient onto low VT.
Bedside checks for adequate humidificationLook at the ETT inner lumen on suction passes — clear droplets visible = adequate; dry, crusty = inadequate. A thick mucus plug retrieved during routine suctioning is a humidification failure, not a patient problem.
Key takeaways
  • RSI checklist (7 P's): prep equipment, preoxygenation 3–5 min (apneic oxygenation if hypoxemic), pre-treat shock, paralysis + induction simultaneous, position & proof (capnography is gold-standard), post-intubation drips ready.
  • Ketamine + rocuronium is standard in shock; etomidate in borderline-hypotensive patients; propofol only if hemodynamically stable.
  • Difficult airway plan: have Plan A (video laryngoscopy + bougie), Plan B (supraglottic), Plan C (awake fibro if predictable), Plan D (surgical airway if CICO).
  • Three attempts maximum by one operator before escalating — each failed laryngoscopy causes airway edema and makes the next attempt harder.

35Sedation, analgesia & neuromuscular blockade — drug reference

The wrong sedation strategy doubles ventilator days, triples delirium, and worsens long-term cognitive outcomes. The right one — analgesia first, lightest sedation that achieves the goal, daily interruption — is one of the highest-leverage things you can do for a vented patient. Drug doses and titration are second to which patient gets what.

35.1 The ICU sedation philosophy — "analgosedation" + lightest possible
  • Analgesia first: most ICU agitation is pain. Treat pain (fentanyl bolus / drip) before reaching for sedation.
  • Target light sedation (RASS 0 to −2) unless specific indication for deeper. The ABC trial (Awakening + Breathing Coordination) and the SPICE-III trial confirmed: lighter is better for outcomes.21
  • Daily sedation interruption (SAT): pause sedation each morning. If the patient wakes up calmly → SBT. If agitated → restart at 50% prior dose.
  • Avoid benzodiazepines as routine sedation — strong association with delirium and prolonged ventilation. Use propofol or dexmedetomidine first-line.
  • Treat delirium nonpharmacologically first: orient, mobilize, reduce noise, restore sleep-wake cycle. Antipsychotics have weak evidence; quetiapine 25–100 mg at night is reasonable for hyperactive delirium.

35.2 Sedation goals and monitoring
RASS (Richmond Agitation–Sedation Scale)
ScoreTermDescription
+4CombativeOvertly combative, danger to staff
+3Very agitatedPulls at tubes/lines, aggressive
+2AgitatedFrequent non-purposeful movement
+1RestlessAnxious, movements not aggressive
0Alert and calmGoal for most patients
−1DrowsyAwakens to voice (eye-opening > 10 s)
−2Light sedationBriefly awakens to voice (eye-opening < 10 s) — typical light sedation goal
−3Moderate sedationMovement to voice but no eye contact
−4Deep sedationMovement to physical stimulation only
−5UnarousableNo response — only for refractory ARDS, status epilepticus, severe ICP

Pain assessment: Critical-Care Pain Observation Tool (CPOT) or Behavioral Pain Scale (BPS) for the unable-to-self-report. Score > 2 (CPOT) or > 5 (BPS) = uncontrolled pain.

Delirium screen: CAM-ICU once per shift. Positive = ↑ mortality, ↑ vent days, ↑ long-term cognitive impairment.

35.3 Sedative agents — comparison
Continuous-infusion sedatives
AgentDose rangeOnset / offsetMechanism / strengthsWatch for
Propofol5–80 mcg/kg/min (max 4 mg/kg/h sustained)30 s / 5–10 minGABA agonist; rapid titration; ↓ ICP; antiseizure; first-line for most ICU sedationHypotension, hypertriglyceridemia, propofol infusion syndrome (rhabdo + acidosis + cardiac collapse if > 5 mg/kg/h × 48 h), pancreatitis, lipid burden in nutrition calc (1.1 kcal/mL)
Dexmedetomidine0.2–1.5 mcg/kg/h (no bolus in ICU)15 min / 2–3 hα₂-agonist; "cooperative sedation" — patient stays arousable; analgesic-sparing; less delirium than benzos; preserves respiratory driveBradycardia, hypotension; rebound HTN if abruptly stopped after > 24 h; ineffective for deep sedation needs (max RASS achievable ≈ −2 to −3)
Midazolam0.02–0.1 mg/kg/h2–3 min / 30 min – hours (much longer if > 48 h)GABA agonist; only when other options fail; status epilepticus second-lineDelirium ↑, prolonged duration after discontinuation (active metabolites accumulate in renal failure), tachyphylaxis, withdrawal
Ketamine0.1–2 mg/kg/h infusion30 s / 10–15 minNMDA antagonist; analgesic + sedative + bronchodilator; preserves hemodynamics; useful in opioid-tolerant patients and severe asthmaHTN, tachycardia, secretions, emergence reactions (mitigate with low-dose benzo); hepatotoxicity rare with prolonged use

35.4 Analgesics
Continuous-infusion analgesics
AgentDose rangeNotes
Fentanyl25–250 mcg/h (bolus 25–100 mcg)First-line ICU opioid. Rapid onset, no histamine release, hemodynamically clean. Accumulates with prolonged infusion (lipophilic).
Hydromorphone0.5–3 mg/h (bolus 0.2–1 mg)Alternative; less accumulation in renal failure than morphine; avoid in liver failure.
Morphine1–10 mg/h (bolus 2–4 mg)Histamine release → hypotension; active metabolites accumulate in renal failure (morphine-6-glucuronide → prolonged sedation). Less favored.
Remifentanil0.025–0.2 mcg/kg/minUltra-short acting (esterase metabolism, no accumulation). Rapidly weans for SBT but expensive and rebound pain on stopping.
Acetaminophen650–1000 mg PO/PR/IV q6h (max 4 g/day)Underutilized adjunct. Reduces opioid needs by 20–30%.
Ketorolac15–30 mg IV q6h × max 5 daysNSAID; useful for procedural pain in otherwise healthy patients. Rarely the right choice in the acutely critically ill — most ICU patients have at least one absolute or relative contraindication: AKI risk in shock/hypovolemia, GI bleeding risk on stress-ulcer prophylaxis, platelet dysfunction, age > 65, concurrent anticoagulation, or cardiovascular disease. Reach for IV acetaminophen as the opioid-sparing adjunct of first choice in the ICU.

35.5 Neuromuscular blockers — extended use in the ICU
NMB infusions for ARDS / refractory dyssynchrony
AgentBolus / infusionMetabolismNotes
Cisatracurium0.15 mg/kg bolus → 0.5–10 mcg/kg/min (typical 1–3 mcg/kg/min)Hofmann elimination (organ-independent)First-line for prolonged ICU NMB. Does not accumulate in liver/renal failure. ACURASYS regimen: 15 mg bolus + 37.5 mg/h × 48 h.
Rocuronium0.6 mg/kg bolus → 8–12 mcg/kg/minHepatic; renal excretionCheaper. Reversible with sugammadex. Less ideal in liver failure.
Vecuronium0.1 mg/kg bolus → 0.8–1.7 mcg/kg/minHepatic + renalActive metabolite accumulates in renal failure. Less used now.

NMB monitoring (mandatory): train-of-four (TOF) at the orbicularis oculi or adductor pollicis q4h. Target 1–2 of 4 twitches (not 0/4 — total paralysis is associated with prolonged weakness). Hold infusion daily to assess depth.

NMB always with: deep sedation (RASS −5; verify no awareness), eye lubrication + tape, DVT prophylaxis, pressure-injury prevention, daily reassessment for discontinuation. ICU-acquired weakness rises sharply with concurrent corticosteroid use.

35.6 The ABCDEF bundle (PADIS guidelines synthesis)

The structured approach that ties pain/sedation/delirium/mobility together. Each letter is a daily action:

ABCDEF liberation bundle
LetterElementAction
AAssess, prevent, manage painCPOT q-shift; treat to CPOT ≤ 2
BBoth SAT and SBTDaily sedation interruption + spontaneous breathing trial when criteria met
CChoice of sedation/analgesiaLight sedation, analgosedation, avoid benzos
DDeliriumCAM-ICU q-shift; nonpharm interventions; avoid benzos
EEarly mobility & exerciseOut of bed by day 2 if hemodynamically stable
FFamily engagementFamily present at rounds, visiting flexibility

Synthesized from the PADIS guidelines (Pain, Agitation/sedation, Delirium, Immobility, Sleep — Devlin 2018, Crit Care Med).49 Implementation reduces ICU mortality, vent days, and long-term cognitive impairment.22

The single most impactful sedation moveIf your ICU still defaults to a benzodiazepine drip, switch to propofol or dex. The mortality and delirium benefit is larger than nearly any disease-specific therapy you'll order.
Key takeaways
  • Analgesia first (most ICU agitation is pain), then lightest sedation that achieves the goal (RASS 0 to −2), daily sedation interruption (SAT) — ABC trial and SPICE-III confirm lighter is better.
  • Avoid benzodiazepines as routine sedation (↑ delirium, ↑ vent days); propofol or dexmedetomidine first-line — dex preserves respiratory drive, is less deliriogenic, but max RASS ~ −2 to −3.
  • RASS scale: 0 = alert/calm (goal), −1 to −2 = light sedation (typical goal), −3 to −4 = moderate/deep, −5 = unarousable (only for refractory ARDS, seizures, ICP).
  • ABCDEF bundle: Assess pain, Both SAT & SBT, Choice (light sedation, analgosedation), Delirium (CAM-ICU), Early mobility, Family engagement — implementation ↓ mortality, vent days, long-term cognitive impairment.
Part VII

Liberation

Most patients leave the vent more by way of careful liberation than by elegant initial settings. Daily readiness, structured SBT, disciplined extubation criteria, and end-of-life withdrawal done well save ICU days and dignify the unsalvageable.

36Spontaneous breathing trials

Liberation hinges on a single daily test: can the patient breathe without vent support? Done well — clear readiness criteria, a structured trial, defined tolerance and failure thresholds — the SBT is the best predictor we have of successful extubation. Done sloppily, it churns ICU days, exhausts the patient, and feeds the cycle of reintubation.

Daily SBT readiness criteria
  • Underlying problem improving.
  • SpO₂ ≥ 90% on FiO₂ ≤ 0.4 and PEEP ≤ 5–8.
  • Hemodynamic stability without escalating vasopressors.
  • Adequate cough, manageable secretions.
  • Awake and follows commands (or progressing toward it; reasonable RASS −1 to 0).
  • Absence of active uncontrolled ICP / ischemia / arrhythmia.
Pair the SBT with a spontaneous awakening trial (SAT)The 2008 ABC trial showed that combining a daily SAT (sedation interruption) with the SBT shortened ventilator days by 3 and 1-year mortality by 14% versus SBT alone.21 Sequence: SAT first (turn off sedation if eligible — no active seizures, withdrawal, escalating shock, paralytics) → if patient remains comfortable, proceed to SBT. This is the foundation of the modern ICU liberation (ABCDEF) bundle.
How to perform
  • PSV trial — most common variant: PSV 5–8 cmH₂O over PEEP 5 × 30–120 min.
  • T-piece — most rigorous variant: PSV 0 + PEEP 0 (no support), usually 30 min.
  • Both are acceptable — choose by institutional preference and patient context (PSV gentler; T-piece more closely mimics post-extubation conditions).
  • Automatic tube compensation (ATC): machine offsets ETT resistance; alternative to low PSV.
Predictors of success
  • Rapid shallow breathing index (RSBI) < 105 (RR ÷ VT in liters) at 1 min into a T-piece — sensitivity 0.97, specificity 0.65 for success.19
  • Negative inspiratory force (NIF, also called maximum inspiratory pressure) more negative than −20 to −25 cmH₂O.
  • VT > 5 mL/kg PBW; RR < 35; pH stable; SpO₂ ≥ 90%.
  • No agitation, diaphoresis, accessory muscle use, hemodynamic deterioration.
Figure 36.1 — Daily liberation pathway. Five sequential gates: readiness screen → SAT → SBT → tolerance check → extubation gate. Each step prevents the most common errors at the next; failure at any gate routes back to current support with a 24-hour reassessment, not abandonment of the plan.
Nutrition affects weaning — overfeeding is a hidden cause of SBT failureThe PaCO₂ ∝ V̇CO₂ / V̇A equation (§4.1) cuts both ways: more CO₂ production from the metabolic side requires more ventilation to maintain the same PaCO₂. Overfeeding raises V̇CO₂ and the respiratory quotient (RQ), and can be the difference between a passed and a failed SBT.
  • RQ targets (indirect calorimetry if available): RQ < 0.85 = underfeeding; RQ 0.85–0.95 = mixed-substrate appropriate range; RQ > 1.0 = lipogenesis from carbohydrate overfeeding (every 4 g of glucose stored as fat releases ~ 1 g of CO₂). High-RQ patients fail SBTs they would otherwise pass.
  • Carbohydrate-heavy formulas raise V̇CO₂ more than fat- or protein-balanced ones; pulmonary-formula tube feeds (lower CHO, higher fat) are mostly historical, but reducing the carbohydrate fraction in difficult-to-wean patients can drop V̇CO₂ by 5–10%.
  • Refeeding syndrome after starvation or chronic critical illness drops phosphate, magnesium, and potassium — and the diaphragm is the first muscle to feel hypophosphatemia. Phosphate < 2.0 mg/dL is a common, missable cause of SBT failure. Replete before retrying the SBT.
  • Protein target in critical illness is 1.2–2.0 g/kg/day (higher than the non-ICU 0.8 g/kg recommendation) to limit catabolic muscle loss including the diaphragm. Protein malnutrition prolongs weaning more than caloric malnutrition does.
  • Total calories: most modern guidelines target 70–100% of measured energy expenditure (or 25 kcal/kg/d as estimate) — not aggressive overfeeding. The TARGET (2018) and EPaNIC (2011) trials found permissive underfeeding non-inferior or superior to aggressive feeding in the ICU.
Key takeaways
  • Daily readiness screen first; SBT only when criteria met. Skipping the screen = wasted SBT and demoralizing failures.
  • SBT settings: PSV 5–8 over PEEP 5 (most common variant) or PSV 0 + PEEP 0, T-piece (most rigorous); both are acceptable; 30–120 minutes.
  • Pass = RR < 35, RSBI < 105, VT > 4 mL/kg PBW, no distress, hemodynamics stable.
  • Pair the SBT with a SAT (sedation interruption) the same morning — the ABC approach reduces vent days and mortality.21
  • Reasons SBTs fail are usually fixable (cardiac, fluid, neuro, mechanical, metabolic — including overfeeding and hypophosphatemia) — diagnose the failure mode rather than just retrying.

37Extubation & tracheostomy decisions

37.1 The four extubation gates
  1. Mental status — follows commands; will protect airway.
  2. Cough — adequate, brisk, productive on demand. Voluntary "white card" cough strength predicts success.
  3. Secretions — manageable burden, infrequent suctioning.
  4. Cuff leak — present (≥ 110 mL air loss with cuff down for VC, or audible leak); no leak suggests airway edema and risk of post-extubation stridor — consider IV steroid (methylprednisolone 20 mg q4h × 4 doses prior, or 40 mg single dose 4 h prior).

37.2 Post-extubation respiratory support
  • High-risk (heart failure, COPD, age > 65, multiple co-morbidities, prolonged intubation): high-flow nasal cannula (HFNC) + intermittent NIV reduces re-intubation vs HFNC alone.20
  • Average risk: HFNC alone is non-inferior to facemask; preferred for comfort.

37.2a Endotracheal tube cuff management — pressures, leaks, herniation

The ETT cuff is the small print of vent management — under-pressurize and the patient aspirates and leaks; over-pressurize and you injure the trachea. Both errors are common.

Cuff pressure management
ItemTarget / approach
Cuff pressure target20–30 cmH₂O (most institutions). < 20 → aspiration risk + leak. > 30 → tracheal mucosal ischemia (capillary perfusion pressure ~ 25–30).
How to measureManual cuff manometer connected at the pilot balloon line. Check at least q-shift and after any ETT manipulation, repositioning, or new leak.
"Minimum-leak" techniqueInflate the cuff just until the audible leak at end-inspiration disappears, then add 1 cmH₂O. Avoids over-inflation but doesn't quantify pressure.
Continuous cuff-pressure devicesSome ICUs use closed-loop systems that maintain cuff pressure automatically; reduces VAP rates in some studies.
Subglottic suction tubesSpecialty ETTs with a port above the cuff for continuous suction of pooled secretions. Reduces VAP by ~ 50% in long-stay vented patients. Use in expected vent > 48–72 h.
Diagnosing a new air leak
  1. Audible cuff leak with normal cuff pressure: most likely cuff is herniated above the cords, ETT is too high, or cuff is damaged. Confirm depth (CXR or laryngoscopy); reposition or replace.
  2. Cuff requires more air than usual to seal: cuff valve damage, pilot balloon damage, or true rupture. Plan for ETT exchange.
  3. Sudden complete loss of cuff seal: tube migration up to vocal cords, cuff rupture, or accidental extubation. Evaluate immediately.
  4. VT delivered > VT exhaled: leak around cuff, in circuit, or in the lung (BPF, see §42.4).
  5. Persistent low cuff pressure despite re-inflation: pilot tube valve failure — temporize with hemostat clamp on pilot tube; plan ETT exchange.
Cuff complications
  • Tracheal mucosal injury / pressure necrosis — cuff pressure > 30 cmH₂O for hours; long-term cuff > 25. Maintain 20–30; rotate the cuff position with each ETT-tape change.
  • Tracheomalacia — late effect of prolonged cuff injury; cartilage softening leads to dynamic collapse on extubation. Requires tracheal stenting in severe cases.
  • Tracheo-esophageal fistula — the catastrophic late complication, usually with concurrent NG/OG tube creating posterior pressure. Suspect if new copious secretions or feeds in the ETT.
  • Tracheo-innominate fistula — also a late complication, more common with tracheostomy than ETT. Sentinel bleed → may be followed by exsanguinating hemorrhage. Overinflate cuff to tamponade and emergent OR.
The cuff-check on every roundsMake cuff pressure a routine vital sign — once per shift at minimum. The 30 seconds it takes to check prevents weeks of late tracheal complications.

37.3 Tracheostomy — indications, timing, technique

Indications:

  • Anticipated need for ventilation > 14 days
  • Failure to wean despite optimization
  • Secretion management burden (can't clear with suctioning)
  • Neuromuscular weakness (high cervical SCI, ALS, persistent diaphragmatic dysfunction)
  • Upper-airway obstruction / planned head-and-neck surgery (often emergent)
  • Comfort and reduced sedation needs in long-term ventilation

Timing: the TracMan trial (Tracheostomy Management in critical care) and meta-analyses show no mortality difference between early (≤ 4 days) and late (≥ 10 days) tracheostomy in critically ill adults. Early trach does reduce sedation needs and ventilator days but at the cost of operating on patients who would have been extubated. Practical default: re-evaluate at day 10–14; tracheostomy if vent dependence likely > 7 more days.

37.4 Tracheostomy techniques
Surgical vs percutaneous tracheostomy
FeatureSurgical (open)Percutaneous dilational (PDT)
LocationOR or bedsideBedside (ICU)
Time30–45 min15–25 min
Bronchoscopic guidanceNot requiredStandard (visualize tracheal puncture)
BleedingHigher early, lower lateLower early; risk of innominate artery erosion if too low
Stomal infection~10%~3%
Best forDifficult anatomy, obesity, abnormal landmarks, coagulopathy, prior neck surgeryStandard adults; cost-effective; no transport
CostHigher (OR time)Lower

Most ICU tracheostomies in current practice are bedside PDT in eligible patients. Contraindications to PDT: emergency airway, cervical spine instability, infected neck tissue, abnormal anatomy, severe coagulopathy, anticipated difficult airway.

37.5 Tracheostomy tube types
Tracheostomy tube features
FeatureUse
CuffedRequired for positive-pressure ventilation; reduces aspiration. Default in ICU.
UncuffedSpontaneously breathing chronic patient; allows speech and reduces tracheal mucosal pressure injury.
FenestratedHole in the cannula above the cuff allows airflow up through vocal cords for speech (with cuff down). Risk: granulation tissue at fenestration.
Adjustable-flange / extended-shaftFor obese / high-BMI patients where standard tube is too short.
Double-cannula (most adult trachs)Inner cannula removable for cleaning without changing the outer tube. Standard.
Single-cannulaPediatric or specialty; requires complete change for cleaning.

37.6 Tracheostomy weaning & decannulation

Sequential weaning:

  1. Wean ventilator settings as in standard SBT (PSV → trach mask).
  2. Trach collar (T-piece) trials: increasing periods of unassisted breathing.
  3. Cuff deflation: tolerate cuff down without aspiration. Pre-test with methylene blue swallow if concerned.
  4. Downsizing: smaller tube (e.g., 8 → 6) to facilitate airflow around the tube; precedes capping.
  5. Capping trial: occlude the tube while patient breathes around it through the upper airway. Confirms patent upper airway. Begin with brief periods, progress to 24–48 h sustained.
  6. Decannulation: after 24–48 h of cap tolerance with adequate cough and secretion clearance. Cover stoma with occlusive dressing; closes within 5–7 days.

37.7 Passy-Muir valve (speaking valve)

One-way valve that opens on inspiration (gas in via the trach), closes on expiration (gas out via the upper airway through the vocal cords) — restores phonation, swallow coordination, smell, and dignity. Requires:

  • Cuff fully deflated (no air leak around cuff to upper airway = no phonation)
  • Patient tolerates cuff down without aspiration
  • Adequate airflow around the tube (downsize if needed)
  • Awake / cooperative

Place the valve only after confirming cuff is fully deflated (a partially-inflated cuff with valve in place creates a closed system → catastrophic barotrauma in seconds). Have a "cuff-deflate-first" checklist on the trach algorithm.

37.8 Swallow evaluation in the trach patient

Aspiration risk persists after trach in ~50%. Bedside evaluation by speech-language pathology should occur before any oral intake. Modified barium swallow or fiberoptic endoscopic evaluation of swallowing (FEES) confirms safe swallow. Cuff status during swallow: fully deflated (allowing normal upper-airway flow); inflated cuff increases dysphagia by tethering the larynx.

37.9 Major tracheostomy complications
  • Early bleeding (< 48 h): venous oozing usually self-limited; arterial bleeding (innominate artery — late, 1–6 weeks) is catastrophic — manage by overinflating the cuff to tamponade and emergent OR transport.
  • Tracheo-esophageal fistula: late complication from cuff over-inflation or tube erosion. Suspect with new copious secretions or feeds appearing in the trach.
  • Tracheal stenosis (10–15% at 6 months): from cuff pressure or excessive granulation. Maintain cuff pressure 20–30 cmH₂O.
  • Stomal infection: typical wound care; rarely severe.
  • Accidental decannulation: especially < 7 days post-trach (immature tract, high re-cannulation risk). Have replacement trach + smaller spare at bedside; if cannot recannulate, oral intubation through the cords.
  • Tube blockage: mucous plug — first-line is removing the inner cannula and cleaning. If blocked at the level of the outer cannula: emergency tube change.
The "lost airway" emergency in a tracheostomy patientIf the patient is decompensating and the cause is unclear, follow the green-pathway algorithm (UK National Tracheostomy Safety Project): (1) call for help; (2) apply oxygen to face AND stoma; (3) check tube patency (suction, then remove inner cannula); (4) if still obstructed, deflate cuff (allows airflow around); (5) if still obstructed, remove the tracheostomy tube; (6) ventilate via face if upper airway intact, or via stoma. Mature tract (> 7 days) means stomal access is reliable; immature tract may close → require oral intubation.

37.10 Bronchoscopy through the ETT — settings, procedure, complications

Fiberoptic bronchoscopy (FOB) at the bedside is one of the most common procedures done on the ventilated patient. The trade-off: the scope occludes a substantial fraction of the ETT lumen, so resistance and airway pressures spike during scope passage. A few preparatory moves prevent most of the trouble.

Indications

  • BAL for VAP / PJP / unusual organisms in immunocompromised patients
  • Mucus-plug clearance / lobar atelectasis not resolving with suctioning
  • Post-extubation stridor or respiratory distress — visualize larynx and subglottis
  • Hemoptysis localization
  • Foreign-body retrieval
  • Suspected tracheal stenosis or late ETT/trach complications

Pre-procedure preparation

  1. Increase FiO₂ to 1.0 for 5 minutes — the scope displaces oxygen and desat happens fast.
  2. Sedation: target RASS −3 to −4. Bolus propofol 0.5–1 mg/kg or midazolam 1–2 mg IV right before.
  3. Brief NMB (rocuronium 0.6–1 mg/kg or succinylcholine 1–1.5 mg/kg) prevents coughing/bucking and reduces hypoxemia — strongly consider in difficult cases.
  4. Deep tracheal suction first — clear the secretions you would otherwise blindly push around.
  5. Document baseline HR, BP, SpO₂; have epinephrine and bronchodilator at bedside.

Equipment & sizing — what fits in what

Standard adult fiberscope OD is 5.7 mm. Match scope to ETT to avoid catastrophic occlusion:

Bronchoscope vs ETT sizing
ETT ID (mm)Standard scope (5.7 mm)Lumen filledResistance multiplierMode preference
8.0OK~50%~3×VC or PCV — minor PIP rise
7.5OK (snug)~64%~5×PCV preferred — VC will alarm on PIP
7.0Tight — use 4.9 mm pediatric scope if available~78%~10–15×PCV mandatory; reduce RR; raise PIP ceiling
6.0–6.5Pediatric scope only (4.9 mm OD)——Consider de-cuffing briefly for scope passage

Ventilator settings during the scope

By Poiseuille, halving lumen radius increases resistance ~16×. A 5.7 mm scope in a 7.5 ETT raises Raw 5-fold; in a 7.0 ETT, 10–15-fold. Switch from VC to PCV if you're not already there: pressure ceiling protects against barotrauma even as resistance spikes. Set PIP limit 5–10 cmH₂O above baseline; tolerate transient VT drop. Maintain PEEP. Reduce alarm sensitivities for the duration only.

BAL technique (typical for VAP)

  1. Advance scope through swivel adapter to main carina.
  2. Inspect anatomy: secretions, edema, blood, masses.
  3. Wedge in the targeted segmental bronchus (right lower lobe most common for VAP).
  4. Inject 50 mL warm sterile saline; immediately suction back into a sterile trap. Repeat × 3–4 (total lavage 150–200 mL). Discard the first aliquot if testing for VAP (proximal contamination).
  5. Withdraw; document any bleeding or unusual findings.
  6. Send: Gram stain, quantitative culture (≥ 10⁴ CFU/mL = positive), viral PCR; add fungal culture / silver stain / DFA / mycobacterial stains if immunocompromised.

Complications and how to prevent them

Bronchoscopy complications
ComplicationFrequencyPrevention / management
Hypoxemia (SpO₂ < 88%)~30%Pre-O₂; limit scope time < 5 min; abort and re-oxygenate if SpO₂ < 85% × 20 s
HypercapniaCommon, transientResume normal ventilation post-scope; check ABG if symptomatic
Transient hypotension5–10%Adequate sedation depth; small fluid bolus if needed
Bleeding (minor)< 1%Gentle technique; avoid biopsy unless indicated; epinephrine 1:10,000 down the channel for ooze
Pneumothorax< 0.1%Avoid aggressive distal wedging; post-procedure CXR if any concern; chest tube if tension
Bronchospasm1–3%Pre-treat asthma/COPD with albuterol; have nebulizer at bedside

Post-procedure

  1. Return ventilator immediately to pre-procedure mode/settings.
  2. Hold FiO₂ at 0.6–0.8 for the first 30–60 min — the lavaged segment is briefly atelectatic and recruits over time.
  3. Listen for new wheezing (bronchospasm), asymmetric breath sounds (PTX), or hemoptysis.
  4. Recruitment maneuver if SpO₂ is slow to recover and CXR/clinical picture suggests post-FOB atelectasis.
  5. CXR if any concern for pneumothorax or aspiration.
  6. Wean sedation back to baseline.
The post-bronch FiO₂ trapDecreasing FiO₂ too quickly after the scope. The lavaged segment is atelectatic and takes 30–60 min to fully re-aerate. Hold FiO₂ at 0.6–0.8 for an hour before titrating down — premature weaning triggers a desat that looks like the procedure went badly when it was just impatience.

37.11 The physiology of extubation failure — a heart-lung problem

About 10–15% of patients who pass an SBT and are extubated will need to be re-intubated within 48–72 hours. Extubation failure is independently associated with prolonged ICU stay and mortality. The causes are classically split into airway / mechanical and cardiopulmonary, but the latter is consistently underestimated.25

MechanismWhat happens at extubationClinical clues
Cardiogenic — "weaning-induced pulmonary edema"Sudden return to negative pleural pressure: ↑ venous return (preload bolus to RV and LV) and ↑ LV transmural pressure (afterload load). In a marginal LV this precipitates flash pulmonary edema within 1–4 hours.History of HF, ischemia, valvular disease; rising NT-proBNP / hemoconcentration during SBT; new B-lines on lung US; failure to extubate despite normal mechanics.
Respiratory pump failureIncreased work of breathing without ventilator support exceeds the patient's diaphragm reserve — pH drops, RR climbs, RSBI rises late.Diaphragm thickening fraction < 30% on US; RSBI > 105; prior ICU-acquired weakness.
Airway / upper-airwayPost-extubation stridor from glottic edema; aspiration; secretion burden exceeding cough strength.Failed cuff-leak test; weak cough; CN-X dysfunction; prolonged or traumatic intubation.
EncephalopathicInadequate airway protection, inability to clear secretions, agitation.RASS −2 or below despite sedation hold; persistent delirium; failure to follow commands.
Practical bedside framework
  • SBT-induced changes that predict cardiogenic failure: rising B-line count on lung US, rising NT-proBNP > 20% from baseline, ↑ hemoconcentration (Hb ↑ > 5%, surrogate for fluid shift into the lung), new mitral E/e′ > 13 on echo. Treat with diuresis and re-attempt; consider NIV bridge.
  • Diaphragm ultrasound (thickening fraction at the zone of apposition) predicts respiratory-pump failure independently of RSBI and is gaining traction at experienced centers.
  • Cuff-leak test — air loss < 110 mL with cuff deflated suggests laryngeal edema; pre-treat with IV methylprednisolone 4 hours pre-extubation in high-risk cases.
  • Post-extubation NIV / HFNC in high-risk patients reduces re-intubation (see §37.2).
The Kenny insightThe transition from positive-pressure to spontaneous ventilation reverses every cardiopulmonary interaction described in §29.4 within seconds. The same physiology that makes intubation a treatment for cardiogenic edema makes extubation a stress test for the LV. Watch the heart, not just the lung, in the post-extubation hour.25
Key takeaways
  • Four extubation gates: (1) mental status (follows commands, airway protection), (2) cough (adequate, brisk, productive), (3) secretions (manageable, suctioning < q2h), (4) cuff leak (≥ 110 mL or audible = no leak → consider IV methylprednisolone 4 h prior).
  • Post-extubation support: high-risk (HF, COPD, age > 65, prolonged intubation) → HFNC + intermittent NIV (Thille 2019 reduced reintubation & mortality); average risk → HFNC alone is non-inferior.
  • Cuff pressure target 20–30 cmH₂O (checked q-shift, after manipulation); < 20 → aspiration + leak; > 30 → tracheal ischemia (capillary pressure ~ 25–30).
  • Tracheostomy indications: anticipated > 14 days ventilation, weaning failure, secretion burden, neuromuscular weakness, upper-airway obstruction; timing: no mortality difference early vs late — practical default re-evaluate day 10–14.

38End-of-life ventilator withdrawal — terminal extubation

The most consequential decision in many ICUs is when and how to stop. Withdrawing the ventilator is a deliberate clinical procedure, not a failure of care. Done well, it preserves comfort and dignity; done poorly, it causes preventable suffering.

38.1 Pre-withdrawal
  • Goals-of-care alignment documented and confirmed with family. Palliative care consult if available.
  • Discontinue non-comfort interventions: vasopressors, antibiotics, nutrition, blood draws, monitors visible to family. Keep what supports comfort.
  • Treat reversible discomfort first: pain, secretions, anxiety. Many patients become more comfortable before withdrawal.
  • Prepare the family: explain the time course (typically minutes-hours), the expected appearance (changes in breathing pattern, terminal secretions, gasping), and that the appearance is not the patient's experience.
  • Position: head of bed elevated; positioned for family access; oral care; eyes closed if possible.

38.2 Pharmacologic preparation
Comfort medications for terminal extubation
DrugPre-extubation doseContinuousPurpose
Morphine2–10 mg IV bolus 5–10 min before extubation2–10 mg/h infusion, titrated to RR < 30 and absence of distressDyspnea, pain, anxiety
Hydromorphone0.5–2 mg IV bolus0.5–3 mg/hAlternative to morphine; better in renal failure
Midazolam or lorazepam1–4 mg IV bolus1–4 mg/h infusionAnxiety, agitation
Glycopyrrolate or scopolamine0.2 mg IV / patchq4h PRNTerminal secretions ("death rattle")
Haloperidol1–5 mg IV q4h PRN—Delirium, agitation refractory to opioid + benzo

The "no ceiling" principle: in terminal care, the comfort dose is whatever it takes. Doses far higher than ICU norms are appropriate when distress requires it. The doctrine of double effect is well-established legally and ethically.

38.3 The withdrawal procedure
  1. Confirm comfort meds running. Recheck family readiness.
  2. Pre-bolus opioid + benzo 5–10 minutes before.
  3. Reduce vent settings stepwise to FiO₂ 0.21 and PEEP 0 over a few minutes (or extubate directly — institution preference).
  4. Suction once if needed. Remove ETT or keep in place with T-piece per family preference.
  5. Stay at the bedside. Titrate comfort meds for any visible distress (RR > 30, accessory muscle use, agitation). Do not titrate to RR alone — comfort is the endpoint.
  6. Document: time, doses given, family presence, time of death.

38.4 Common pitfalls
  • Under-medicating for fear of "hastening death" — the literature consistently shows that adequately-treated dyspnea does not shorten survival.
  • Premature withdrawal of analgesia after extubation when patient appears settled — agitation often returns within 30–60 min as drugs redistribute.
  • Family unprepared for terminal secretions — counsel ahead that the sound is not distressing to the patient.
  • Overlooking the option of extubation to comfort — many families are relieved to see the tube removed even if the patient is sedated; emotional closure matters.
The bedside script"We're going to stop the breathing tube and the medications that aren't making him more comfortable. We'll keep the ones that do. He'll breathe on his own as long as he's able. We'll stay right here with you. He may take some breaths that look uncomfortable to you, but we'll treat him for any signs of distress so he stays peaceful. There's no set timeline — sometimes minutes, sometimes hours. Take the time you need."
Part VIII

Bedside application

When the alarm goes off, the patient drops their sat, or the chest stops rising — the diagnostic algorithms in this section get you to a fix.

39Troubleshooting at the bedside

When the alarm goes off, the patient drops their saturation, or the chest stops rising, three rules apply: look at the patient before the screen, FiO₂ to 1.0 buys time, and if you can't tell what's wrong, disconnect the circuit and bag. The figure below is the single page to consult; the subsections that follow are the deep dives.

Figure 39.1 — The single-page bedside troubleshooting flowchart. The top red banner is the universal first move. Three branches triage by what changed (pressure alarm, volume/flow alarm, gas exchange). The central navy banner is the obstructive-crash emergency — disconnect the circuit and let the chest empty. The bottom green boxes are the two-minute action sequences for acute hypoxemia and the three-bucket framework for unexpected hypercapnia. The bottom cross-reference map points to the deep-dive sections for each.

39.1 High-pressure alarm — DOPES / DOTTS

The mnemonic DOPE(S) for sudden deterioration on the vent: Displaced ETT · Obstruction · Pneumothorax (PTX) · Equipment failure — and the optional Stacked breaths (auto-PEEP). Most teaching has used the four-letter "DOPE" since the 1990s ATLS / PALS era; the "S" was added later to highlight auto-PEEP as a distinct and reversible cause specifically in obstructive disease. Both versions are correct; use whichever you were trained on, but never skip the S when ventilating an asthma or COPD patient.

The fix mnemonic is DOTTS: Disconnect from vent (bag with PEEP valve and FiO₂ 1.0) · Oxygenate · Tube position (waveform capnography + auscultation) · Tweak the vent (or check it) · Sonography (POCUS for PTX, ETT depth, pleural sliding).

Concrete approach when PIP alarms — the two-question algorithm (§16):

  1. Look at PIP and Pplat. If PIP↑ but Pplat normal → resistance: suction, check ETT for kink/biting, consider bronchospasm, ETT cuff over-inflation.
  2. If PIP↑ and Pplat↑ → compliance: PTX (auscultate, POCUS — §19), atelectasis (mucus plug — bronchoscopy via §37.10), pulmonary edema (CXR, BNP), abdominal hypertension (bladder pressure).
  3. Address auto-PEEP if flow trace shows expiratory tail not returning to zero (§17) — and in obstructive patients with sudden hypotension, default to the disconnect rescue (§17.3).
The single highest-yield bedside save in obstructive diseaseIf a COPD or asthma patient becomes hypotensive within minutes of intubation — even if the obvious differential includes PTX or sedation — disconnect the ventilator circuit at the ETT and let the chest passively empty for 20–30 seconds before doing anything else. Most stacked-breaths arrests recover hemodynamics within 1–2 minutes of release. Full discussion of the rescue, the mechanism, and the post-rescue settings is in §17.3.

39.2 Low-pressure / low-VT alarm
  • Circuit disconnect — re-connect; check humidifier, water trap, ETT cuff.
  • Cuff leak — measure pilot pressure (target 20–30 cmH₂O); re-inflate or replace tube. Full cuff-leak workup in §37.2a.
  • Bronchopleural fistula (BPF) — refractory leak despite intact cuff; chest tube and consider low-VT / low-PEEP strategy; ECMO at experienced centers for refractory cases. (HFOV is no longer recommended in adult ARDS — §20.9.)
  • Apnea / missed triggers — check trigger sensitivity, sedation depth, respiratory drive. Trigger anomalies in detail at §18.3.

39.3 Acute hypoxemia on the vent

Move quickly. The 2-minute action sequence: FiO₂ to 1.0 → look at the patient → POCUS (pleural sliding, B-lines, RV) → CXR for ETT depth, PTX, new infiltrate. Bedside differential while you work:

  • Tube — displaced, plugged, mainstem (auscultate, capnography, depth).
  • PTX — sliding lost on POCUS, hyperresonance, hypotension (§19).
  • Atelectasis — recent left lower-lobe collapse from a mucus plug; bronchoscopy (§37.10).
  • Pulmonary edema — CXR, BNP, exam. Cardiogenic vs ARDS distinction in §22.
  • PE — high index of suspicion, especially if PaCO₂↓ and EtCO₂↓ with stable minute ventilation. RV-protective vent strategy in §25.
  • Right-to-left shunt opening — patent foramen ovale (PFO) from acute right-ventricle (RV) strain in PE / ARDS.
  • Asynchrony / fighting the vent — sedate; or address the asynchrony pattern directly (§18).
  • Obstructive crash (asthma / COPD) → disconnect the circuit as the first move; auto-PEEP is the highest-yield diagnosis (§17.3).

39.4 Unexpected hypercapnia

The PaCO₂ ∝ V̇CO₂ / V̇A framework (§4.1) splits unexpected hypercapnia into three buckets:

  • ↑ V̇CO₂ (production): fever, sepsis, shivering, overfeeding (high respiratory quotient — see §36 nutrition discussion), seizures, recent bicarbonate bolus.
  • ↑ dead space: PE, low cardiac-output state, high PEEP overdistension, hyperinflation. Apparatus dead space (HME, Y-piece, catheter mount) contributes 50–100 mL on most circuits — meaningful at low VT (§4.1).
  • ↓ alveolar ventilation: auto-PEEP (§17), circuit leak (§37.2a), sedation, neuromuscular weakness, mode change, missed triggers (§18.3).
  • Compensatory CO₂ retention for metabolic alkalosis is rare in mechanical ventilation but worth checking pH and bicarbonate before pushing minute ventilation higher.
Key takeaways
  • High-pressure alarm (DOPES/DOTTS): Displaced ETT, Obstruction, Pneumothorax, Equipment, Stacked breaths (auto-PEEP) — Disconnect, Oxygenate, Tube position, Tweak vent, Sonography.
  • Diagnostic: if PIP↑ but Pplat normal → resistance (suction, kink, bronchospasm); if both ↑ → compliance (PTX, atelectasis, edema, abdominal hypertension).
  • Low-pressure/VT alarm: circuit disconnect, cuff leak (check pilot pressure), bronchopleural fistula (refractory leak).
  • Acute hypoxemia: tube (displaced, plugged, mainstem), PTX (no sliding on POCUS), atelectasis (mucus plug), edema, PE, PFO opening, asynchrony.

40Cases — putting it together

Eight teaching cases that walk the full reasoning chain — assessment, mechanism, vent strategy, anticipated trouble — for the archetype patients an ICU sees.

Case 1 — Severe ARDS, day 2

62F with influenza pneumonia, intubated 36 h ago. On VC-A/C: VT 380 mL (=6 mL/kg PBW), RR 26, PEEP 12, FiO₂ 0.90, set inspiratory flow 60 L/min, I:E 1:2.
ABG: pH 7.28, PaCO₂ 52, PaO₂ 70 → P/F 78. Pplat 32, total PEEP 13 (auto-PEEP 1), ΔP 20. Cstat = 380 / 20 = 19 mL/cmH₂O. Lactate 1.4. Norepi 0.06 µg/kg/min.

Reasoning: Severe diffuse ARDS (likely recruitable); driving pressure 20 is too high; Pplat at limit. Pure shunt physiology (P/F 78, FiO₂ 0.9 → still hypoxemic). Need to drop ΔP and recruit.

Plan (in order):

  1. Drop VT to 5 mL/kg PBW (≈ 320 mL); recheck Pplat at 5 min. Expected ΔP ~17.
  2. Increase PEEP per high-PEEP table: at FiO₂ 0.9, PEEP 18; check ΔP and SpO₂. If ΔP rises (overdistension), back off PEEP.
  3. Increase RR to 30 to compensate for VE loss (target pH ≥ 7.20).
  4. Sedate to RASS −3 to −4 (propofol + fentanyl). If persistent dyssynchrony, NMB cisatracurium 15 mg bolus + 37.5 mg/h × 24 h.
  5. Prone positioning ≥ 16 h/day (criteria met: P/F < 150 on FiO₂ ≥ 0.6 and PEEP ≥ 5).
  6. If P/F < 80 sustained after optimization + 2 prone sessions: ECMO referral.
  7. Conservative fluids (no resuscitation since shock controlled), Hb > 7, HOB 30°, head-of-bed care, DVT/stress-ulcer prophylaxis.

What to anticipate: prone-related desaturation in first 5 min (recovers); PEEP-induced hypotension (have norepi up-titration ready); P/F response in 1–4 h; failure to respond after 2 sessions = consider ECMO transfer.

Case 2 — Status asthmaticus, post-intubation crash

34M intubated 30 min ago for status asthmaticus. On VC-A/C: VT 500, RR 18, PEEP 5, FiO₂ 0.5, peak flow 60 L/min.
Now: BP 70/40, HR 130, SpO₂ 88. PIP 55, Pplat 22.

Reasoning: PIP–Pplat = 33 = resistance problem. Auto-PEEP/dynamic hyperinflation crashing venous return → hypotension. (Differential: tension PTX, but disconnect test will tell.)

Plan:

  1. Disconnect circuit, let chest empty 10 s. BP improves → confirms auto-PEEP. (If no improvement: needle decompress for PTX.)
  2. Reconnect with: RR 8–10, VT 6 mL/kg PBW (~ 420 mL), peak flow 80 L/min, I:E 1:4, square waveform, PEEP 0 initially (set to ~75% of measured auto-PEEP later if triggering).
  3. Volume bolus 500 mL crystalloid; norepi as needed.
  4. Continuous albuterol nebulizer 10–20 mg/h, ipratropium q4h.
  5. Methylprednisolone 80 mg IV; IV Mg 2 g over 20 min.
  6. Sedation to deep RASS −4 with ketamine + propofol; NMB if dyssynchrony.
  7. Permit hypercapnia: PaCO₂ 60–80 OK with pH ≥ 7.15.
  8. Reassess PIP, Pplat, auto-PEEP q1h. If pH < 7.10 despite optimization: ECMO referral.

Case 3 — COPD exacerbation on NIV, decision point at 2 h

72F, GOLD-D (Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease, severity group D) COPD, exacerbation. On BiPAP IPAP 14 / EPAP 6, FiO₂ 0.4 × 2 h.
Started: pH 7.22, PaCO₂ 80, PaO₂ 58, RR 32, HR 110, GCS 13.
At 2 h: pH 7.32, PaCO₂ 60, PaO₂ 62, RR 22, HR 95, GCS 14.

Reasoning: Classic NIV success — pH up, PaCO₂ down, RR down, mentation improving. HACOR at 1 h was likely ≤ 5.

Plan: Continue BiPAP; same settings × 4–6 more h, then reassess. Plan to wean as tolerated. Treat exacerbation: nebs, steroids (prednisone 40 mg PO × 5 d), antibiotics if purulence, optimize home regimen for discharge.

Counterfactual: If at 2 h pH was 7.18 / PaCO₂ 85 / RR 36 / GCS 11 — intubate. Delay risks aspiration arrest. The 1–2 h reassessment is the safety net.

Case 4 — Cardiogenic edema, intubated despite NIV

78M, ischemic cardiomyopathy, EF 25%, presents with acute pulmonary edema, hypoxemia. NIV trial × 30 min: still SpO₂ 84% on FiO₂ 1.0, RR 38. Intubated.
Initial vent: VC-A/C, VT 6 mL/kg PBW, RR 18, PEEP 5, FiO₂ 1.0. ABG: pH 7.30, PaCO₂ 36, PaO₂ 65 → P/F 65. BP 95/55.

Reasoning: Cardiogenic shunt (flooded alveoli). PEEP recruits and reduces LV preload + afterload. Diuresis is the actual treatment.

Plan:

  1. Increase PEEP to 10–12 (afterload reduction, recruit). Reassess P/F at 30 min.
  2. Wean FiO₂ as oxygenation improves; target SpO₂ 92–96%.
  3. IV furosemide bolus + drip; goal net negative 2–3 L over 24 h.
  4. Inotropic support if needed (dobutamine if BP tolerates); revascularization if ischemic.
  5. Once euvolemic and EF recovering: rapid PSV transition, SBT within 24–48 h.
The post-extubation flash edema riskThis patient has marginal LV function. Post-extubation, the swing from positive to negative intrathoracic pressure raises LV afterload abruptly. Plan post-extubation NIV (IPAP 10/EPAP 5) and aggressive diuresis pre-extubation to avoid weaning-induced pulmonary edema (§37.4).

Case 5 — Septic shock + ARDS

54M, urinary sepsis, intubated. P/F 140 on PEEP 10 / FiO₂ 0.6. Norepi 0.4 µg/kg/min, vasopressin 0.04 U/min, lactate 4.5, MAP 62. Pplat 28, ΔP 13.

Reasoning: Moderate ARDS in distributive shock. Vent strategy is fine; the issue is competing physiology — PEEP titration may worsen shock; conservative fluids may worsen perfusion.

Plan:

  1. Lung-protective vent: continue VT 5–6 mL/kg, PEEP 10, FiO₂ to SpO₂ 88–95%, ΔP ≤ 15. Acceptable as is.
  2. Source control: confirm urology consulted, drainage adequate, antibiotics broad-spectrum.
  3. Resuscitation: 30 mL/kg crystalloid completed; further volume only if dynamic markers (PPV, SVV, passive leg raise) suggest fluid responsiveness. Otherwise, hold fluids.
  4. Vasopressors: norepi titrated for MAP 65; add vasopressin (already on); epinephrine if refractory; consider hydrocortisone 200 mg/d if shock persists ≥ 6 h on max norepi.
  5. Once shock resolved (off pressors × 24 h): conservative fluid strategy (diurese to even balance per FACTT).
  6. Reassess for prone if P/F drops < 150.

Case 6 — Massive PE with shock

61F, post-op day 3 from total knee, sudden hypotension and hypoxemia. CT: saddle PE. Echo: dilated RV, septal flattening, McConnell's sign (apical sparing of RV free-wall hypokinesis — classic for acute PE). BP 80/50 on norepi 0.3 µg/kg/min, SpO₂ 88% on a non-rebreather (NRB) mask.

Reasoning: RV-led obstructive shock. Intubation will likely worsen RV (induction drops SVR; positive pressure drops preload). Consider catheter-directed therapy or thrombolysis before intubation.

Plan (escalating):

  1. HFNC 60 L/min FiO₂ 1.0 to maintain SpO₂ > 92% (avoid hypoxia → ↑ PVR).
  2. Norepi up; goal MAP 65 — RV coronary perfusion depends on systemic BP.
  3. Inhaled nitric oxide 20 ppm via mask if available — selectively drops PVR.
  4. Definitive: alteplase 100 mg IV over 2 h (or "half-dose" 50 mg if bleeding-prone), or catheter-directed thrombectomy at experienced center.
  5. If intubation unavoidable: pre-load norepi, ketamine + rocuronium, hand-bag low rate / low PEEP, have iNO and ECMO ready.
  6. VA-ECMO if shock refractory.

Case 7 — Guillain-Barré progression

43M, GBS day 4, on the ward. FVC has dropped from 3.2 → 1.8 L over 24 h. NIF −25 cmH₂O. RR 24, says "I'm tired."

Reasoning: Neuromuscular failure imminent. The 20–30–40 trigger is met (FVC dropping past threshold; NIF less negative than −30). Don't wait for ABG.

Plan:

  1. Transfer to ICU; intubate semi-electively (RSI with rocuronium — not succinylcholine: hyperkalemia risk in NMD).
  2. Vent: VC-A/C, VT 8–10 mL/kg PBW (lungs are normal, prevent atelectasis), PEEP 5, FiO₂ 0.3, RR matched to drive.
  3. Plasma exchange or IVIG.
  4. Aggressive secretion clearance — chest physiotherapy, in-exsufflator, suctioning.
  5. Plan tracheostomy at day 7–14 if no improvement in respiratory mechanics.
  6. DVT prophylaxis (immobile), nutrition, pressure-injury prevention, watch for autonomic instability (BP swings, arrhythmias).

Case 8 — Failure to wean post-CABG (coronary artery bypass graft)

71M, post-coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) day 5, persistent vent dependence. SBT today on PSV 5/PEEP 5 × 30 min: RR 28, VT 320, RSBI 88, but agitated and diaphoretic, BP rose 95 → 140, HR 80 → 110.

Reasoning: Numerical RSBI passes, patient fails. Look for fixable causes (the four classics: cardiac, fluid, neuro, mechanical).

Plan:

  1. Stop SBT, return to rest settings.
  2. Cardiac: echo for new wall-motion abnormality, troponin trend, BNP. SBT in marginal LV is a stress test.
  3. Fluid: net positive 6 L since OR. Diurese to even by tomorrow.
  4. Neuro: delirium screen (Confusion Assessment Method for the ICU [CAM-ICU]); minimize benzodiazepines; mobilize.
  5. Mechanical: cuff leak test for upper-airway edema; bronchoscopy for occult mucus plug or atelectasis; assess for diaphragm dysfunction (POCUS).
  6. Optimize nutrition (overfeeding raises VCO₂ → demand); treat pain (chest tubes); minimize nocturnal disruption.
  7. Re-attempt SBT in 24 h with above corrected. Discuss tracheostomy if vent dependence likely > 7 more days.
The "stress test" framing of every SBTAn SBT in a patient with marginal cardiac reserve, positive fluid balance, or weak diaphragm is exactly that: a stress test. Failure is information, not defeat — fix what failed, then retry. The biggest mistake is repeating the same SBT every day expecting a different result.

40.9 Worked calculation cases — the math at the bedside

Three patients at three points on the severity spectrum. For each: vignette, the bedside calculations, the changes that follow.

Case A — Mild ARDS, post-pneumonia, average build

Vignette. 68F, 5'4" (64 in), 80 kg actual, post-pneumonia ARDS. VC-A/C, VT 480 mL, RR 18, PEEP 8, FiO₂ 0.50. ABG pH 7.32 / PaCO₂ 50 / PaO₂ 88 (P/F 176). Pplat 24, PIP 28.

1. PBW (women) = \(45.5 + 2.3 \times (64 - 60) = 54.7\) kg.

2. VT/PBW = \(480 / 54.7 = 8.8\) mL/kg — too high (target 6 mL/kg = ~330 mL).

3. Driving pressure ΔP = \(P_{plat} - \text{PEEP} = 24 - 8 = 16\) cmH₂O — above target ≤ 15.

4. Mechanical power = \(0.098 \times 18 \times 0.480 \times (28 - \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 16) = 0.098 \times 18 \times 0.48 \times 20 \approx 16.9\) J/min — borderline elevated (concerning > 17).

Plan. Drop VT to 330 mL (6 mL/kg PBW) — this alone cuts MP and ΔP. Recheck Pplat in 1 h; if still ≥ 25, raise PEEP by 2 to recruit. If pH falls below 7.20, accept hypercapnia or escalate to deeper sedation.

Case B — Severe ARDS in obesity

Vignette. 55M, 5'10" (70 in), 145 kg actual (BMI 46), severe COVID ARDS, sedated and paralyzed. VC-A/C, VT 480 mL, RR 22, PEEP 14, FiO₂ 0.80. ABG pH 7.28 / PaCO₂ 55 / PaO₂ 76 (P/F 95). Pplat 32, PIP 40. No esophageal manometer available.

1. PBW (men) = \(50 + 2.3 \times (70 - 60) = 73\) kg. Use PBW, not actual weight — lungs don't scale with adiposity.

2. VT/PBW = \(480 / 73 = 6.6\) mL/kg — appropriate.

3. ΔP = \(32 - 14 = 18\) cmH₂O — elevated. Pplat 32 also exceeds the standard target.

4. The obesity caveat. In a BMI 46 patient, much of the airway pressure is chest-wall pressure, not alveolar pressure. With esophageal manometry you'd target PL,end-insp < 25; an "acceptable lung pressure" can correspond to a Pplat of 30–35. Without the manometer, accept Pplat up to ~35 if oxygenation and hemodynamics allow.

Plan. Don't drop VT further — that's not the problem. Initiate prone positioning ≥ 16 h/day. Place an esophageal balloon if available. Continue NMB while prone. If P/F stays < 80 despite optimization, ECMO consult. If improving on prone after 4–6 h, hold settings.

Case C — COPD exacerbation with auto-PEEP

Vignette. 72M, 5'8" (68 in), 70 kg, severe COPD. Just intubated. VC-A/C, set RR 14 (actual 24 — over-triggering), VT 600 mL, set PEEP 5, peak flow 60 L/min. ABG pH 7.22 / PaCO₂ 68 / PaO₂ 78. Pplat 20, PIP 28. Lightly sedated, agitated.

1. PBW = \(50 + 2.3 \times (68 - 60) = 68.4\) kg. VT/PBW = \(600/68.4 = 8.8\) mL/kg — slightly high but acceptable in COPD.

2. End-expiratory hold: total PEEP rises from 5 to 13. Auto-PEEP = \(13 - 5 = 8\) cmH₂O. Significant air-trapping.

3. True driving pressure (use total PEEP, not set PEEP) = \(P_{plat} - \text{total PEEP} = 20 - 13 = 7\) cmH₂O. Lung mechanics are fine — the patient's lungs aren't injured; the problem is incomplete exhalation.

4. PIP 28 vs Pplat 20 = a 8 cmH₂O resistive pressure (high airflow obstruction).

Plan.

  • Lengthen Te. Drop set RR to 12 (more time per breath to exhale).
  • Shorten Ti. Raise peak flow from 60 → 80 L/min (gets the breath in faster, frees expiratory time).
  • Set extrinsic PEEP ≈ 6 (~75% of auto-PEEP). Counterintuitive: adding PEEP reduces trigger work because the patient now starts each breath from 6 cmH₂O instead of 13.
  • Bronchodilators on full schedule (continuous albuterol or stacked nebs + ipratropium); steroids (methylprednisolone 60–125 mg IV q6 h).
  • Sedation — fentanyl + propofol — to take the edge off the air-trapping sensation.
  • Recheck end-expiratory hold in 30 min and ABG in 1 h. Goal pH > 7.25, falling auto-PEEP, slowing RR.
Key lesson from Case CIn auto-PEEP physiology, the danger is not high Pplat (it's normal) but ineffective triggering, RV strain, and patient distress. Fix it by giving the lung more time to exhale (low RR, high peak flow), not by lowering VT.

40.10 ICU case simulator — manage a patient over 24 hours

Vent settings

Adjuncts

Current state · hour 0

Key takeaways
  • Severe ARDS case: ↓VT to 5 mL/kg (ΔP too high), ↑PEEP per high-PEEP table (recruit), ↑RR to maintain VE, deep sedation ± NMB, prone ≥ 16 h/day, ECMO if P/F < 80 sustained.
  • Status asthmaticus crash: disconnect to decompress (confirms auto-PEEP), then revent: RR 8–10, VT 6 mL/kg, peak flow 80, I:E 1:4, PEEP 0 (later match ~75% auto-PEEP), permit PaCO₂ 60–80.
  • COPD exacerbation on NIV: if improving at 1–2 h (pH up, PaCO₂ down, RR down, GCS better) → continue; if worsening (pH ≤ 7.18, RR > 35) → intubate (aspiration arrest risk).
  • Cardiogenic edema: PEEP 8–12 for afterload reduction + recruitment, aggressive diuresis, inotropes if needed, rapid PSV transition; post-extubation NIV prophylaxis to prevent flash edema.

41Ventilator-associated events & the ABCDEF liberation bundle

The longer the ventilator stays, the more the patient is at risk for nosocomial pneumonia, ICU-acquired weakness, delirium, and post-ICU syndrome. Two frameworks have moved the needle.

41.1 Ventilator-associated events (VAEs) — the CDC surveillance ladder

The CDC's 2013 VAE definition replaced the older subjective VAP criteria with an objective, ventilator-data-driven algorithm so that surveillance is reproducible across centers and not biased by clinician interpretation. The ladder is hierarchical — each tier requires the previous tier plus additional criteria:

CDC ventilator-associated event surveillance algorithm
TierRequired preceding stateTriggerWindow
VAC
(ventilator-associated condition)		≥ 2 calendar days of stable or improving daily-minimum FiO₂ and PEEPFiO₂ ↑ ≥ 0.20 (20 percentage points) or PEEP ↑ ≥ 3 cmH₂O — sustained for ≥ 2 calendar daysFrom ventilator day 3 onward (cannot occur in first 2 days)
IVAC
(infection-related VAC)		VAC criteria metOn the day of VAC onset ± 2 days: temp < 36 °C or > 38 °C or WBC < 4 or ≥ 12 K/µL AND new antimicrobial agent started and continued ≥ 4 calendar days± 2 days of VAC onset
Possible VAP (PVAP)IVAC criteria metPurulent respiratory secretions or positive culture from sputum, ETT aspirate, BAL, lung tissue, or protected specimen brush meeting the CDC quantitative thresholds (e.g., BAL ≥ 10⁴ CFU/mL; ETT aspirate ≥ 10⁵ CFU/mL); pleural fluid culture; histopathology consistent with pneumonia± 2 days of VAC onset
VAE ≠ VAP, and surveillance ≠ diagnosisThe CDC algorithm is for surveillance — comparing infection rates across hospitals — not for clinical diagnosis or treatment decisions at the bedside. A patient can meet PVAP criteria without true pneumonia (atelectasis with fever and antibiotic for an unrelated indication will satisfy the algorithm), and a patient can have clinical VAP without meeting any VAE tier (e.g., pneumonia in the first 48 h, or pneumonia without a sustained 2-day worsening). Use VAE for quality reporting; use clinical judgment + cultures for individual patient decisions.

Clinical VAP diagnosis requires: ≥ 48 h of intubation + new or progressive infiltrate + at least 2 of (fever, leukocytosis, purulent secretions, oxygenation deterioration). Differential always includes pulmonary edema, atelectasis, ARDS progression, PE, and chemical pneumonitis. Treat empirically per local antibiogram (typically anti-pseudomonal β-lactam ± MRSA coverage in late-onset / risk-factor-positive); de-escalate at 48–72 h based on cultures; treatment course 7 days for most VAP (IDSA/ATS 2016).

41.2 VAP prevention bundle — the IHI & SHEA recommendations

Bundles work because they're compliance multipliers — implementing each element > 90% of the time matters more than which specific elements are included. Modern bundles include:

VAP prevention elements with strength of evidence
ElementMechanismEvidence strength
Head-of-bed elevation 30–45°Reduces gastric reflux and microaspirationModerate; recommended unless contraindicated (shock, spine injury)
Subglottic-suction ETT (continuous or intermittent)Removes pooled secretions above the cuff before they leak past itStrong — RCT meta-analyses show ~50% VAP reduction; standard for expected ventilation > 48 h
Cuff pressure 20–30 cmH₂OBelow 20 → microaspiration; above 30 → tracheal mucosal injuryStrong; check q-shift
Daily SAT + SBTShortens ventilator duration → fewer days at riskStrong (ABC trial); core ABCDEF
Early mobilityImproves secretion clearance, prevents atelectasisModerate; reduces ICU days, may reduce VAP
Oral care every 2–4 hReduces oropharyngeal colonizationStrong for cleaning (toothbrushing, suctioning); chlorhexidine controversial after 2017 meta-analyses suggesting harm in non-cardiac-surgery populations — many centers now de-emphasize chlorhexidine and emphasize mechanical cleaning
Stress-ulcer + DVT prophylaxisStandard ICU bundleStrong
Avoid unnecessary circuit changesRoutine changes increase contamination, do not reduce VAPStrong; change only when visibly soiled or malfunctioning
Closed (in-line) suctionReduces cross-contamination from open-system splashModest; benefit in derecruitment prevention more than VAP rate
Selective digestive decontamination (SDD)Topical + IV antibiotics suppress gut Gram-negativesStrong in ICUs with low baseline resistance (Netherlands data); not adopted in US due to AMR concerns
ProbioticsModulates gut floraConflicting — recent large trials negative; not currently recommended
PPI vs H₂ blocker for stress prophylaxisH₂ blockers may have lower VAP rate (less alkalinization → less gastric overgrowth)Modest; PPI may be preferred for GI bleeding prevention; tradeoff is institution-dependent
Bundle compliance > bundle compositionThe strongest VAP-prevention evidence is for high-fidelity bundle implementation (audit feedback, daily checklist on rounds), not for any single intervention. A 90% implementation of a 6-element bundle beats a 50% implementation of a 10-element bundle. If your unit's VAP rate is high, audit compliance before adding new elements.

41.3 The ABCDEF bundle (ICU Liberation)

The Society of Critical Care Medicine's evidence-based bundle reduces delirium, mortality, and ICU/vent days when implemented coherently:22

LetterElementTarget / how
AAssess, prevent, manage painCPOT or BPS q4h; treat before sedation
BBoth SAT and SBTDaily, paired
CChoice of analgesia and sedationLight targets (RASS 0 to −1); prefer dexmedetomidine or propofol over benzodiazepines
DDelirium — assess, prevent, manageCAM-ICU or ICDSC q-shift; non-pharmacologic first; avoid benzos; consider dex if delirium
EEarly mobility & exerciseActive range-of-motion, sitting, ambulation as tolerated even on the vent
FFamily engagementOpen visiting, family on rounds, communication aids
Two ABCDEF tables in this book — different framing on purposeThe ABCDEF bundle also appears in §35.6 as part of the sedation chapter, framed around the choice of drugs and the analgosedation philosophy. Here in §41.3 it is framed around liberation outcomes — vent-free days, ICU mortality, delirium reduction at the unit level. The elements are identical; the lens is different. Use §35.6 when choosing what to write on the sedation order; use §41.3 when evaluating whether your ICU's bundle compliance is moving the unit-level metrics. Both originate in the PADIS guidelines (Devlin 201849); both are validated by the ICU Liberation Collaborative (Pun 201922) showing benefit only when implemented as a coherent whole rather than letter-by-letter.

41.4 ICU-acquired weakness (ICU-AW)

Up to 50% of patients ventilated > 7 days develop generalized weakness — from disuse atrophy, critical-illness neuromuscular disease, and prolonged neuromuscular blockade. The term ICU-AW is an umbrella covering several distinct entities with overlapping presentations but different prognoses.

ICU-AW — the three principal entities
EntityMechanismElectrodiagnosticsPrognosis
Critical illness myopathy (CIM)Direct injury to muscle fibers — sepsis, hyperglycemia, corticosteroids, NMB, immobility. Selective loss of myosin thick filaments.EMG: low-amplitude motor units, normal nerve conduction velocity. Muscle biopsy: myosin loss confirms.Better than CIP. Most patients recover meaningful function within 3–6 months if survive ICU.
