Critical illness polyneuropathy (CIP)Axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy from microcirculatory dysfunction in nerves; sepsis + multi-organ failure are the strongest risk factors.EMG/NCS: reduced amplitude with preserved conduction velocity (axonal pattern). Reduced sensory action potentials. Distal > proximal weakness.Worse than CIM. Often residual weakness at 1 year; some never fully recover.
Combined CIM + CIP (CIPM)Most common in practice — features of both; sepsis is the unifying risk factor.Mixed EMG findings.Intermediate; the CIP component drives long-term outcomes.
Prolonged NMB weaknessPersistent neuromuscular junction blockade after the drug should have worn off — usually acetylcholine-receptor desensitization in the setting of prolonged infusion + steroids + renal dysfunction. Distinct from CIM/CIP but often coexists.TOF abnormal; train-of-four monitoring during NMB infusion catches it early.Usually resolves within hours-days once NMB cleared, unless CIM/CIP also present.

Diaphragm-specific atrophy begins within 18 hours of controlled ventilation and is independently associated with prolonged liberation. The mechanism is disuse — passive ventilation removes the diaphragm's contractile load, mitochondrial protein synthesis falls, and atrophy progresses ~1–6% per day. Diaphragm thickening fraction on ultrasound (§19.3) is the bedside surrogate; TF < 30% predicts liberation failure.

Risk factors are largely overlapping for the three entities: sepsis, multi-organ failure, prolonged mechanical ventilation, immobility, hyperglycemia, prolonged corticosteroid use, prolonged neuromuscular blockade (especially with concurrent steroids), and total parenteral nutrition.

Mitigation strategies (in order of evidence strength):

  1. Minimize sedation depth — RASS 0 to −1; the single highest-leverage intervention.
  2. Avoid unnecessary NMB — reserve for refractory dyssynchrony or severe ARDS per ROSE-era criteria (§33).
  3. Early mobility — passive range of motion within 24 h, sitting at the edge of the bed by day 2 if hemodynamically stable, ambulation as tolerated even on the vent. RCTs show shorter delirium and earlier liberation.
  4. Modes that preserve diaphragmatic activity — PSV, NAVA, PAV+ — once the patient can tolerate them.
  5. Glucose control 140–180 mg/dL (NICE-SUGAR; avoid the lower 80–110 range that increased mortality).
  6. Minimize corticosteroid duration when possible; the combination of steroids + NMB sharply raises CIM risk.
Over-sedation kills weeksThe single biggest reversible cause of prolonged ventilation is unnecessary deep sedation. Each day of light, targeted sedation (RASS 0 to −1) versus deep sedation roughly halves the duration of ventilation. Default to "less," not "more."
Sepsis + steroids + NMB = the perfect stormThe patient at highest risk for severe, prolonged CIPM is the septic ARDS patient on dexamethasone + cisatracurium infusion. Each component independently raises CIPM risk; together they are nearly additive. Hold NMB at the earliest possible point, taper steroids on the protocolized schedule, and consider early diaphragm ultrasound for objective tracking of recovery.
Key takeaways
  • VAE surveillance ladder: VAC → IVAC → PVAP. Each tier requires the previous + additional criteria.
  • Most reversible cause of prolonged ventilation: unnecessary deep sedation. Target RASS 0 to −1.
  • The ABCDEF bundle (Pain, Both SAT+SBT, Choice of meds, Delirium, Early mobility, Family) reduces mortality, vent days, and delirium when implemented coherently.
  • Diaphragm atrophy begins within 18 h of controlled ventilation. Preserve diaphragmatic activity when safe (PSV, NAVA).
  • Avoid benzodiazepine drips routinely — propofol or dexmedetomidine first-line.
Part IX

Adjunctive topics

Special clinical scenarios that don't fit the standard archetypes, plus the unglamorous bedside skills (cough assist, in-line drug delivery) that make the difference between passed and failed extubations.

42Less common but high-stakes scenarios

Scenarios that don't fit the standard archetypes but show up often enough that every intensivist should know the strategy: TRALI vs TACO, burn / inhalation injury, diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, bronchopleural fistula, high-frequency ventilation as rescue, and drug-induced respiratory failure. (End-of-life ventilator withdrawal is covered separately in §38.)

42.1 TRALI vs TACO vs cardiogenic edema vs ARDS — post-transfusion respiratory failure

New hypoxemia and bilateral infiltrates within 6 hours of transfusion forces a four-way differential:

Post-transfusion respiratory failure differential
FeatureTRALITACOCardiogenic edemaNew ARDS
Timing after transfusionWithin 6 hDuring or within 6 hVariableVariable
BPOften hypotensiveHypertensive (volume overload)VariableVariable
JVP / B-line scoreNormal-low; B-lines + (capillary leak)Elevated; B-lines +++Elevated; B-lines +++Variable; B-lines +
BNP / NT-proBNPNot elevatedElevatedElevatedNot elevated
EchoNormal LV; clearNormal LV but volume-overloaded↓ LV functionVariable
Response to diureticMinimalRapid (hours)RapidMinimal
Response to lung-protective ventRecovers in 48–72 hResolves with diuresisResolves with diuresis + LV supportDays–weeks course

TRALI: HLA/HNA antibodies in donor plasma activate recipient pulmonary neutrophils → permeability edema. Treatment is supportive: lung-protective vent (VT 4–6 mL/kg, Pplat ≤ 30), avoid further plasma-rich products, vasopressors if shock. Diuretics don't help (it's not volume overload). Most resolve in 48–72 h.

TACO: hydrostatic edema from volume overload. Treatment: stop the transfusion, IV diuretic, oxygen, NIV if needed. Resolves rapidly with diuresis.

The single discriminating testBNP or NT-proBNP differentiates TRALI (normal) from TACO (elevated) when the clinical picture is mixed. Echo confirms.

42.2 Burn & inhalation injury

Three injuries co-exist and progress on different timelines:

  • Upper-airway thermal injury — from steam, hot smoke. Edema progresses over 24–48 h. Indications for early intubation: facial burns, singed nasal hairs, soot in pharynx, hoarseness, stridor, full-thickness burns > 40% TBSA. Intubate early — once edema sets in, the airway is unmanageable.
  • Lower-airway / parenchymal injury — from soot, particulate, chemical irritants. ARDS-like; develops over 24–72 h.
  • Systemic toxicity — carbon monoxide (CO) and cyanide (CN). CO has 200× O₂ affinity for hemoglobin → falsely normal SpO₂ (the pulse oximeter cannot distinguish carboxyhemoglobin from oxyhemoglobin). Co-oximetry measures carboxyhemoglobin directly. CN inhibits cytochrome oxidase → severe lactic acidosis with normal-appearing oxygenation.
Burn / inhalation injury management
IssueAction
Suspected CO toxicityFiO₂ 1.0 (reduces COHb half-life from 5 h to 1 h); HBO if COHb > 25%, neuro symptoms, or pregnancy
Suspected CN toxicityHydroxocobalamin 5 g IV; sodium thiosulfate 12.5 g IV; treat empirically if soot + lactate > 8 in fire victim
Vent strategyLung-protective (VT 4–6 mL/kg); high humidification; bronchoscopy on day 1 to assess and clear airway
Fluid resuscitationParkland or modified Brooke formula; over-resuscitation causes ARDS in burns
BronchoscopyDiagnostic + therapeutic (clear soot/casts); repeat as needed

42.3 Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage (DAH)

DAH is the radiographic appearance of bilateral infiltrates with progressive hypoxemia caused by alveolar bleeding rather than edema or pneumonia. Three pathologic patterns:

  • Pulmonary capillaritis — neutrophil-mediated capillary destruction. Causes: ANCA-associated vasculitis (GPA, MPA), anti-GBM (Goodpasture), SLE, drug-induced.
  • Bland hemorrhage — no inflammation. Causes: anticoagulation, mitral stenosis, antiphospholipid syndrome, idiopathic pulmonary hemosiderosis.
  • Diffuse alveolar damage with hemorrhage — superimposed on ARDS.

Recognition: hemoptysis is present in only 1/3 of cases; falling Hb, bilateral infiltrates with hypoxemia, normal-to-elevated DLCO (the only condition where DLCO rises with worsening lung disease — because intra-alveolar Hb takes up CO).

Diagnostic confirmation: bronchoscopy with sequential lavages — progressively bloodier returns confirm DAH. Send for cell count, cytology, microbiology.

Management:

  • Reverse anticoagulation if applicable.
  • High-dose IV methylprednisolone (1 g/day × 3 d) for vasculitis or autoimmune causes.
  • Plasma exchange for anti-GBM disease and severe ANCA disease.
  • Cyclophosphamide or rituximab for ANCA disease.
  • Vent strategy: standard lung-protective; transfuse Hb > 8; correct coagulopathy.
  • VV-ECMO is contraindicated relative to standard ECMO criteria (heparin requirement makes hemorrhage worse) — but can be considered with anticoagulation-free or bivalirudin-based circuits.

42.4 Bronchopleural fistula (BPF)

A persistent communication between an airway and the pleural space — manifested as a continuous air leak through a chest tube despite full lung expansion. Causes: post-pneumonectomy / lobectomy dehiscence, necrotizing pneumonia (Staph aureus, anaerobes, fungi), barotrauma, malignancy.

The vent challenge: positive-pressure breaths preferentially go through the low-resistance fistula rather than ventilating the patient. Three goals: (1) minimize the volume lost through the fistula, (2) preserve gas exchange, (3) allow the leak to heal.

BPF vent strategy
SettingGoal
ModePSV preferred (smaller efforts, less leak); VC/PC with low VT acceptable
VT4–5 mL/kg PBW (lower than standard ARDS) — minimize leak volume
RRLower (10–14) to reduce minute leak
PEEPLowest possible (0–5) — every cmH₂O of PEEP increases leak
I:EShort Ti (lower mean airway pressure)
Pplat target< 25 if possible
Permissive hypercapniaYes — pH ≥ 7.20
AdjunctsIndependent / differential lung ventilation (double-lumen tube, two ventilators) for unilateral disease; bronchial blocker; HFOV historic; surgical or bronchoscopic occlusion (Watanabe spigot, glue) for refractory leaks
The chest-tube quizIf air leak through the chest tube increases on inspiration with mandatory breaths but disappears on PSV, the vent is driving the leak. Switch to PSV with the lowest support that maintains gas exchange.
Quantifying the leak — and watching it heal

Document the leak in objective terms so you can track resolution:

  • Leak fraction = (delivered VT − exhaled VT) / delivered VT. A 10% leak is small; > 30% is large and may compromise ventilation.
  • Continuous vs inspiratory-only bubbling at the chest-tube water seal. Inspiratory-only = small fistula or pleural defect; continuous bubbling through expiration = large fistula or persistent connection. Healing trends from continuous → inspiratory-only → cough-only → none.
  • End-tidal CO₂ at the chest-tube exit: in a true bronchopleural fistula the exhaled gas through the fistula contains CO₂ at near-arterial values, mirroring what comes out of the ETT. This confirms the airway-to-pleural connection (vs an isolated pleural-space leak from a cracked rib or surgical defect).
Refractory leaks — escalation ladder
  1. Lowest mean-airway-pressure strategy: PSV at the lowest tolerable support; minimize PEEP; accept hypercapnia; reduce I:E ratio.
  2. Suction modulation: chest-tube suction at the lowest level that maintains lung expansion (often −10 to −20 cmH₂O); higher suction increases the trans-pleural gradient and propagates the leak.
  3. Independent / differential lung ventilation: double-lumen tube (or bronchial blocker) with two ventilators — one for the affected lung at very low pressures (or even no PEEP), one for the unaffected lung at conventional settings. Used for unilateral disease (e.g., post-pneumonectomy stump dehiscence). Requires ICU expertise; significant nursing burden.
  4. Bronchoscopic occlusion: endobronchial valves (one-way: air out, none in), Watanabe spigots, fibrin glue, or coil/stent placement at the segmental bronchus feeding the fistula. Increasingly first-line for proximal fistulas.
  5. HFOV / HFJV (historic): low tidal volumes around a constant mean airway pressure may reduce leak in selected cases; rarely used now given the negative adult ARDS trials and limited operator experience.
  6. Surgery: pleural decortication, muscle flap (latissimus, intercostal), thoracoplasty, or completion pneumonectomy. Generally only for fistulas that fail to close after weeks of conservative management and the underlying disease has resolved (otherwise recurrence is the rule).
The patient does not die from the BPFBPF is an ominous sign because the underlying pathology is severe (necrotizing pneumonia, malignancy, post-resection complication), but the leak itself is rarely lethal. Most close once the disease process resolves. The temptation to fix the leak surgically while the patient is still acutely ill usually fails. Manage with the lowest pressures that keep gas exchange acceptable, treat the underlying process, and consider surgery only when the patient is otherwise recovering.

42.5 High-frequency ventilation — historical context and remaining niche

HFOV (high-frequency oscillatory ventilation) and HFJV (high-frequency jet ventilation) deliver tiny tidal volumes (1–4 mL/kg) at high frequencies (3–15 Hz for HFOV; 100–300/min for HFJV) around a constant elevated mean airway pressure. The conceptual appeal in ARDS was clear: maintain "open lung" via high mean pressure while avoiding the cyclic stretch injury of conventional tidal breaths.

Two large RCTs in moderate-severe ARDS (OSCILLATE, Ferguson 2013, NEJM; OSCAR, Young 2013, NEJM) did not show benefit; OSCILLATE was stopped early for harm (HFOV mortality 47% vs 35% control). Mechanism of harm probably hemodynamic — high mean airway pressures dropped venous return and CO. HFOV is no longer recommended for routine adult ARDS management (ATS 2017 / ESICM 2023 guidelines).19

Remaining niches in adult care:

  • Bronchopleural fistula — low tidal volumes around a constant pressure may reduce leak (anecdotal).
  • Severe air-leak syndromes / barotrauma — selected rescue when conventional ventilation cannot maintain gas exchange at acceptable pressures.
  • Pediatric & neonatal ICU — still in routine use for pediatric ARDS, persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn, congenital diaphragmatic hernia, meconium aspiration. Adult skill is limited; transfer to a center with HFOV experience if seriously considered.
  • Otolaryngologic surgery — HFJV used for laryngeal / tracheal procedures requiring an open field.

42.6 Drug-induced respiratory failure — toxicology on the vent

Several poisonings present primarily as respiratory failure and have idiosyncratic ventilator considerations. The general principle: treat the toxidrome, not the breathing.

Drug overdoses with characteristic ventilator implications
ToxinTypical patternVent strategyAntidote / specifics
Salicylate (aspirin)Tachypnea + mixed acid-base (resp alkalosis + AG metabolic acidosis)Avoid intubation if possible. If intubated: match or exceed the patient's spontaneous minute ventilation (often 30–40 L/min); never let PaCO₂ rise — drives salicylate into CNSNaHCO₃ 1–2 mEq/kg IV bolus, then 150 mEq in 1 L D5W at 150–250 mL/h (goal serum pH 7.50–7.55, urine pH > 7.5); KCl repletion (alkalinization causes hypokalemia); HD if level > 100 mg/dL (acute) or > 60 (chronic), refractory acidosis, AMS, or pulmonary edema
Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA)Sedation, seizures, wide QRS, hypotensionStandard sedation/airway protection; lung-protective settingsNaHCO₃ 1–2 mEq/kg IV bolus for QRS > 100 ms (repeat q5–10 min until QRS narrows); maintenance NaHCO₃ drip (150 mEq in 1 L D5W at 150–250 mL/h) to keep arterial pH 7.50–7.55. Avoid Class Ia/Ic antiarrhythmics. Lipid emulsion 1.5 mL/kg IV bolus → 0.25 mL/kg/min for refractory cardiotoxicity.
OpioidApnea / hypoventilation, miosis, decreased LOCBag-mask ventilate; intubate only if reversal incomplete or aspiration riskNaloxone first: 0.04–0.4 mg IV (low-dose start in chronic users to avoid acute withdrawal); titrate up to 2 mg q2–3 min PRN; up to 10 mg may be required for synthetics (fentanyl analogues). For sustained reversal: drip at 2/3 of effective bolus dose per hour. Watch for re-narcotization with long-acting opioids (methadone, fentanyl patches).
Sedative-hypnotic (benzo, GHB, barbiturate)CNS depression, hypoventilation, hypotension (barbiturate), aspirationSupportive ventilation; usually short course; flumazenil 0.2 mg IV (avoid in benzo OD — precipitates seizures in chronic users; reserved for iatrogenic over-sedation in naïve patient)GHB typically resolves spontaneously in 4–6 h; intubate only for airway protection if severe; barbiturate severe — alkalinize urine (NaHCO₃ as above) for phenobarbital
β-blocker overdoseHypotension, bradycardia, often awakeStandard if intubated; manage hemodynamicsGlucagon 5–10 mg IV bolus (children 50 mcg/kg) → 2–10 mg/h infusion; high-dose insulin euglycemia 1 U/kg IV bolus + dextrose, then 0.5–1 U/kg/h infusion (titrate up to 10 U/kg/h) with dextrose to maintain glucose; pressors (norepi, epi); consider pacing; lipid emulsion 1.5 mL/kg → 0.25 mL/kg/min for refractory lipid-soluble agents (propranolol)
Ca-channel blocker overdoseHypotension, bradycardia (varies by agent)Standard if intubated; manage hemodynamicsCalcium gluconate 1–3 g IV (or CaCl₂ 1 g IV — central line); repeat q10–20 min × 3–4; high-dose insulin euglycemia 1 U/kg IV bolus → 0.5–1 U/kg/h titrated up to 10 U/kg/h with dextrose; pressors; methylene blue for refractory vasoplegia (dihydropyridine); lipid emulsion 1.5 mL/kg → 0.25 mL/kg/min
Cholinergic (organophosphate, nerve agent)SLUDGE-M, bronchorrhea, bronchospasm, paralysisAggressive suctioning (bronchorrhea floods lungs); ventilate against secretions until atropine/pralidoxime workAtropine 1–3 mg IV (children 0.05 mg/kg), double dose every 3–5 min until secretions dry (titrate to bronchorrhea, NOT pupil size — total doses of 50–100+ mg are normal); maintenance infusion 10–20% of cumulative loading dose per hour. Pralidoxime (2-PAM) 1–2 g IV over 30 min (children 25–50 mg/kg), then 8–10 mg/kg/h infusion × 24–72 h. Benzodiazepines (diazepam 5–10 mg or lorazepam 2–4 mg IV) for seizures.
CO / cyanide / smoke inhalationSee §42.2 (burn / inhalation injury)FiO₂ 1.0; HBO if CO > 25% or LOC; hydroxocobalamin for CNSpO₂ falsely normal in CO poisoning (oximetry can't distinguish OHb from COHb); use co-oximetry
Why salicylate intubation kills patientsThe patient with severe salicylate toxicity is hyperventilating to maintain serum pH (the metabolic acidosis is severe; the respiratory alkalosis is compensatory). Intubation typically requires sedation and paralysis — minute ventilation drops, PaCO₂ rises, pH plummets, salicylate ionization shifts to non-ionized form, salicylate enters CNS, seizures, cardiac arrest. If intubation is unavoidable: pre-intubation NaHCO₃ bolus, awake or ketamine-only induction with no paralytic, set the vent to MATCH the spontaneous minute ventilation (often 30+ L/min), and arrange dialysis. Most preventable salicylate deaths happen in the 5 minutes after sedation.
Key takeaways
  • TRALI vs TACO: TRALI is HLA antibody-driven capillary leak (BNP normal, no volume overload, recovers 48–72 h on lung-protective vent); TACO is volume overload (BNP↑, resolves with diuresis).
  • Burn/inhalation: upper-airway thermal injury edema progresses 24–48 h (intubate early if signs present); lower-airway ARDS-like develops 24–72 h; CO toxicity (FiO₂ 1.0, HBO if COHb > 25%); CN toxicity (hydroxocobalamin + thiosulfate).
  • DAH (diffuse alveolar hemorrhage): hemoptysis only 1/3 of cases; falling Hb, bilateral infiltrates, normal-to-elevated DLCO (intra-alveolar Hb takes up CO); bronchoscopy progressive lavages confirm.
  • BPF (bronchopleural fistula): vent preferentially leaks through low-resistance fistula — use PSV (lower leak), minimal VT/RR/PEEP, permit hypercapnia; surgical/bronchoscopic occlusion (endobronchial valves) for refractory leaks; the patient does not die from the BPF, treat the underlying disease.
  • HFOV is no longer recommended for routine adult ARDS (OSCILLATE / OSCAR negative, harm signal); residual niche in BPF, severe air-leak rescue, pediatric/neonatal ICU.
  • Drug-induced respiratory failure: salicylate do not let PaCO₂ rise on intubation (match minute ventilation, alkalinize, dialyze); TCA give NaHCO₃ for QRS > 100 ms; opioid reverse first; cholinergic atropine to dry secretions, not pupils.

43Airway clearance & secretion management on the vent

Retained secretions are the unglamorous reason vented patients re-collapse, develop VAP, and fail extubation. The mucociliary escalator that normally moves a half-liter of fluid up the airway every day is paralyzed by the cuffed ETT, the dry circuit gas, and ICU-acquired diaphragm weakness. Active secretion management is therefore a daily intervention, not an afterthought.

43.1 Why secretions matter
  • Atelectasis & shunt — mucus plugging causes lobar / segmental collapse, immediate loss of compliance, and refractory hypoxemia. The most common cause of acute desaturation in a stable vented patient is a mucus plug.
  • Resistance load — secretions in the ETT or central airways raise Raw, increase work of triggering, and prolong expiratory time constants → auto-PEEP risk in obstructive patients.
  • Infection — stagnant pooled secretions above the cuff are the substrate for VAP (§41).
  • Liberation failure — inability to clear secretions independently is one of the most-cited reasons SBTs are passed but extubation fails (§37).

43.2 Suctioning — physiology and technique

Suctioning is the most basic intervention and the most often done badly. Get the basics right.

Open vs closed (in-line) endotracheal suction
FeatureOpen suctionClosed (in-line) suction
Circuit broken?Yes — disconnect ventilatorNo — preserves PEEP and FiO₂
DerecruitmentSignificant — alveolar pressure drops to atmospheric, FiO₂ falls instantlyMinimal
Hypoxemia riskHigh in ARDS / high-PEEP patientsLow
Cross-contaminationHigher (hands, splash)Lower (closed system)
VAP rate—No clear difference (despite intuition)
Best forStable patients on low PEEPARDS, high PEEP > 10, unstable oxygenation, MRSA / TB / aerosol-precaution patients
How to suction without harm
  1. Pre-oxygenate with FiO₂ 1.0 for 30–60 s before suction in any patient on FiO₂ > 0.4 or PEEP > 8.
  2. Suction depth: just past the tip of the ETT — do not advance to the carina (causes tracheal injury, vagal-mediated bradycardia, and bronchospasm).
  3. Duration ≤ 10–15 s per pass. Apply suction only on withdrawal, not on insertion.
  4. Pressure 100–150 mmHg (negative). Higher causes mucosal stripping; lower is ineffective.
  5. Allow 3–5 vent breaths between passes; recheck SpO₂ and hemodynamics.
  6. Saline lavage is not routine — RCTs show it does not improve secretion mobilization and worsens oxygenation. Use only if a discrete plug is suspected and not clearable otherwise.
The bradycardia of suctioningVagal stimulation from carinal contact + hypoxia drops HR sharply, sometimes asystolic. If this happens once, switch to closed suction, pre-oxygenate aggressively, and consider atropine 0.5 mg ready at the bedside for the next pass.

43.3 Mucolytics — what works and what doesn't
Mucolytic agents in mechanical ventilation
AgentMechanismEvidenceUse
Hypertonic saline (3–7%) nebulizedOsmotic mucus rehydration; ↑ ciliary beatModest; routine in CF, less so in ICUReasonable in thick secretions; pre-medicate with bronchodilator (causes bronchospasm)
Dornase alfa (rhDNase) nebulizedCleaves extracellular DNA from neutrophilsStrong in CF; weak in non-CFNiche in pediatric ICU and CF; not routine adult ICU
N-acetylcysteine (NAC) nebulizedDisulfide-bond cleavageOld, controversial — may cause bronchospasmLargely abandoned for routine use; still occasionally for inspissated mucus
Bland aerosols (normal saline mist)Humidification onlyNo benefit beyond circuit humidificationUse heated humidifier instead
β₂-agonists (albuterol)Increase ciliary beat frequency, bronchodilateStrong for bronchospasm; modest for clearance aloneStandard adjunct before MIE / chest physiotherapy in obstructive patients

43.4 Mechanical insufflation–exsufflation (MIE)

MIE simulates a cough by delivering positive pressure (insufflation) to inflate the lung, then rapidly switching to negative pressure (exsufflation) to generate high expiratory flow that propels mucus toward the upper airway. The peak expiratory flow generated is far higher than any patient with weak cough can produce.

Standard MIE settings
PhasePressureTime
Insufflation (in)+25 to +35 cmH₂O (start +20, titrate up)1–2 s
Pause—0–1 s
Exsufflation (out)−30 to −40 cmH₂O (start −20, titrate down)2–4 s
Pause—2–3 s rest between cycles
Cycles per session5–10, separated by 30 s of rest3–5 sessions/day or PRN

Indications: neuromuscular weakness with peak cough flow < 270 L/min (the threshold below which secretion clearance fails), spinal cord injury, post-operative atelectasis, prolonged ventilation. Can be used through a face mask (extubated NMD patient), through an in-line port on the ventilator circuit, or directly on the ETT/trach.

Adjunct: combine with manually-assisted abdominal thrusts (timed to exsufflation) to amplify expiratory flow. This is the ALS/MND playbook (§26).

Contraindications: bullous emphysema (risk of barotrauma), recent pneumothorax, hemodynamic instability, severe bulbar weakness with inability to maintain airway during inhalation phase (risk of upper-airway closure / glottic adduction).

Peak cough flow < 270 L/minThe single best predictor of which patient needs cough assist. Measure on a peak-flow meter at the mouth (extubated) or at the trach (vented, on a brief disconnect). Below 270 L/min, secretion-related extubation failure is the rule, not the exception. Below 160 L/min, cough is essentially absent.

43.5 The expiratory-flow-bias principle

Bench and animal data (Volpe, Marini) show that the direction mucus moves in a vent circuit depends on the ratio of peak expiratory to peak inspiratory flow:

  • Expiratory flow ≫ inspiratory flow (B/A > 1) → mucus moves toward the airway opening (good).
  • Inspiratory flow ≫ expiratory flow (B/A < 1) → mucus moves toward the alveoli (bad — atelectasis).

Clinical implication: in the vented patient with abundant secretions, modify settings to bias flow expiratorily. Lower the inspiratory flow (longer Ti), shorten Te to maintain rate (within auto-PEEP limits), or use modes with higher peak expiratory flow (PSV with low support, decelerating-flow PC). The opposite — high peak inspiratory flow with brief expiratory time — drives mucus distally.

43.6 High-frequency chest-wall oscillation (HFCWO)

The "vest" — pneumatic compressions at 10–20 Hz applied to the chest wall, generating small high-frequency oscillations in airflow that loosen and mobilize peripheral secretions. Strong evidence in CF; growing use in non-CF bronchiectasis and ICU patients with retained secretions and consolidation. Typical session: 20–30 minutes 2–4×/day. Can be combined with MIE (HFCWO to mobilize → MIE to expel).

43.7 Bronchoscopy for airway clearance

When all else fails — atelectasis refractory to suction and physiotherapy, lobar collapse from a mucus plug, blood clots — flexible bronchoscopy at the bedside is the answer. See §42 for indications and technique.

Daily rounds checklistFor every vented patient: (1) Are secretions thick? Hydration status, humidification, mucolytic. (2) Are they mobilizing? In-line suction frequency, MIE if neuromuscular. (3) Are they being expelled? Cough strength assessment, peak cough flow if liberation pending. Failing any of these tees up the next 24-h plan.
Key takeaways
  • Mucus plugging is the most common cause of acute desaturation in a previously stable vented patient — suction and bronch first, before adjusting the vent.
  • Closed (in-line) suction preserves PEEP and oxygenation in ARDS / high-PEEP patients; open suction causes derecruitment.
  • Saline lavage with suctioning does not help and can harm — abandon the routine practice.
  • Mechanical insufflation–exsufflation (MIE) at +30 / −30 cmH₂O simulates an effective cough — first-line in neuromuscular patients with peak cough flow < 270 L/min.
  • Vent settings affect the direction mucus moves — bias toward expiratory flow (B/A > 1) to clear, away from inspiratory flow to retain.
  • Bronchoscopy is the rescue for refractory lobar atelectasis or persistent plugging.

44Inhaled drug delivery on the ventilator

Inhaled-drug deposition in a vented patient is dismal — typical lung deposition is 10–15% of nominal dose, versus 30–50% in a spontaneous breather using an MDI with spacer. The vent circuit, ETT, humidifier, and gas dynamics all conspire to lose drug. Knowing the mechanics turns a routine "give albuterol q4h" into a delivery problem you can actually solve.

44.1 Device selection — MDI vs nebulizer
Aerosol device comparison in the ventilator circuit
DeviceParticle size (MMAD)Lung depositionProsCons
MDI + spacer (in-line chamber)1–5 µm10–15%Cheap, fast, sterile, dose-precise, no circuit changesLimited drug menu (mostly bronchodilators, ICS); needs spacer to slow particles
Vibrating-mesh nebulizer2–4 µm15–40% (highest)No gas flow added (no VT distortion); efficient; works during inspiration onlyExpensive; clogs with viscous drugs; needs cleaning
Jet (pneumatic) nebulizer2–5 µm5–15%Universal, cheap, accepts most drugsAdds 6–8 L/min gas flow → distorts VT in VC, reduces FiO₂; long treatment time (10–15 min)
Ultrasonic nebulizer3–6 µm~10%Quiet, no added gas flowHeats drug (denatures proteins like dornase, antibiotics); generally inferior to vibrating mesh
Dry-powder inhaler (DPI)———Do not use in vented patients — requires high inspiratory flow patient cannot generate

44.2 Optimal placement in the circuit

The single biggest determinant of deposition is where the device sits in the circuit, because of how aerosol particles interact with the ventilator's inspiratory gas stream and the inertia of the carrier gas.

  • MDI: place the actuator 15 cm proximal to the Y-piece in the inspiratory limb (not at the ETT). The ~10 cm of tubing between MDI and Y-piece acts as a spacer, slowing large particles and letting smaller respirable particles continue. Placing the MDI at the ETT (no spacer) wastes 80% of the dose to inertial impaction.
  • Nebulizer: vibrating-mesh nebulizer is best placed before the humidifier on the inspiratory limb (or at the Y-piece if using a "dry" circuit). Jet nebulizers should sit 15 cm proximal to the Y-piece, similar to MDI placement, so the inspiratory gas accelerates the aerosol cloud forward.
  • Heat-and-moisture exchanger (HME): must be removed or bypassed during nebulization — otherwise the HME filters the aerosol out before it reaches the patient. Replace after the treatment.

44.3 Vent-setting effects on deposition

The ventilator itself is part of the delivery device. Five settings change deposition meaningfully:

How vent settings affect aerosol deposition
SettingDirectionWhy
Larger VT↑ deposition (modestly)More aerosol-laden gas reaches alveoli per breath
Lower inspiratory flow (longer Ti)↑ depositionLess turbulence, more time for sedimentation in distal airways
Decelerating flow pattern (PC) vs square (VC)↑ depositionSmoother flow profile; less inertial impaction
Synchronized with inspiration (vibrating mesh)↑ efficiency 2–3×Aerosol generated only when gas flowing inspiratorily; nothing wasted on expiration
Bias flow / leak compensation↓ depositionContinuous gas flow during expiration washes out aerosol cloud
Higher humidity (heated humidifier on)↓ deposition (with jet nebulizer)Particles grow hygroscopically and impact in upper circuit; less effect with vibrating mesh
Practical recipe — albuterol via MDI in a vented patient1. Shake MDI vigorously. 2. Pause the ventilator briefly if able, or coordinate puffs with onset of inspiration. 3. Remove HME if present. 4. Insert MDI in-line spacer 15 cm from Y-piece. 5. Deliver 4–8 puffs (NOT 2 — vent circuits lose > 80% of each puff vs spontaneous use). 6. Wait 30–60 s between puffs. 7. Replace HME. 8. Reassess — wheezing, plateau, dynamic compliance.

44.4 Specific drugs and doses
Common inhaled drugs in the ventilated patient
DrugIndicationVent doseNotes
Albuterol (MDI)Bronchospasm4–8 puffs q4h or q1h in statusSpontaneous-breather equivalent of 2 puffs is 4–8 in vent due to losses
Albuterol (neb)Bronchospasm; status asthmaticus2.5–5 mg q4h or continuous 10–20 mg/hContinuous infusion in status — fewer interruptions; check K⁺, troponin
IpratropiumCOPD exacerbation4 puffs MDI or 0.5 mg neb q6hSynergistic with albuterol
Inhaled corticosteroid (e.g., budesonide)Asthma maintenance, post-extubation laryngeal edema0.5–1 mg neb q12hLimited evidence in acute ICU; mostly maintenance
Inhaled epoprostenolRefractory hypoxemia (ARDS), pulmonary HTN, RV failure10–50 ng/kg/min continuous neb (vibrating mesh preferred)Selective pulmonary vasodilator. Cheaper than iNO. Re-titrate q1h. §20.7, §25
Inhaled nitric oxide (iNO)Refractory hypoxemia, pulmonary HTN, RV failure (PE, post-op)5–40 ppmSpecial delivery system; expensive; rebound HTN on weaning. §3.2a
Inhaled antibiotics (tobramycin, colistin, amikacin)VAP / tracheobronchitis with MDR organismsTobra 300 mg, colistin 75–150 mg, amikacin 400 mg neb q8–12hAdjunct to systemic; vibrating-mesh preferred (heat denatures). Pause vent flow during nebulization if possible
Hypertonic salineMucus retention3–7% × 4 mL neb q4–6hPre-treat with bronchodilator (causes bronchospasm)
Racemic epinephrinePost-extubation stridor, croup0.5 mL of 2.25% in 3 mL saline nebBrief effect (~ 2 h); rebound common; monitor for 4 h after
"He's not responding to nebs"Nine times out of ten the issue is delivery, not pharmacology. Before doubling the dose: confirm the device (MDI vs neb), placement (15 cm proximal, not at the ETT), HME removed, vent flow synchronized, patient awake enough to coordinate. Switch jet → vibrating mesh if available. Then escalate dose.
Key takeaways
  • Lung deposition in a vented patient is 10–15% with standard delivery — most of the drug is lost in the circuit, ETT, and upper airway.
  • Vibrating-mesh nebulizer > jet nebulizer for efficiency and circuit compatibility (no added gas flow).
  • MDI placement is 15 cm proximal to the Y-piece in the inspiratory limb — not at the ETT.
  • Remove the HME during nebulization; replace afterward.
  • Doses in vented patients should be 2–4× spontaneous-breather doses (4–8 MDI puffs vs 2; 5 mg neb vs 2.5 mg) to compensate for circuit losses.
  • Inhaled epoprostenol and iNO are pulmonary vasodilators useful in refractory hypoxemia and RV failure — see §20 and §25.
Appendix

Equations, drugs, abbreviations, references

Quick-reference materials: high-yield reference cards (the few tables you'll re-open most often), equation cheat sheet, drug-dosing tables (filterable by class), alarm reference, abbreviations glossary, and citations.

High-yield reference cards

The tables in this section live here on purpose: each one earns a place because a busy clinician will want to re-open it dozens of times after reading the book once. Cards are added as new ones prove their worth across the rest of the chapters. Each card is printable on its own (use the per-chapter print button at the top of this section).

Card 1 — Four-phenotype side-by-side (from §7.6)

Almost every patient on a ventilator falls into (or between) four physiologic phenotypes. The same vocabulary — compliance, resistance, V/Q, FRC, time constant — applies to all four, but the direction each axis moves is different, and every ventilator decision in the book follows from these moves. This is the single most decision-relevant table in the book; the original is in §7.6.

The four phenotypes side-by-side. Arrows show direction of change vs. normal; † = primary lesion driving the rest.
Axis ARDS COPD (emphysema) Severe asthma Cardiogenic edema
Static compliance (Cstat) ↓↓ (stiff lung)† ↑ (loss of recoil)† ↔ to ↓ (hyperinflated, near TLC) ↓ (wet, heavy lung)
Airway resistance (Raw) ↔ to mildly ↑ ↑↑ (expiratory collapse) ↑↑↑ (bronchospasm + mucus)† ↑ (edema in airway walls; "cardiac asthma")
FRC ↓↓ (collapsed/consolidated units) ↑↑ (dynamic hyperinflation, auto-PEEP) ↑↑ (dynamic hyperinflation, auto-PEEP) ↓ (alveolar flooding)
V/Q pattern Shunt dominates (consolidated alveoli) Dead space dominates (destroyed capillary bed) Mixed — mostly low-V/Q from mucus plugging Shunt + low-V/Q (flooded alveoli, vascular congestion)
Recruitable lung Yes (variable; high in early, low in late/fibrotic) No — alveoli are destroyed, not closed Minimal — the obstruction is in the airway, not the alveolus Yes — diuresis + PEEP recruits flooded units
Time constant (τ = R × C) Short (low C dominates) Long (high R + high C compound) Long (high R; C near normal) Short to mid
Dominant mechanism Loss of aerated lung volume → "baby lung" → pressure injury risk Expiratory flow limitation → incomplete emptying → auto-PEEP Acute airway narrowing → expiratory flow limitation → auto-PEEP Hydrostatic/permeability flooding → shunt + reduced compliance
Vent strategy in one line Low VT (4–6 mL/kg PBW), Pplat ≤ 30, PEEP titrated to keep open (§20) Low RR, long TE, accept hypercapnia, watch for auto-PEEP (§17) As COPD plus aggressive bronchodilation; permissive hypercapnia (§17) PEEP unloads the LV preload & afterload; diurese; oxygenate (§29.4)
What kills the patient VILI (volutrauma + atelectrauma + biotrauma) Dynamic hyperinflation → hemodynamic collapse on intubation Same as COPD — induction-related arrest from auto-PEEP Untreated cause (ischemia, AS, MR); secondary VILI if mismanaged
The single most useful column"Recruitable lung." It is the question that decides whether PEEP helps or harms. ARDS and edema are recruitable; COPD and asthma are not. Putting more PEEP into a non-recruitable patient just pushes the operating point up the chest-wall curve — more dead space, more hemodynamic compromise, no oxygenation benefit.

Card 2 — Two-question algorithm: PIP vs Pplat (from §6.3, §16)

Every pressure alarm in the ICU triages on two questions and three numbers.

Ask in order: (1) Is PIP high? (2) Is Pplat high?
The two-question algorithm at a glance
PIPPplatProblemCommon causesFix
↑NormalResistanceKinked / bitten / plugged ETT · secretions · bronchospasm · water in circuitSuction · bite block · bronchodilator · drain water trap
↑↑Lung mechanicsPTX · ARDS progression · pulmonary edema · atelectasis · abdominal hypertensionPOCUS for PTX · diuresis · recruit · bladder pressure · esophageal manometry if chest-wall component suspected
Normal↑Stiff lung with high PEEP / chest wallRising auto-PEEP · obesity · ascites · evolving ARDSCheck auto-PEEP (end-exp hold); consider esophageal manometry

Driving pressure ΔP = Pplat − PEEP. Target ≤ 15 cmH₂O across the book (lower is better; Amato 2015). Resistive ΔP = PIP − Pplat; > 15 cmH₂O suggests significant airway resistance.

Card 3 — Auto-PEEP detection & fix flow (from §17)

Detect:

  1. Look at the flow-time trace — expiratory flow does not return to zero before the next breath.
  2. Perform an end-expiratory hold (0.5–1 s, passive patient). Total PEEP − set PEEP = auto-PEEP.
  3. Cross-check with an end-inspiratory hold in heterogeneous obstruction; the inspiratory hold may unmask higher regional PEEPi (§14.7).

Fix (in priority order):

  1. ↓ RR — single most effective lever; accept permissive hypercapnia (pH ≥ 7.20).
  2. ↓ VT — less air to exhale per breath; couples with ↓ RR for additive effect on minute ventilation.
  3. ↑ peak inspiratory flow (VC) or ↓ Ti (PC) — shortens inspiration to lengthen expiration.
  4. Treat the bronchospasm — aggressive β-agonist + ipratropium + steroid; in severe asthma add IV magnesium, ketamine, heliox; consider NMB.
  5. Match ~75–80% of total PEEP if the patient is triggering against auto-PEEP — reduces trigger work (§17.4).
  6. If hemodynamic collapse: DISCONNECT the circuit and let the chest passively empty for 20–30 s. Resume bagging RR 6–8, slow flow, long TE. The post-intubation obstructive crash rescue (§17.3).

Card 4 — ARDSnet PEEP / FiO₂ ladders (from §20.3)

Two ladders. The low-PEEP table (ARMA / ARDSnet) is the day-1 default. The high-PEEP table (ALVEOLI / LOV-eD) is used in moderate-severe ARDS (P/F < 200) with clearly recruitable phenotype. Pick the row matching the patient's current FiO₂; advance down for more support, up as oxygenation improves. Re-check Pplat ≤ 30 and ΔP ≤ 15 after every step.

Low-PEEP / high-FiO₂ ladder (ARMA / ARDSnet)
StepFiO₂PEEP (cmH₂O)
10.35
20.45
30.48
40.58
50.510
60.610
70.710–14
80.814
90.914–18
101.018–24
High-PEEP / low-FiO₂ ladder (ALVEOLI / LOV-eD)
StepFiO₂PEEP (cmH₂O)
10.312
20.314
30.414–16
40.516–18
50.5–0.820
60.822
71.022–24

Full source tables with explanatory notes and citations: §20.3.

Card 5 — Daily liberation pathway (from §36)

Five gates. Failure at any gate routes back to current support with a 24-hour reassessment, not abandonment.

The five liberation gates
StepCriteriaFailure → action
1. Readiness screenUnderlying problem improving · SpO₂ ≥ 90% on FiO₂ ≤ 0.4, PEEP ≤ 5–8 · hemodynamically stable · adequate cough, manageable secretions · awake, follows commands (RASS −1 to 0)Maintain support, reassess daily
2. Spontaneous awakening trial (SAT)Hold sedation; if patient remains comfortable, proceedResume prior sedation at 50%; reattempt next day
3. Spontaneous breathing trial (SBT)PSV 5–8 over PEEP 5 (most common) or PSV 0 + PEEP 0 / T-piece (most rigorous); 30–120 minReturn to prior support; address cause; retry 24 h
4. Tolerance checkRSBI < 105 · VT > 5 mL/kg PBW · RR < 35 · SpO₂ ≥ 90% · pH stable · no agitation/diaphoresis/accessory muscle use · HR < 140 · SBP 90–180Return to prior support; investigate failure mode
5. Extubation gateCuff leak ≥ 110 mL · secretions manageable (suction < q2h) · GCS ≥ 8, follows commands · adequate cough (white-card test) · post-extubation support planned (HFNC/NIV if high-risk)Steroids (cuff leak), suction protocol, trach discussion

Full pathway with the pre-SBT pearl and the nutrition discussion: §36.

Card 6 — Bedside troubleshooting flowchart (from §39.1)

Three branches by what changed, plus the universal first move and the obstructive-crash emergency. Full annotated figure at §39.1 (Fig 39.1); condensed text form below.

First move — always, in order: Look at the patient → FiO₂ to 1.0 → confirm chest rise + EtCO₂ → if no chest rise: DISCONNECT from vent, bag with PEEP valve at slow rate (RR 6–8).
Triage by what changed
SymptomDiscriminatorCauses / next step
Pressure alarm (PIP up)PIP↑ + Pplat normal → resistance; PIP↑ + Pplat↑ → complianceResistance: ETT, secretions, bronchospasm, water. Compliance: PTX, atelectasis, edema, abdominal HTN. Card 2.
Volume / flow alarm (low VT, V̇E, PIP; apnea)Compare VTi vs VTe; check pilot pressureCircuit disconnect, cuff leak, tube migration, cracked HME, BPF, apnea / failed trigger. Cuff workup at §37.2a; trigger anomalies at §18.3.
Gas exchange (SpO₂ drop, PaCO₂ rising)DOPE(S) for hypoxemia; PaCO₂ ∝ V̇CO₂ / V̇A for hypercapniaTube, obstruction, PTX, equipment, stacked breaths, PE, atelectasis, PFO, asynchrony. Hypercapnia bucket: ↑VCO₂ vs ↑dead space vs ↓V̇A. Card 3 for auto-PEEP rescue.
EMERGENCY — Obstructive crash (asthma / COPD): Sudden post-intubation hypotension or hypoxemia → assume stacked breaths until proven otherwise. DISCONNECT the circuit, let the chest passively empty 20–30 s, resume bagging RR 6–8, slow flow, long TE. Full rescue at §17.3.

More cards will be added as new high-yield tables and decision charts earn their place. Candidate additions for future editions: asynchrony pattern atlas (§18), ECMO criteria (§33.3), NIV failure scores (§27), VAP prevention bundle (§41.2).

Drug dosing reference

Compact reference for all drugs you'll order in the first 72 hours of mechanical ventilation. Verify each dose against your institutional formulary; renal/hepatic adjustment may be required.

D.1 RSI induction agents

DrugDose (IV)Onset / DurationNotes
Etomidate0.2–0.3 mg/kg30 s / 5–10 minHemodynamically neutral; brief adrenal suppression (clinical impact debated in sepsis)
Ketamine1–2 mg/kg (reduce to 0.5–1 in shock)30 s / 10–15 minBronchodilator; sympathomimetic; preferred in shock and asthma; safe in TBI
Propofol1.5–2.5 mg/kg (reduce 25–50% if hypotensive/elderly)30 s / 5–10 min↓ ICP, ↓ CMRO₂, anti-seizure; vasodilator → hypotension
Midazolam0.1–0.3 mg/kg2–3 min / 30+ minSlow onset; rarely first-line for RSI

D.2 Neuromuscular blockers

DrugRSI doseOnset / DurationInfusion (ICU)Notes
Rocuronium1.0–1.2 mg/kg RSI (0.6–0.9 standard intubation)45–60 s / 45–70 min8–12 mcg/kg/minFirst-line non-depolarizing; reversible with sugammadex 16 mg/kg
Succinylcholine1.0–1.5 mg/kg30–45 s / 5–10 min—Avoid: hyperK+, burns > 24 h, denervation, NMD, malignant hyperthermia history
Cisatracurium0.1–0.2 mg/kg (typical 0.15)2–3 min / 25–40 min0.5–10 mcg/kg/min (typical 1–3)Hofmann elimination — safe in liver/renal failure; ARDS standard infusion
Vecuronium0.08–0.12 mg/kg2–3 min / 25–40 min0.8–1.7 mcg/kg/minHepatic metabolism; active metabolites in renal failure

D.3 Sedation infusions

DrugDose rangeOnset / OffsetNotes
Propofol5–80 mcg/kg/min (max 4 mg/kg/h sustained)30 s / 5–10 minFirst-line; PRIS (rhabdo + acidosis + cardiac collapse) if > 5 mg/kg/h × 48 h; lipid load
Dexmedetomidine0.2–1.5 mcg/kg/h (no bolus)15 min / 2–3 h"Cooperative sedation"; preserves respiratory drive; bradycardia/hypotension
Midazolam0.02–0.1 mg/kg/h2–3 min / hours-daysAvoid as routine; ↑ delirium; accumulates in renal failure
Ketamine0.1–2 mg/kg/h30 s / 10–15 minAnalgesic + sedative + bronchodilator; ↑ HR/BP; emergence reactions

D.4 Analgesics

DrugDoseNotes
Fentanyl25–250 mcg/h infusion; 25–100 mcg bolusFirst-line; rapid onset; no histamine
Hydromorphone0.5–3 mg/h infusion; 0.2–1 mg bolusLess accumulation in renal failure than morphine
Morphine1–10 mg/h infusion; 2–4 mg bolusHistamine release, hypotension; active metabolites in renal failure
Remifentanil0.025–0.2 mcg/kg/minUltra-short; rebound pain; expensive
Acetaminophen650–1000 mg PO/PR/IV q6h (max 4 g/day)Adjunct; ↓ opioid needs ~25%
Ketorolac15–30 mg IV q6h × max 5 dAvoid in renal disease, GI bleed risk, age > 65

D.5 Vasopressors and inotropes

DrugDose rangeReceptors / EffectUse
Norepinephrine0.01–3 mcg/kg/min (titrate to MAP)α₁ >> β₁; pure vasoconstrictorFirst-line for septic shock; RV-led shock
Phenylephrine0.1–10 mcg/kg/min; push 50–200 mcgPure α₁Push-dose for transient hypotension; norepi alternative if tachyarrhythmias
Vasopressin0.01–0.04 U/min (fixed)V1 receptorAdd to norepi in septic shock; not titrated above 0.04
Epinephrine0.01–1 mcg/kg/min (drip); 5–20 mcg pushα + β₁ + β₂Refractory shock, anaphylaxis, post-arrest
Dopamine2–20 mcg/kg/minDose-dependent: D > β > αLargely abandoned; ↑ arrhythmia vs norepi (SOAP-II)
Dobutamine2.5–20 mcg/kg/minβ₁ > β₂Inotrope for cardiogenic / septic shock with ↓ CO; vasodilation can worsen hypotension
Milrinone0.125–0.75 mcg/kg/minPDE3i; inodilatorRV failure; cardiac surgery LV failure; renal adjustment

D.6 Inhaled pulmonary vasodilators

DrugDoseNotes
Inhaled nitric oxide (iNO)5–40 ppm (typical start 20)Selective; methemoglobinemia (monitor MetHb if > 40 ppm or prolonged); rebound HTN on abrupt withdrawal; expensive
Inhaled epoprostenol10–50 ng/kg/min (typical start 30–50; titrate q1h)Cheaper than iNO; sticky on circuits — change q24h; same physiology
Inhaled iloprost2.5–5 mcg q2–4hIntermittent; less continuous coverage

D.7 Bronchodilators on the vent

DrugDose via vent circuitNotes
Albuterol2.5 mg neb q4–6h, or 8–10 puffs MDI q4–6h; continuous 10–20 mg/h in severe asthmaEffective via vent if MDI placed in inspiratory limb with spacer; tachycardia, ↓ K⁺
Ipratropium500 mcg neb q6hAdjunct to β₂; bronchodilator + secretion drying
Magnesium sulfate2 g IV over 20 min (severe asthma adjunct)Smooth muscle relaxant
IV methylprednisolone60–125 mg IV q6h × 5 d (severe asthma); 1–2 mg/kg/day (ARDS, COPD)Tapers per indication
Heliox 70:30Continuous via vent (compatible models)Reduces airway resistance in severe asthma; check vent compatibility

D.8 Reversal agents

DrugUseDose
SugammadexReverse rocuronium / vecuronium2 mg/kg (TOF 2/4); 4 mg/kg (PTC ≥ 1); 16 mg/kg (immediate after rocuronium 1.2)
Neostigmine + glycopyrrolateReverse non-depolarizing NMB (TOF must have twitches)Neostigmine 0.04–0.07 mg/kg + glyco 0.2 mg per 1 mg neostigmine
NaloxoneOpioid reversal0.04–0.1 mg IV titrated; avoid full reversal in opioid-dependent (precipitates withdrawal/agitation)
FlumazenilBenzodiazepine reversal0.2 mg IV q1 min, max 1 mg; avoid in chronic benzo use (seizures)
Push-dose pressor reminderFor peri-intubation hypotension: phenylephrine 100 mcg q2 min is the workhorse. Epinephrine 10–20 mcg pushes give β + α (use if bradycardic or β-agonism wanted). Have one drawn before pushing induction; do not chase BP after the fact.

Equations & numbers worth memorizing

QuantityFormula / value
Predicted body weight (men)\(\text{PBW} = 50 + 2.3 \times (\text{height}_{in} - 60)\)
Predicted body weight (women)\(\text{PBW} = 45.5 + 2.3 \times (\text{height}_{in} - 60)\)
Alveolar gas equation\(P_{AO_2} = F_iO_2(P_{atm} - P_{H_2O}) - \tfrac{P_aCO_2}{0.8}\)
A–a gradient (room air, normal)\(\tfrac{\text{age}}{4} + 4\)
Equation of motion\(P_{aw} = \tfrac{V}{C} + \dot{V}R + \text{PEEP}\)
Static compliance\(C_{stat} = \tfrac{V_T}{P_{plat} - \text{PEEP}}\); normal 60–100; ARDS 20–40
Airway resistance\(R_{aw} = \tfrac{P_{IP} - P_{plat}}{\dot{V}}\); normal < 5
Driving pressure\(\Delta P = P_{plat} - \text{PEEP}\); target ≤ 15
Mechanical power (J/min)\(\text{MP} = 0.098 \cdot \text{RR} \cdot V_T \cdot (P_{IP} - \tfrac{1}{2}\Delta P)\); concerning > 17
RSBI\(\text{RSBI} = \tfrac{\text{RR}}{V_T \,(L)}\); < 105 predicts SBT success
Vd/Vt\(\tfrac{V_d}{V_T} = \tfrac{P_aCO_2 - P_eCO_2}{P_aCO_2}\); > 0.6 predicts difficult wean
P/F vs S/F\(\tfrac{S}{F} \approx 0.5 \cdot \tfrac{P}{F} + 100\)
Permissive hypercapnia floorpH 7.20 (severe ARDS) — 7.15 (asthma)

Ventilator alarm reference — the differential for every common alarm

Alarms aren't noise; each is a question your ventilator is asking. This chapter is a consolidated card: every common alarm, its 3–5 likely causes, the distinguishing feature for each, and the immediate fix.

F.1 High peak pressure (PIP) alarm — usually triggered > 35 cmH₂O

High PIP differential
CauseDistinguishing featureImmediate fix
ETT kinked / patient bitingSudden onset; resistive crackle; bite block missing or displacedReposition head; bite block; suction mouth; sedation bolus if needed
Mucus plug / secretionsGradual rise over hours; ↓ exhaled VT; coarse crackles; ↑ work of breathingAggressive suction ± saline lavage; humidification; bronchoscopy if refractory
BronchospasmWheezing on exam; Pplat normal (resistance, not compliance); reactive historyAlbuterol + ipratropium nebs; deep sedation if asynchronous; consider IV steroids
ETT migrated to mainstemAsymmetric breath sounds (usually L absent); CXR shows tube tip below carinaWithdraw 1–3 cm; reconfirm bilateral breath sounds; re-image
PneumothoraxAcute rise + unilateral silence/hyperresonance ± hypotension; deep sulcus on supine CXRNeedle decompression if tension; emergent chest tube
Pulmonary edema / ARDS progressionPplat also high (lung mechanics, not airway); new infiltrates; ↑ WOBDiuresis if cardiogenic; recruit if ARDS; check PEEP and ΔP
Atelectasis (dependent zones)Gradual onset; Pplat ↑; focal CXR opacity; supine or post-op patientRecruitment maneuver; ↑ PEEP 2–3; lateral decubitus or prone positioning
Abdominal hypertension / ascites / tight binderPplat ↑; tense abdomen; bladder pressure > 12 mmHgLoosen binders; paracentesis if severe; consider esophageal manometry
The PIP/Pplat splitHigh PIP with normal Pplat = airway/resistance problem (kink, secretions, bronchospasm, mainstem). High PIP with also-high Pplat = lung or chest-wall problem (edema, ARDS, pneumothorax, abdominal hypertension). One inspiratory hold tells you which lane you're in.

F.2 High plateau pressure (Pplat) alarm — usually > 30 cmH₂O

High Pplat differential
CauseDistinguishing featureImmediate fix
ARDS progressionFalling P/F; rising ΔP; new infiltrates; ↓ complianceLung-protective settings; recruit; prone; NMB if dyssynchrony
AtelectasisGradual; focal CXR opacity; dependent lung; ↓ SpO₂Recruit; ↑ PEEP; reposition; suction
PneumothoraxAcute; unilateral silence; deep sulcus on supine CXREmergent needle decompression / chest tube
Intra-abdominal hypertensionPplat ↑ but PIP near baseline; tense abdomen; bladder pressure > 20 mmHgDecompression (drainage, laparotomy if ACS); loosen binders
Chest-wall stiffness (e.g., reduced compliance from body habitus, ascites, edema)Pplat ↑ but PL (transpulmonary pressure) may be normal — esophageal manometry tells the truthAccept Pplat 30–35 if oxygenation tolerable; place esophageal balloon if available
Auto-PEEP / dynamic hyperinflationExpiratory flow doesn't reach zero before next breath; common in COPD/asthma↓ RR; ↑ peak flow (lengthen Te); set PEEPe ~ 75% of auto-PEEP; bronchodilators

F.3 Low exhaled VT / low minute ventilation alarm

Low VT differential
CauseDistinguishing featureImmediate fix
Circuit disconnect (most common)Sudden VT loss; low PIP alarm coincident; circuit hissesReconnect immediately; hand-bag; check all junctions (Y-piece, HME, exhalation port)
Cuff leak (ETT)Gradual VT loss; hoarseness; audible leak around mouth; pilot balloon softReinflate cuff to 20–30 cmH₂O; if won't hold, change tube
Bronchopleural fistulaPersistent leak with chest tube bubbling; recent biopsy / barotrauma / necrotizing infectionAdjust chest tube suction; minimize PEEP / VT; surgical / IR consult
Chest tube leakVT drops correlate with new tube placement; air escapes through tubeVerify tube position; replace if intra-parenchymal; ensure water seal
ETT cuff failure / ruptureCuff pressure drops despite inflation; pilot line damagedReplace ETT — cuff cannot be repaired
Weak respiratory drive (oversedation, NMB residual)Spontaneous rate < set rate; minimal trigger effort; recent sedative bolusLighten sedation; reverse NMB if appropriate; switch to controlled mode
Sensor calibration errorIllogical VT readings; alarms persist after troubleshootingHand-ventilate to confirm patient OK; biomedical engineering / vent swap

F.4 Apnea alarm

Apnea alarm differential
CauseDistinguishing featureImmediate fix
OversedationRASS ≤ −3; recent sedative bolus; respiratory rate slow↓ sedative infusion; consider naloxone / flumazenil for reversible causes
Acute neuro event (stroke, herniation)Sudden change in mental status; new focal deficit; pupil changeStat neuro exam; CT head; raise HOB 30°; mannitol/3% saline if ICP
Hyper-oxygenation / drive suppression in COPDSpO₂ > 95% on FiO₂ > 0.30; chronic CO₂ retainerDrop FiO₂ to target SpO₂ 88–92%
Iatrogenic respiratory alkalosispH > 7.55; over-ventilation on prior settings↓ RR or VT to target PaCO₂ 35–40
Circuit disconnectSudden; total lack of pressure swing; SpO₂ falling fastReconnect; hand-bag; full vent restart
Apnea alarm ruleAlways hand-bag first to confirm oxygenation and ventilation are intact. Then diagnose the cause. A pink patient who happens to be deeply sedated is a different fire than a circuit disconnect.

F.5 Low PEEP / PEEP-loss alarm

PEEP-loss differential
CauseDistinguishing featureImmediate fix
Circuit disconnect at expiratory portPEEP falls to zero; coincident low-VT alarmReconnect; restart vent
Cuff leakGradual PEEP loss; audible leak around mouthReinflate cuff; replace ETT if persistent
Stuck or broken expiratory valvePEEP cannot be set; mechanical failure indicatorSwitch to backup ventilator; biomed engineering
Strong patient effort generating subatmospheric pressureVent detects pressure below set PEEP; trigger threshold too lowRaise trigger threshold; deepen sedation; treat pain/anxiety

F.6 Auto-triggering

Auto-trigger differential
CauseDistinguishing featureImmediate fix
Trigger threshold too sensitiveVent fires breaths the patient didn't trigger; high RRRaise trigger threshold (2–3 cmH₂O / 2–3 L/min)
Circuit water / condensateVisible water in tubing; oscillations on flow traceDrain water trap; check humidifier/HME
Circuit leakSustained pressure oscillation; audible hissTrace and seal leak
Cardiogenic oscillationTrigger fires synchronously with HR, not respirationRaise trigger threshold; switch to controlled mode if passive
Hiccups / fasciculationsSudden brief pressure spikes; NMB wearing offAdjust NMB / sedation; raise trigger threshold

F.7 Circuit disconnect — emergency protocol

Disconnect is a true emergency: positive pressure and PEEP are lost; hypoxemia within seconds in lung-injured patients.

  1. Call for help.
  2. Pick up self-inflating bag; manually ventilate with 100% O₂ — confirms airway is patent.
  3. Visually trace circuit while bagging: Y-piece, HME, inspiratory limb, expiratory limb, exhalation valve, temperature probe port.
  4. Reconnect at the disconnection site.
  5. Restart vent; confirm PEEP, FiO₂, alarms re-armed.
  6. ABG and CXR if any concern for aspiration during the event.

F.8 FiO₂ / gas-supply alarm

Oxygen / gas-supply differential
CauseDistinguishing featureImmediate fix
Wall O₂ disconnectedWall pressure gauge zero; recent line bumpReattach; if no wall O₂, attach E-cylinder (green tank) backup
FiO₂ sensor failure / calibration driftVent reports impossible FiO₂; alarm persistsRun room-air calibration; if persistent, sensor replacement
Central medical-gas system failureMultiple ventilators affected simultaneously; overhead alertAll patients to E-cylinders; activate hospital disaster protocol; manual ventilation as needed

F.9 Power / battery alarm

Vents run on AC power; battery is a 30–60 minute backup. Action: assess battery charge → try a different outlet (breaker may have tripped) → if still failing, switch to backup ventilator or hand-bag while biomed engineering responds. Verify outlet works by plugging in another known device.

F.10 The DOPE(S) & DOTTS mnemonics

Two complementary frameworks for any acute deterioration on the vent. DOPE(S) is the differential — what causes might explain this crisis. DOTTS is the action sequence — what to do, in order.

DOPE(S) — differential for acute vent deterioration
LetterCause
DDisplaced ETT (out the cords, mainstem)
OObstruction (mucus plug, kink, biting, bronchospasm)
PPneumothorax (especially tension)
EEquipment failure (circuit, cuff, vent malfunction)
SStacked breaths / auto-PEEP — added later; the auto-PEEP cause specifically in obstructive disease (asthma, COPD)
DOTTS — action sequence for acute vent deterioration
LetterAction
DDisconnect from the ventilator; bag with PEEP valve and FiO₂ 1.0 at slow rate (RR 6–8)
OOxygenate — confirm chest rise, capnography waveform, SpO₂ recovery
TTube position — waveform capnography, bilateral breath sounds, depth at lip (confirm vs CXR)
TTweak the vent (or check it) — review settings, alarm log, recent changes; swap ventilator if hardware suspected
SSonography — POCUS for pneumothorax (pleural sliding, lung point), ETT depth (suprasternal notch view), pleural effusion, RV

Run through DOPE(S) to enumerate what could be wrong; run DOTTS as the parallel action sequence to actually fix it. Together they cover > 95% of acute vent crises. Full bedside discussion of both at §39.1; the obstructive-crash variant (disconnect rescue) at §17.3.

Key takeaways
  • Every alarm has a structured differential — treat alarms as questions the vent is asking.
  • High PIP + normal Pplat = proximal airway problem. High PIP + high Pplat = lung or chest-wall problem.
  • Apnea alarms: hand-bag first (confirm oxygenation), then diagnose. Don't panic without information.
  • Circuit disconnect is an emergency. Have a bag-mask within arm's reach of every vented patient.
  • Auto-triggering is usually a trigger-threshold or circuit-water problem before it's mechanical failure.
  • Use DOPES / DOTTS as your standard mental framework — covers nearly every acute crisis.

Abbreviations

Abbr.MeaningAbbr.Meaning
A/CAssist-controlAPRVAirway pressure release ventilation
ARDSAcute respiratory distress syndromeATCAutomatic tube compensation
BiPAPBilevel positive airway pressureCPAPContinuous positive airway pressure
CstatStatic complianceCXRChest X-ray

EITElectrical impedance tomographyETTEndotracheal tube
FiO₂Fraction of inspired oxygenFRCFunctional residual capacity
HFNCHigh-flow nasal cannulaHFOVHigh-frequency oscillatory ventilation
I:EInspiratory : expiratory time ratioLIP / UIPLower / upper inflection point
MPMechanical powerNAVANeurally adjusted ventilatory assist
NIVNon-invasive ventilationNMBNeuromuscular blockade
Paw / PalvAirway / alveolar pressurePBWPredicted body weight
PCPressure controlPEEPPositive end-expiratory pressure
PEEPiIntrinsic PEEP (auto-PEEP)PesEsophageal pressure
P/FPaO₂ / FiO₂PIPPeak inspiratory pressure
PLTranspulmonary pressurePplatPlateau pressure
PRVCPressure-regulated volume controlPSVPressure support ventilation
P-SILIPatient self-inflicted lung injuryRASSRichmond Agitation–Sedation Scale
RawAirway resistanceRSBIRapid shallow breathing index (f/Vt)
S/FSpO₂ / FiO₂SBTSpontaneous breathing trial
SIMVSynchronized intermittent mandatory ventilationTe / TiExpiratory / inspiratory time
VCVolume controlVd / VtDead-space fraction
VE / VAMinute / alveolar ventilationVILIVentilator-induced lung injury
VV-ECMOVeno-venous extracorporeal membrane oxygenationVTTidal volume
VA-ECMOVeno-arterial ECMOHACORNIV-failure score (Heart rate, Acidosis, Consciousness, Oxygenation, RR)
ROXHFNC outcome index = (SpO₂/FiO₂)/RRNMDNeuromuscular disease
NIF / MIPNegative inspiratory force / Maximum inspiratory pressureMEPMaximum expiratory pressure
FVCForced vital capacityFEV₁Forced expiratory volume in 1 s
DLCODiffusing capacity for carbon monoxideTLCTotal lung capacity
HPVHypoxic pulmonary vasoconstrictionPVRPulmonary vascular resistance
RV (heart)Right ventricleLVLeft ventricle
iNOInhaled nitric oxideSvO₂Mixed venous oxygen saturation
DADDiffuse alveolar damagePEPulmonary embolism
GBSGuillain-Barré syndromeMGMyasthenia gravis
ALSAmyotrophic lateral sclerosis2,3-BPG2,3-bisphosphoglycerate
P₅₀PaO₂ at 50% Hb saturation (normal 26.5)P(a−et)CO₂Arterial-to-end-tidal CO₂ gap
BiPAPBi-level positive airway pressure (NIV)IPAP / EPAPInspiratory / expiratory positive airway pressure
SVRSystemic vascular resistancePOCUSPoint-of-care ultrasound
TOFTrain-of-four (NMB monitoring)RSIRapid sequence intubation
AVMArteriovenous malformationILDInterstitial lung disease
IVIGIntravenous immunoglobulinCABGCoronary artery bypass graft
NRBNon-rebreather (mask)CAM-ICUConfusion Assessment Method for the ICU
LCALatent class analysis (ARDS subphenotype)GOLDGlobal Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease
DO₂Oxygen delivery (CaO₂ × CO × 10)BNPB-type natriuretic peptide

  13. Combes A, Hajage D, Capellier G, et al. Extracorporeal membrane oxygenation for severe acute respiratory distress syndrome (EOLIA). N Engl J Med. 2018;378(21):1965-1975.
  14. Brochard L, Slutsky A, Pesenti A. Mechanical ventilation to minimize progression of lung injury in acute respiratory failure. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2017;195(4):438-442.
  15. Beitler JR, Sarge T, Banner-Goodspeed VM, et al. Effect of titrating positive end-expiratory pressure with an esophageal pressure-guided strategy vs an empirical high PEEP-FiO₂ strategy on death and days free from mechanical ventilation among patients with acute respiratory distress syndrome (EPVent-2). JAMA. 2019;321(9):846-857.
  16. Cavalcanti AB, Suzumura ÉA, Laranjeira LN, et al. Effect of lung recruitment and titrated positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) vs low PEEP on mortality in patients with acute respiratory distress syndrome (ART). JAMA. 2017;318(14):1335-1345.
  17. Serpa Neto A, Deliberato RO, Johnson AEW, et al. Mechanical power of ventilation is associated with mortality in critically ill patients: an analysis of patients in two observational cohorts. Intensive Care Med. 2018;44(11):1914-1922.
  18. Papazian L, Forel JM, Gacouin A, et al. Neuromuscular blockers in early acute respiratory distress syndrome (ACURASYS). N Engl J Med. 2010;363(12):1107-1116.
  19. Yang KL, Tobin MJ. A prospective study of indexes predicting the outcome of trials of weaning from mechanical ventilation. N Engl J Med. 1991;324(21):1445-1450.
  20. Thille AW, Muller G, Gacouin A, et al. Effect of postextubation high-flow nasal oxygen with noninvasive ventilation vs high-flow nasal oxygen alone on reintubation among patients at high risk of extubation failure. JAMA. 2019;322(15):1465-1475.
  21. Girard TD, Kress JP, Fuchs BD, et al. Efficacy and safety of a paired sedation and ventilator weaning protocol for mechanically ventilated patients in intensive care (Awakening and Breathing Controlled — ABC trial). Lancet. 2008;371(9607):126-134.
  22. Pun BT, Balas MC, Barnes-Daly MA, et al. Caring for critically ill patients with the ABCDEF Bundle: results of the ICU Liberation Collaborative in over 15,000 adults. Crit Care Med. 2019;47(1):3-14.
  23. Thille AW, Cabello B, Galia F, Lyazidi A, Brochard L. Reduction of patient-ventilator asynchrony by reducing tidal volume during pressure-support ventilation. Intensive Care Med. 2008;34(8):1477-1486.
  24. Blanch L, Villagra A, Sales B, et al. Asynchronies during mechanical ventilation are associated with mortality. Intensive Care Med. 2015;41(4):633-641.
  25. Kenny JE. An Approach to Mechanical Heart-Lung Interaction. 1st ed. Toronto: Spectral Envelope Publishing House; 2020. (Creative Commons free cultural work; available at heart-lung.org.)
  26. Hess DR, Kacmarek RM. Essentials of Mechanical Ventilation. 4th ed. New York: McGraw-Hill Education; 2019.
  27. Owens W. The Advanced Ventilator Book. 1st ed. Columbia, SC: First Draught Press; 2017.
  28. West JB, Luks AM. West's Respiratory Physiology: The Essentials. 10th ed. Philadelphia: Wolters Kluwer; 2016.
  29. Nuckton TJ, Alonso JA, Kallet RH, et al. Pulmonary dead-space fraction as a risk factor for death in the acute respiratory distress syndrome. N Engl J Med. 2002;346(17):1281-1286.
  30. West JB, Luks AM. West's Pulmonary Pathophysiology: The Essentials. 9th ed. Philadelphia: Wolters Kluwer; 2017.
  31. Roca O, Caralt B, Messika J, et al. An index combining respiratory rate and oxygenation to predict outcome of nasal high-flow therapy. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2019;199(11):1368-1376. (ROX index)
  32. Duan J, Han X, Bai L, Zhou L, Huang S. Assessment of heart rate, acidosis, consciousness, oxygenation, and respiratory rate to predict noninvasive ventilation failure in hypoxemic patients. Intensive Care Med. 2017;43(2):192-199. (HACOR score)
  33. Mauri T, Spinelli E, Scotti E, et al. Potential for lung recruitment and ventilation-perfusion mismatch in patients with the acute respiratory distress syndrome from coronavirus disease 2019. Crit Care Med. 2020;48(8):1129-1134.
  34. Slutsky AS, Ranieri VM. Ventilator-induced lung injury. N Engl J Med. 2013;369(22):2126-2136.
  35. Tobin MJ. Principles and Practice of Mechanical Ventilation. 3rd ed. New York: McGraw-Hill; 2013.
  36. Yartsev A. Intrinsic PEEP and the expiratory hold maneuver; Alveolar pressure and the inspiratory hold maneuver. Deranged Physiology (CICM Primary Exam — Respiratory System, Chapters 559 & 5591). Available at derangedphysiology.com.
  37. Yartsev A. Interpreting the shape of the pressure-volume loop; Hysteresis of the pulmonary pressure-volume curve. Deranged Physiology (CICM Primary Exam — Respiratory System, Chapters 554 & 0312). Available at derangedphysiology.com.
  38. Harris RS. Pressure-volume curves of the respiratory system. Respir Care. 2005;50(1):78-99. (Definitive review of LIP/UIP measurement and interrater variability; also referenced by Deranged Physiology Ch 555 — pressure-volume loops in the presence of lung pathology.)
  39. Yartsev A. Interpreting the shape of the pressure waveform; Interpreting the shape of the ventilator flow waveform; Interpreting the shape of the flow-volume loop; Pressure-volume loops in the presence of lung pathology; Normal and abnormal capnograph waveforms; An introduction to the ventilator waveform. Deranged Physiology (CICM Primary Exam — Respiratory System, Chapters 551, 552, 553, 555, 556, 5592, 5593). Available at derangedphysiology.com.
  40. Grasso S, Stripoli T, De Michele M, et al. ARDSnet ventilatory protocol and alveolar hyperinflation: role of positive end-expiratory pressure. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2007;176(8):761-767. (The reference for the stress-index β as a bedside marker of overdistension and recruitment.)
  41. Frat JP, Thille AW, Mercat A, et al. High-flow oxygen through nasal cannula in acute hypoxemic respiratory failure (FLORALI). N Engl J Med. 2015;372(23):2185-2196.
  42. Aubier M, Murciano D, Milic-Emili J, et al. Effects of the administration of O₂ on ventilation and blood gases in patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease during acute respiratory failure. Am Rev Respir Dis. 1980;122(5):747-754. (Demonstrates V/Q redistribution as the dominant mechanism of oxygen-induced hypercapnia in COPD, not "loss of hypoxic drive.")
  43. Mellengaard K. The alveolar-arterial oxygen difference: its size and components in normal man. Acta Physiol Scand. 1966;67(1):10-20. (Original derivation of the age-adjusted normal A-a gradient.)
  44. Dankiewicz J, Cronberg T, Lilja G, et al. Hypothermia versus normothermia after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (TTM2). N Engl J Med. 2021;384(24):2283-2294. (Established targeted normothermia 36–37.5 °C as the current standard, retiring routine 33 °C hypothermia.)
  45. Berggren SM. The oxygen deficit of arterial blood caused by non-ventilating parts of the lung. Acta Physiol Scand. 1942;4(Suppl 11):1-92. (Original derivation of the shunt equation; standard citation in physiology textbooks.)
  46. Plant PK, Owen JL, Elliott MW. Early use of non-invasive ventilation for acute exacerbations of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease on general respiratory wards: a multicentre randomised controlled trial. Lancet. 2000;355(9219):1931-1935. (Established NIV / BiPAP as first-line for acute hypercapnic COPD exacerbation.)
  47. Masip J, Roque M, Sánchez B, et al. Noninvasive ventilation in acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema: systematic review and meta-analysis. JAMA. 2005;294(24):3124-3130. (Established NIV / CPAP as first-line for acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema.)
  48. Vogelmeier CF, Criner GJ, Martinez FJ, et al. Global Strategy for the Diagnosis, Management, and Prevention of Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease 2023 Report: GOLD Executive Summary. Eur Respir J. 2023;61(4):2300239. (Current GOLD severity staging for COPD.)
  49. Devlin JW, Skrobik Y, Gélinas C, et al. Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Prevention and Management of Pain, Agitation/Sedation, Delirium, Immobility, and Sleep Disruption in Adult Patients in the ICU (PADIS). Crit Care Med. 2018;46(9):e825-e873.

For clinician education only; not a substitute for institutional protocols, current FDA labeling, or specialist consultation. Verify dosing and ventilator-specific behavior at the bedside. Companion bedside one-pager (single-page rounds reference) · standalone interactive calculator with threshold-coded outputs, LPV-bundle status row, ARDSnet PEEP/FiO₂ ladder lookup, Vd/Vt, DO₂, OI, transpulmonary pressures, target-PaCO₂ helper, and saveable snapshots.
